### 2.4.3 Cost of Quality — Percentage-of-Sales Benchmark
A commonly cited industry benchmark for an *acceptable* COQ is **2.5% to 4% of sales revenue**. World-class organizations target **< 2.5%**. Poor quality systems can exceed **10-15%** of sales.

---

## 2.5 Total Quality Management — Core Principles

### 2.5.1 Deming's 14 Points (Summarized for KTU)

| # | Point (Brief) |
|---|---|
| 1 | Create constancy of purpose for improvement |
| 2 | Adopt the new philosophy |
| 3 | Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality |
| 4 | End the practice of awarding business on price alone |
| 5 | Improve constantly every process |
| 6 | Institute training on the job |
| 7 | Institute leadership |
| 8 | Drive out fear |
| 9 | Break down barriers between departments |
| 10 | Eliminate slogans and exhortations |
| 11 | Eliminate numerical quotas |
| 12 | Remove barriers to pride of workmanship |
| 13 | Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement |
| 14 | Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation |

### 2.5.2 Juran's Quality Trilogy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Quality Planning} &: \text{Identify customers \& their needs} \\
\textbf{Quality Control} &: \text{Evaluate actual performance vs. goals} \\
\textbf{Quality Improvement} &: \text{Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.5.3 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality
1. Quality = **Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness")
2. Prevention, not inspection
3. Zero defects performance standard
4. Measure quality by the **price of non-conformance (PONC)**

### 2.5.4 Ishikawa's Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)
- Quality is everyone's responsibility.
- Emphasis on **Quality Circles** — small groups of workers meeting voluntarily to identify and solve work-related quality problems.

### 2.5.5 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF)

The QLF quantifies the loss to society from a product deviating from its target value $T$:

$$
L(x) = k(x - T)^2
$$

Where:
- $L(x)$ = Quality loss (in monetary terms)
- $x$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $T$ = Target value
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient (a constant determined by company policy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Taguchi's QLF argues that even a product *within* specification limits incurs a loss to society — the further from target, the greater the loss. This shifts focus from "meeting specs" to "hitting the target."

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Cost optimization |
| 2 | COQ as % of Sales | $\%\text{COQ} = \dfrac{\text{Total COQ}}{\text{Sales Revenue}} \times 100$ | Industry benchmarking |
| 3 | QFD Technical Weight | $W_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot R_{ij}$ | House of Quality |
| 4 | QFD Relative Weight | $w_j = \dfrac{W_j}{\sum_{k=1}^{m} W_k} \times 100\%$ | Ranking TRs |
| 5 | Taguchi QLF | $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ | Robust design |
| 6 | Six-Sigma DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}}$ | Process capability |
| 7 | First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Entered}} \times 100\%$ | Process efficiency |
| 8 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Multi-stage yield |
| 9 | Crosby's PONC | $\text{PONC} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Cost of non-conformance |
| 10 | PDCA Cycle | Plan → Do → Check → Act | Continuous improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars / absolute values in formulas above have been written using standard LaTeX `()\vert{}` or `^2` notation to prevent breaking the markdown table structure.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota):** TQM + Jidoka (autonomation) + Kaizen = world-class manufacturing.
- **Software (Microsoft, Google):** CMMI + Agile + Six Sigma = quality assurance in IT services.
- **Healthcare (Apollo Hospitals):** TQM-driven NABH accreditation system.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, ISRO):** AS9100 QMS + Taguchi's robust design for mission-critical reliability.
- **Construction (L&T, Tata Projects):** TQM in EPC projects for defect-free handover.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Worked Numerical Derivations

### 3.1.1 Problem 1: Calculating Total COQ & Optimization

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing company reports the following annual quality costs:
- Prevention Costs ($C_P$) = ₹ 5,00,000
- Appraisal Costs ($C_A$) = ₹ 8,00,000
- Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$) = ₹ 12,00,000
- External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$) = ₹ 15,00,000
- Annual Sales = ₹ 4,00,00,000

**Calculate:** (a) Total COQ, (b) COQ as % of sales, (c) Conformance vs. Non-conformance cost, (d) Comment on quality cost structure.

---

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the given values**
From the problem:
- $C_P$ = ₹ 5,00,000
- $C_A$ = ₹ 8,00,000
- $C_{IF}$ = ₹ 12,00,000
- $C_{EF}$ = ₹ 15,00,000
- Sales = ₹ 4,00,00,000

**Step 2: Compute the Total COQ**
Applying the master equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total COQ} &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 40{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Total COQ = **₹ 40,00,000** (Forty Lakhs).

---

**Step 3: Compute COQ as % of Sales**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{COQ} &= \frac{\text{Total COQ}}{\text{Sales Revenue}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4}{400} \times 100 \\
&= 1.0\% \times 100 \\
&= 10.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** COQ = **10% of sales** — this is **high** (world-class target is <2.5%).

---

**Step 4: Split into Conformance vs. Non-conformance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Conformance}} &= C_P + C_A = 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{\text{Non-conformance}} &= C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = 27{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Ratio (Non-conformance : Conformance)} &= 27 : 13 \\
&\approx 2.08 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The company is spending **~2.08 times more on failures than on prevention/appraisal**. This is a sign of a **reactive** quality system.

---

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation (TQM Recommendation)**
If the company increases prevention spending by ₹ 3,00,000 (taking $C_P$ to ₹ 8,00,000) and, as a result, $C_{IF}$ drops by 30% and $C_{EF}$ drops by 40%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New } C_{IF} &= 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 = 8{,}40{,}000 \\
\text{New } C_{EF} &= 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 9{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{New } C_A &= 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ (unchanged)} \\
\text{New } C_P &= 8{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{New Total COQ} &= 8{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}40{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 33{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net Savings** = ₹ 40,00,000 − ₹ 33,40,000 = **₹ 6,60,000 per year.**

This numerically demonstrates Juran's principle: **"Investment in prevention yields more than proportional savings in failure costs."**

---

## 3.2 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function — Worked Example

### 3.2.1 Problem 2: Quantifying Societal Loss

**Problem Statement:**
A bolt has a target diameter of **T = 10.00 mm**. The quality loss coefficient is **k = ₹ 1,000 per mm²** (Taguchi's constant). Two bolts are produced:
- Bolt A: diameter = 9.95 mm
- Bolt B: diameter = 9.50 mm

**Calculate:** The quality loss for each bolt. Comment on the implication.

---

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Recall the QLF equation**

$$
L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate loss for Bolt A**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(A) &= 1000 \times (9.95 - 10.00)^2 \\
&= 1000 \times (-0.05)^2 \\
&= 1000 \times 0.0025 \\
&= \text{₹ } 2.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Loss for Bolt A = **₹ 2.50** (a small but real loss).

**Step 3: Calculate loss for Bolt B**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(B) &= 1000 \times (9.50 - 10.00)^2 \\
&= 1000 \times (-0.50)^2 \\
&= 1000 \times 0.25 \\
&= \text{₹ } 250.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Loss for Bolt B = **₹ 250.00**.

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation**
Even though Bolt A is *within typical tolerance* (e.g., ±0.10 mm), it still incurs a loss of ₹ 2.50. Bolt B (at the edge of a wider tolerance band) incurs a loss of **100 times** Bolt A. This empirically validates Taguchi's argument that **deviation from target — not conformance to wide specs — is the true measure of quality**.

---

## 3.3 QFD (House of Quality) — Priority Calculation

### 3.3.1 Problem 3: Computing Technical Requirement Weights

**Problem Statement:**
A smart-watch manufacturer has identified 3 customer requirements (CRs) and 3 technical requirements (TRs) with the following importance weights and relationship matrix:

| CR \ TR | TR1 (Battery Life, hrs) | TR2 (Weight, g) | TR3 (Display Size, in) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CR1 (Long usage)** — Weight: 5 | 9 | 3 | 1 |
| **CR2 (Lightweight)** — Weight: 4 | 1 | 9 | 3 |
| **CR3 (Large screen)** — Weight: 3 | 1 | 3 | 9 |

**Calculate:** Absolute and relative weights for each TR.

---

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Compute Absolute Weight for TR1 (Battery Life)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= (C_1 \times R_{1,1}) + (C_2 \times R_{2,1}) + (C_3 \times R_{3,1}) \\
&= (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 1) \\
&= 45 + 4 + 3 \\
&= 52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Absolute Weight for TR2 (Weight)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_2 &= (C_1 \times R_{1,2}) + (C_2 \times R_{2,2}) + (C_3 \times R_{3,2}) \\
&= (5 \times 3) + (4 \times 9) + (3 \times 3) \\
&= 15 + 36 + 9 \\
&= 60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Absolute Weight for TR3 (Display Size)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_3 &= (C_1 \times R_{1,3}) + (C_2 \times R_{2,3}) + (C_3 \times R_{3,3}) \\
&= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 3) + (3 \times 9) \\
&= 5 + 12 + 27 \\
&= 44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Total and Relative Weights**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W &= W_1 + W_2 + W_3 = 52 + 60 + 44 = 156 \\
w_1 &= \frac{52}{156} \times 100\% = 33.33\% \\
w_2 &= \frac{60}{156} \times 100\% = 38.46\% \\
w_3 &= \frac{44}{156} \times 100\% = 28.21\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result — Ranked Priority of Technical Requirements:**

| Rank | TR | Absolute Weight | Relative Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TR2 (Lightweight) | 60 | **38.46%** |
| 2 | TR1 (Battery Life) | 52 | 33.33% |
| 3 | TR3 (Display Size) | 44 | 28.21% |

**Engineering Decision:** The design team should prioritize **reducing weight first**, then **improving battery life**, and lastly **enlarging the display**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quality Cost Behavior

| Quality Cost Type | Behavior as Quality Improves | TQM Action |
|---|---|---|
| Prevention Costs ($C_P$) | **Increases initially**, then plateaus | Maintain high investment |
| Appraisal Costs ($C_A$) | Decreases after initial rise | Replace with process control |
| Internal Failure ($C_{IF}$) | Decreases continuously | Drive toward zero |
| External Failure ($C_{EF}$) | Decreases continuously | Drive toward zero |
| **Total COQ** | **Decreases after optimum** | Find & maintain optimum |

> [!NOTE]
> The curve of Total COQ vs. Prevention Investment is a **U-shaped curve**. Initially, more prevention reduces failures enough to lower total COQ. Beyond the optimum, additional prevention investment yields diminishing returns and may increase total COQ.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Quality Management Hierarchy (Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Quality Policy<br/>Top Management Intent"] --> B["Quality Objectives<br/>SMART Measurable Goals"]
    B --> C["Quality Planning<br/>Juran Step 1"]
    C --> D["Quality Control<br/>Operational Inspection"]
    C --> E["Quality Assurance<br/>Process Improvement"]
    D --> F["Quality Improvement<br/>Juran Step 3"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Total Quality Management<br/>Culture of Excellence"]
    G -.feedback loop.-> A
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows how a Quality Policy cascades into Objectives → Planning → Control & Assurance (parallel) → Improvement → TQM, with a feedback loop re-energizing the policy.

---

## 4.2 QC vs QA — The Process Flow Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QC["QUALITY CONTROL - Reactive / Detective"]
        QC1["Raw Material"] --> QC2["Production"]
        QC2 --> QC3["Inspection Gate"]
        QC3 --> QC4{Defect Found?}
        QC4 -- Yes --> QC5["Reject / Rework"]
        QC4 -- No --> QC6["Ship to Customer"]
    end
    subgraph QA["QUALITY ASSURANCE - Proactive / Preventive"]
        QA1["Customer Need"] --> QA2["Design Review"]
        QA2 --> QA3["Process FMEA"]
        QA3 --> QA4["Process Control"]
        QA4 --> QA5["Continuous Improvement"]
    end
    QC5 -.feeds data to.-> QA3
    QA5 -.improves design for.-> QC2
```

**Interpretation:** QC is a *gate* that inspects finished products; QA is a *pipeline* that prevents defects from forming. The dashed cross-arrows show the two-way feedback between the two systems.

---

## 4.3 The PDCA (Deming) Cycle — The Engine of TQM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["PLAN<br/>Define objective, process,<br/>and success metrics"]
    D["DO<br/>Execute the plan on<br/>a small test scale"]
    C["CHECK<br/>Measure results and<br/>compare to expected"]
    A["ACT<br/>Standardize the improvement<br/>or revise the plan"]
    A --> P
```

**Interpretation:** PDCA is the continuous-improvement engine. Each cycle tightens the loop between reality (Check) and intent (Plan).

---

## 4.4 The Quality Function Deployment (House of Quality)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VOC["Voice of Customer<br/>Whats = Customer Requirements"] -->|Importance Weights| HOQ1
    HOQ1["HOUSE OF QUALITY<br/>Central Relationship Matrix"]
    ENGR["Engineering Characteristics<br/>Hows = Technical Requirements"] -->|Correlation Coefficients| HOQ1
    HOQ1 --> TW["Technical Priorities<br/>Weighted Ranking"]
    HOQ1 --> TV["Target Values for<br/>Each Technical Requirement"]
    HOQ1 --> CA["Competitive Analysis<br/>Customer & Technical"]
    TW --> DPG["Design Planning Group<br/>Decisions"]
    TV --> DPG
    CA --> DPG
    DPG --> PROD["Final Product Design<br/>Specifications"]
```

**Interpretation:** QFD translates "what the customer wants" into "what the engineer must design" — a single matrix that aligns marketing, design, and manufacturing.

---

## 4.5 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The Optimization Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW["Under-Invested in Prevention"]
        LOW1["High Internal Failure"] --> LOW2["High External Failure"]
        LOW2 --> LOW3["Total COQ: HIGH"]
    end
    subgraph OPT["OPTIMUM POINT"]
        OPT1["Balanced C_P + C_A"] --> OPT2["Minimal C_IF + C_EF"]
        OPT2 --> OPT3["Total COQ: MINIMIZED"]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["Over-Invested in Prevention"]
        HIGH1["Excessive Training/Audit Cost"] --> HIGH2["Diminishing Returns"]
        HIGH2 --> HIGH3["Total COQ: HIGH again"]
    end
    LOW --> OPT
    OPT --> HIGH
```

**Interpretation:** The Total COQ curve is U-shaped. World-class firms operate at the optimum, where prevention investment pays the maximum dividend.

---

## 4.6 The TQM Pillars — Building on the Five Gurus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TQM["TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT<br/>Customer Focus + Continuous Improvement"]
    P1["PILLAR 1: Deming<br/>System Thinking + PDCA + 14 Points"]
    P2["PILLAR 2: Juran<br/>Quality Trilogy"]
    P3["PILLAR 3: Crosby<br/>Zero Defects + PONC"]
    P4["PILLAR 4: Ishikawa<br/>CWQC + Quality Circles + Fishbone"]
    P5["PILLAR 5: Taguchi<br/>Quality Loss Function + Robust Design"]
    TQM --> P1
    TQM --> P2
    TQM --> P3
    TQM --> P4
    TQM --> P5
    P1 --> R["TQM Results:<br/>Customer Satisfaction, Lower COQ,<br/>Employee Engagement, Competitive Edge"]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    P4 --> R
    P5 --> R
```

**Interpretation:** TQM does not originate from a single guru — it is the *synthesis* of five complementary philosophies, each addressing a different facet of quality.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Quality Policy. List any four characteristics of an effective Quality Policy. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A Quality Policy is a formal statement of the overall intentions and direction of an organization with regard to quality, as expressed by top management. It serves as a framework for setting quality objectives and includes a commitment to meet customer requirements and to continually improve the Quality Management System.

**Four Characteristics (1.5 Marks):**
1. It is **appropriate** to the organization's purpose and context.
2. It **provides a framework** for setting and reviewing quality objectives.
3. It includes a **commitment to satisfy** customer and applicable regulatory requirements.
4. It includes a **commitment to continual improvement** of the QMS.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA). `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Product / Output | Process / System |
| **Orientation** | Reactive (detective) | Proactive (preventive) |
| **Approach** | Inspection & testing | Process design & control |
| **Time Frame** | After production | Throughout the process |
| **Goal** | Identify defects | Prevent defects |
| **Cost Type** | Primarily Appraisal + Internal Failure | Primarily Prevention + Appraisal |
| **Responsibility** | QC inspectors / operators | All employees, led by QA team |

> Closing line: *"QC finds defects; QA prevents defects."* (This crisp line can earn you the full final mark in KTU valuation.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks): Total Quality Management and Deming's Philosophy**

`(a)` **Explain the concept of Total Quality Management (TQM). Discuss its key elements. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### Model Solution:

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Total Quality Management (TQM) is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members — from top management to front-line workers — to meet or exceed customer expectations.

**Key Elements of TQM (5 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Customer Focus:** The customer (internal or external) is the ultimate judge of quality. TQM begins and ends with understanding and meeting customer needs.

2. **Total Employee Involvement / Participation:** Every employee, from CEO to the janitor, is responsible for quality. Achieved through empowerment, Quality Circles, suggestion systems, and training.

3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Quality is not a destination but a journey. The PDCA cycle drives incremental improvement in every process every day.

4. **Process-Centered Approach:** TQM focuses on improving the *process*, not just blaming the *worker*. A well-designed process yields consistent quality.

5. **Integrated System:** All departments — design, production, marketing, finance, HR — work as one interconnected system. Silos are broken.

6. **Strategic & Systematic Approach:** Quality is integrated into the strategic plan, not bolted on as a separate initiative.

7. **Fact-Based Decision Making:** Decisions are driven by data (SPC, Pareto, histograms), not intuition.

8. **Communication:** Effective communication at all levels sustains the quality culture.

---

`(b)` **Discuss Deming's 14 Points for Management as a foundation of TQM. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

#### Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
W. Edwards Deming, the American statistician who revolutionized Japanese industry post-WWII, proposed 14 obligations that top management must adopt to achieve quality transformation.

**The 14 Points (5 Marks — explain any 6 in detail):**

1. **Create constancy of purpose for improvement:** Focus on long-term goals, not short-term profits. Plan for the future of the product and service.

2. **Adopt the new philosophy:** Top management must embrace quality as a personal responsibility. The old "acceptable quality" mindset must be rejected.

3. **Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality:** Build quality *into* the product at the design and process stage. Inspection is too late and too costly.

4. **End the practice of awarding business on price tag alone:** Purchase on the basis of *total cost*, considering quality and long-term supplier relationships.

5. **Improve constantly every process:** Use the PDCA cycle relentlessly. Every process has waste and variation that can be reduced.

6. **Institute training on the job:** Every employee must be trained in the specific skills and tools needed for their job.

7. **Institute leadership:** Leaders must help people and machines do a better job. Supervision should be supportive, not policing.

8. **Drive out fear:** Employees must feel safe to report problems and suggest improvements without fear of retribution.

9. **Break down barriers between departments:** Marketing, design, production, and sales must work as a single team.

10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations:** "Zero defects" posters cannot fix a broken system. The system must be improved first.

11. **Eliminate numerical quotas:** Quotas breed poor quality. Replace them with leadership and statistical methods.

12. **Remove barriers to pride of workmanship:** Eliminate arbitrary rating systems and annual merit reviews that erode self-respect.

13. **Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement:** Continuous learning for every employee.

14. **Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation:** Quality is everyone's job, every day.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Deming's 14 Points are not a checklist but a *cultural transformation manifesto*. They require unwavering top-management commitment and patience — typically 5 to 10 years of disciplined application to yield results.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks): Cost of Quality and Taguchi's Loss Function**

`(a)` **Define Cost of Quality. Explain its four categories with suitable examples. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Model Solution:

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total cost incurred by an organization in ensuring and assuring quality, plus the cost of failure to achieve that quality. It is the sum of all costs that would disappear if the product or service were built perfectly right the first time.

**The Four Categories (6 Marks):**

**1. Prevention Costs ($C_P$) (1.5 Marks):**
Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring in the first place.
*Examples:* Quality planning, employee training, process design, supplier capability audits, FMEA, design reviews, quality improvement projects.

**2. Appraisal Costs ($C_A$) (1.5 Marks):**
Costs incurred to *detect* defects through inspection, testing, and measurement.
*Examples:* Incoming material inspection, in-process inspection, final product testing, calibration of equipment, product audits.

**3. Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$) (1.5 Marks):**
Costs incurred when defects are found *before* the product reaches the customer.
*Examples:* Scrap, rework, re-inspection, defect analysis, machine downtime due to quality issues, material review.

**4. External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$) (1.5 Marks):**
Costs incurred when defects are discovered *after* the product reaches the customer.
*Examples:* Warranty claims, product recalls, returns, field repairs, customer complaints handling, loss of goodwill, legal liability.

> **Conclusion (Extra for full marks):** The first two categories are *conformance* costs; the latter two are *non-conformance* costs. TQM seeks to shift the cost balance toward prevention by recognizing that prevention is always cheaper than failure.

---

`(b)` **A company has the following annual quality cost data: $C_P$ = ₹ 2,00,000; $C_A$ = ₹ 4,00,000; $C_{IF}$ = ₹ 10,00,000; $C_{EF}$ = ₹ 14,00,000. Annual sales = ₹ 3,00,00,000. Calculate (i) Total COQ, (ii) COQ as % of sales, (iii) Conformance and Non-conformance costs. Comment on the quality cost structure. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified]`

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $C_P$ = ₹ 2,00,000
- $C_A$ = ₹ 4,00,000
- $C_{IF}$ = ₹ 10,00,000
- $C_{EF}$ = ₹ 14,00,000
- Sales = ₹ 3,00,00,000

**Step (i): Total COQ (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total COQ} &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total COQ = ₹ 30,00,000.**

**Step (ii): COQ as % of Sales (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{COQ} &= \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3}{300} \times 100 \\
&= 10.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**COQ = 10% of sales** — well above the world-class benchmark of <2.5%.

**Step (iii): Conformance vs. Non-conformance (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Conformance}} &= C_P + C_A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{\text{Non-conformance}} &= C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Ratio} &= 24 : 6 = 4 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Quality Cost Structure (1 Mark):**
The company is spending **four times more on failure costs than on prevention and appraisal combined**. This is a classic signature of a *reactive, inspection-driven* quality system. The organization should substantially increase prevention spending (training, design reviews, process FMEA) to drive failure costs down. Industry experience (Juran, Crosby) shows that doubling $C_P$ typically halves $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$, yielding net savings of 20-40% of total COQ.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in This Topic (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Conflating QC and QA:** Many students write "QA and QC are the same." This is the #1 mark-killer. Always present a clear comparison table and end with: *"QC detects; QA prevents."*
> 2. **Mere Listing of Deming's 14 Points:** Examiners expect *brief explanation* of each point. Listing all 14 with one-line descriptions fetches only 3 of 5 marks. Pick 6-8 and explain the *why*.
> 3. **Confusing Quality Policy with Quality Objectives:** Policy = *intent* (qualitative). Objectives = *measurable targets* (quantitative with SMART criteria).
> 4. **Skipping Units in COQ Calculations:** Always write "**₹ 30,00,000**" or "**10%**" — bare numbers without units lose 0.5-1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting Crosby's "Quality is Free":** This is a high-yield quote. Use it once in any TQM answer to signal deeper reading.
> 6. **Ignoring Taguchi's QLF in TQM questions:** Examiners frequently ask for one guru's philosophy in depth. Taguchi's $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$ is a frequent KTU favorite.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Quality Policy** is the *intent*; **Quality Objectives** are the *measurable targets* of that intent.
- [x] Policy + Objectives must be **communicated, understood, applied, and reviewed** (ISO 9001:2015, Clause 5.2).
- [x] **Quality Function** has two meanings: cross-functional coordination *and* **QFD (House of Quality)**. Always clarify which one your answer addresses.
- [x] **QFD House of Quality** priority formula: $W_j = \sum C_i \cdot R_{ij}$ and relative weight $w_j = (W_j / \sum W_k) \times 100\%$.
- [x] **QC** = Detective, reactive, product-focused → uses inspection/testing.
- [x] **QA** = Preventive, proactive, process-focused → uses design control, FMEA, audits.
- [x] **TQM = TOTAL** (product, people, process, culture) — driven by the **Five Gurus**: **D, J, C, I, T** (Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Taguchi).
- [x] **Deming** = PDCA + 14 Points + System thinking + "Drive out fear."
- [x] **Juran** = Quality Trilogy (Plan, Control, Improve) + customer-defined quality.
- [x] **Crosby** = Zero Defects + Conformance to requirements + PONC (Price of Non-Conformance) + "Quality is Free."
- [x] **Ishikawa** = Fishbone diagram + Quality Circles + CWQC.
- [x] **Taguchi** = Quality Loss Function $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$ + Robust Design.
- [x] **COQ = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$**; World-class target = **< 2.5% of sales**.
- [x] **TQM philosophy:** Investment in **prevention** yields disproportionate savings in **failure costs**.
- [x] **PDCA** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is the universal engine of continuous improvement.
- [x] SMART objectives: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- [x] Quality is **everyone's responsibility**, but **top management commitment** is the single largest predictor of TQM success.

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Quality Engineering (PCIET503)*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost of Quality, Loss Functions, Manufacturing Tolerances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cost of Quality (COQ)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
The **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is defined as a comprehensive financial measurement framework that quantifies the total expenditure incurred by an organization in ensuring that products and services conform to established quality standards. It explicitly includes the costs of prevention, appraisal, and the costs arising from both internal and external failures. COQ is a strategic management accounting tool used to evaluate the economic efficiency of an organization's quality management system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO1):** The student must be able to *explain the Cost of Quality concepts and categorize them into prevention, appraisal, internal failure, and external failure costs.* This is a **2-marker definition question** almost every KTU cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Interpretation:** COQ does NOT mean the cost of producing a high-quality product. It means the **cost of achieving (or failing to achieve) that quality level.** This is the most common conceptual trap in KTU exams.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you own a small bakery. To ensure your cakes are delicious, you:
1. **Prevent bad cakes** by training bakers, buying good flour, cleaning ovens (Prevention Costs).
2. **Inspect cakes** by tasting samples, weighing them, checking ovens (Appraisal Costs).
3. **Throw away or rework** a cake that is burnt or undercooked before the customer sees it (Internal Failure Costs).
4. **Compensate an angry customer** who finds a hair in the cake, or give a free replacement (External Failure Costs).

The total money spent across all four categories is your **Cost of Quality**. Notice that the *burnt cake thrown away* is also a quality cost — the money spent on flour, sugar, effort, and oven fuel is wasted. This is why minimizing failure costs (by spending more on prevention) usually *reduces* the total COQ — the cornerstone argument of **Philip Crosby's** "Quality is Free" philosophy.

---

### 1.2 Loss Functions (Taguchi Quality Loss Function)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QLF)** is a quadratic mathematical model proposed by **Genichi Taguchi** that quantifies the economic loss to society incurred when a product's quality characteristic deviates from its target value. Unlike the traditional "conformance-to-specification" view, Taguchi argued that any deviation from the target — even within specification limits — generates a loss to the customer, the manufacturer, and society.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO1):** *Apply Taguchi Loss Function to compute quality losses for nominal-the-best, smaller-the-better, and larger-the-better characteristics.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Suppose you order a custom wedding ring with an inner diameter of exactly **20.00 mm** so it fits your finger perfectly. The jeweller delivers a ring of 20.05 mm. Traditionally, if the tolerance is ±0.10 mm, the ring is "acceptable." But Taguchi says: even this 0.05 mm deviation causes a *tiny* loss — the ring slips slightly, irritates you, and you eventually take it to get resized (costing money and time). The further the ring is from 20.00 mm, the larger your loss grows **quadratically** (not linearly). That hidden, accumulated dissatisfaction is what the Loss Function captures.

**Key Taguchi Principles (with bold metrics):**
- Quality is **loss imparted to society** after a product is shipped.
- Loss is **financial**, not just technical.
- Loss occurs the moment the characteristic deviates from target — **even inside spec limits**.
- The loss function is **quadratic** in the deviation.

---

### 1.3 Manufacturing Tolerances

**Formal Definition:**
A **Manufacturing Tolerance** is the permissible limit of variation in a physical dimension, property, or characteristic of a manufactured part. Tolerances define the acceptable range within which a product remains functional and interchangeable. Tolerance design is the engineering discipline of assigning these limits optimally to minimize the total loss to society (combining manufacturing cost and functional loss).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a cylindrical piston that must slide into a cylinder block. If the piston is too tight, it jams. If too loose, gas leaks and the engine loses power. Tolerance is the engineer's "wiggle room" — the difference between the maximum allowable size and the minimum allowable size. A **tight tolerance** (e.g., ±0.001 mm) makes the engine work perfectly but costs a fortune to manufacture. A **loose tolerance** is cheap but makes the engine run poorly. Tolerance design is the art of finding the *optimum* — exactly what Taguchi's loss function enables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Taguchi Revolution:** Traditional engineering says "as long as the dimension is within ±0.05 mm, we are good." Taguchi proved that a dimension at ±0.05 mm generates a **25× higher loss** to society than a dimension at ±0.01 mm, even though both are "in spec." This is the **philosophical foundation** of robust design.

---

### 1.4 Interconnection of the Three Concepts (KTU Synthesis)

These three topics are tightly woven:

$$\text{Total Cost to Society} = \underbrace{\text{Manufacturing Cost}}_{\text{tight spec = expensive}} + \underbrace{\text{Loss from Deviation (QLF)}}_{\text{loose spec = high loss}}$$

The engineer's job is to choose tolerances that **minimize the sum** of these two costs. This is Taguchi's **two-step optimization**:
- **Step 1 (System Design):** Choose the design that minimizes the ideal functional loss.
- **Step 2 (Parameter Design):** Find nominal values and tolerances that minimize the sum of manufacturing cost and quality loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The PAF Model of Cost of Quality (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure)

This is the classical framework for classifying COQ, attributed to **Joseph Juran** (1951). It decomposes all quality-related expenditures into four categories.

#### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs
Expenditures incurred to **prevent defects** from occurring in the first place. These are the only category that *creates value* and *pays for itself*.

- Quality planning and administration
- New product quality engineering
- Supplier capability surveys
- Quality improvement programs (Kaizen, Six Sigma projects)
- Training of personnel in quality methods
- Quality audits (internal, system audits)
- Field testing
- Design reviews (DFMEA, PFMEA)

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Money spent on prevention is an **investment**. It is analogous to vaccinating children to avoid future hospital bills.

#### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs
Expenditures incurred to **detect defects** that have already occurred, *before* the product reaches the customer.

- Incoming material inspection
- In-process inspection (SPC, gaging)
- Final product testing and inspection
- Test equipment calibration and maintenance
- Costs of inspectors' time
- Product Quality audits

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Prevention prevents the defect; Appraisal finds the defect. Both are "conformance costs" — they ensure quality is delivered.

#### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs
Costs of defects discovered **before** the product is shipped to the customer.

- Scrap (material waste)
- Rework and repair
- Re-inspection of reworked items
- Material review / downgrade
- Failure analysis (root cause investigation)
- Production line stoppages due to defects
- Defective components lost in WIP (Work-In-Progress)

#### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs
Costs of defects discovered **after** the product reaches the customer. These are the most damaging and the hardest to quantify.

- Warranty claims
- Field service and repair
- Customer complaint handling
- Product recalls
- Returned materials (RMA) handling
- Loss of goodwill / brand reputation damage
- Litigation and legal settlements
- Concessions and discounts to retain angry customers
- Loss of market share

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *rework* (internal failure) with *warranty repair* (external failure). The line is drawn at the **shipping point** of the product. If the defect is found before shipping → internal. After shipping → external.

#### 2.1.5 The PAF Cost Optimization Curve

When plotted against the level of prevention investment, the four cost categories behave predictably:

| Cost Category | Behavior as Prevention Increases |
|---|---|
| Prevention Costs | **Increases linearly** |
| Appraisal Costs | First increases, then **decreases** (less need to inspect) |
| Internal Failure | **Decreases sharply** |
| External Failure | **Decreases sharply** (delayed effect) |
| **Total COQ** | Has a **U-shape** with a clear minimum (optimum) |

The **optimal point** is where the marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal savings from reduced failure costs.

#### 2.1.6 Alternative COQ Models

| Model | Proposed By | Philosophy |
|---|---|---|
| **PAF Model** | Juran | Prevention + Appraisal = Conformance; Failure = Non-conformance |
| **Process Cost Model** | Feigenbaum | Quality costs vary by the *stage* of the process |
| **ABC Model** | Activity-Based Costing | Quality costs traced to specific activities/products |
| **Crosby's PFA Model** | Philip Crosby | Price of Conformance (POC) + Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) |

---

### 2.2 Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QLF) — Deep Theory

#### 2.2.1 The Quadratic Loss Function (Nominal-is-Best)

For a quality characteristic **y** with target value **m**, the loss to society is:

$$L(y) = K \cdot (y - m)^{2}$$

where:
- $L(y)$ = Quality loss in monetary units (e.g., ₹, $, €)
- $K$ = Proportionality constant (engineering cost coefficient)
- $y$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $m$ = Target (nominal) value
- $(y - m)$ = Deviation from target

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **loss is zero only at y = m**, NOT anywhere within the specification limits. This is Taguchi's paradigm shift. Even if a part is "within spec," it still incurs a financial loss if it is not exactly at the target.

#### 2.2.2 Determining the Constant K (KTU Derivative Step)

The constant **K** is found by anchoring the loss function to a known boundary condition. Suppose at the specification limit $\Delta_0$ (where $\Delta_0$ is the tolerance band half-width, i.e., $y = m \pm \Delta_0$), the loss to society equals **A** (the cost of replacing the product, or the customer's maximum tolerable loss).

At the boundary:
$$A = K \cdot \Delta_0^{2}$$

Solving for K:

$$K = \frac{A}{\Delta_0^{2}}$$

#### 2.2.3 The Three Categories of Quality Characteristics

| Type | Target | Loss Function Form | When Deviation Increases |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal-is-Best (NB)** | $m$ is finite (e.g., 20.0 mm) | $L(y) = K(y-m)^{2}$ | Loss increases in *both* directions |
| **Smaller-is-Better (SB)** | $m = 0$ ideal | $L(y) = K \cdot y^{2}$ | Loss increases as $y$ grows above 0 |
| **Larger-is-Better (LB)** | $m = \infty$ ideal | $L(y) = K \cdot \dfrac{1}{y^{2}}$ | Loss increases as $y$ drops below $\infty$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples (for KTU 2-mark questions):**
> - **NB:** Piston diameter (must be exactly 50.00 mm), Resistance value of a resistor.
> - **SB:** Impurity content, Surface roughness (Ra), Leakage rate, Noise level.
> - **LB:** Strength of concrete, Battery life, Adhesion strength, Fuel efficiency.

#### 2.2.4 Expected Loss Over a Production Run

If the process produces parts with random variation characterized by mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the **average (expected) loss per unit** is:

$$E[L] = K \cdot \left[ (\mu - m)^{2} + \sigma^{2} \right]$$

This decomposes loss into two sources:
- **Bias loss** $= K \cdot (\mu - m)^{2}$ — from a mis-centered process.
- **Variance loss** $= K \cdot \sigma^{2}$ — from random process spread.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** To minimize loss, an engineer should *first* center the process (eliminate bias) and *then* reduce variance. This is the **two-step strategy** Taguchi proposed.

#### 2.2.5 Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio

Taguchi's experimental design uses the S/N ratio to express robustness. For different characteristic types:

- **NB (Nominal-is-Best):**
$$\eta = 10 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\mu^{2}}{\sigma^{2}} \right)$$

- **SB (Smaller-is-Better):**
$$\eta = -10 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_{i}^{2} \right)$$

- **LB (Larger-is-Better):**
$$\eta = -10 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_{i}^{2}} \right)$$

A **higher S/N ratio** means lower loss and more robust design.

---

### 2.3 Manufacturing Tolerances — Deep Theory

#### 2.3.1 Why Tolerances Exist

Every manufacturing process has inherent variability (the "voice of the process"). Tolerances are the engineer's contract between **function** (the part must work) and **manufacturability** (the part must be affordable to make).

#### 2.3.2 Two-Way vs One-Way Tolerances

- **Two-way (Bilateral) tolerance:** Dimension is $m \pm \Delta_0$ (e.g., $20.00 \pm 0.05$ mm).
- **One-way (Unilateral) tolerance:** Dimension is $m^{+ \Delta_0}_{+0}$ or $m^{+0}_{- \Delta_0}$ — deviation allowed in only one direction. Common for clearance holes, mating parts.

#### 2.3.3 Tolerance Stack-Up (Worst-Case Statistical)

When parts are assembled, individual tolerances accumulate. For *n* dimensions stacked in series:

- **Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Stack-Up:**
$$\Delta_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i$$

This is overly conservative; used for safety-critical (aerospace, nuclear) applications.

- **Statistical (RSS — Root Sum of Squares) Stack-Up:**
$$\Delta_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i^{2}}$$

Used when dimensions are independent and randomly distributed.

#### 2.3.4 Process Capability Indices

Process capability is quantified through capability indices, which directly connect manufacturing tolerance with actual process spread:

$$C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min \left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

| Index | Meaning | KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{p}$ | Process *potential* (spread only) | Does not consider centering |
| $C_{pk}$ | Process *performance* (spread + centering) | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is industry-acceptable |
| $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ | Six-Sigma world-class quality | Defect rate < 3.4 ppm |
| $C_{pk} < 1.0$ | Process is not capable | Mean and/or spread is too large |

#### 2.3.5 Taguchi's Tolerance Design Strategy

Taguchi's approach to tolerance design is revolutionary:

1. **Functional Tolerances** are determined from the **Loss Function** (what deviation can society tolerate before loss becomes significant?).
2. **Manufacturing Tolerances** are determined from the **process capability** (how tightly can the process actually hold?).
3. The final assigned tolerance is the **wider** of the two (you cannot manufacture tighter than process capability allows, and you should not specify tighter than functional need).
4. If the manufacturing tolerance is wider than functional tolerance → **redesign the process** (not the tolerance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traditional approach:** Tighten tolerance → lower defect rate → assume lower cost.
> **Taguchi approach:** Evaluate loss at current tolerance using QLF. If loss > cost of tighter manufacturing → tighten. Otherwise, do not. This minimizes **total cost = manufacturing cost + quality loss**.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nominal-is-Best QLF | $L(y) = K(y - m)^{2}$ | $L$ in ₹, $(y-m)$ in measurement units |
| 2 | Constant K (NB) | $K = A / \Delta_0^{2}$ | $A$ = replacement cost, $\Delta_0$ = spec half-width |
| 3 | Smaller-is-Best QLF | $L(y) = K \cdot y^{2}$ | Target $m = 0$ |
| 4 | Larger-is-Best QLF | $L(y) = K / y^{2}$ | Target $m = \infty$ |
| 5 | Expected Average Loss | $E[L] = K[(\mu-m)^{2} + \sigma^{2}]$ | Per unit average loss |
| 6 | S/N Ratio (NB) | $\eta = 10 \log_{10}(\mu^{2}/\sigma^{2})$ | dB; higher is better |
| 7 | S/N Ratio (SB) | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}(\overline{y^{2}})$ | dB; higher is better |
| 8 | S/N Ratio (LB) | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}(\overline{1/y^{2}})$ | dB; higher is better |
| 9 | Worst-Case Tolerance Stack | $\Delta_{\text{total}} = \sum \Delta_i$ | Conservative; arithmetic sum |
| 10 | RSS Tolerance Stack | $\Delta_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum \Delta_i^{2}}$ | Statistical; assumes independence |
| 11 | Process Capability $C_p$ | $C_p = (\text{USL}-\text{LSL})/(6\sigma)$ | Spread only |
| 12 | Process Capability $C_{pk}$ | $C_{pk} = \min[(\text{USL}-\mu)/(3\sigma), (\mu-\text{LSL})/(3\sigma)]$ | Spread + centering |
| 13 | Six-Sigma Defect Rate | $C_{pk} \geq 2.0 \Rightarrow \leq 3.4$ ppm | World-class benchmark |
| 14 | Total COQ | $\text{Total COQ} = P + A + IF + EF$ | Sum of all four cost categories |
| 15 | Conformance Cost | $P + A$ | Prevention + Appraisal |
| 16 | Non-Conformance Cost | $IF + EF$ | Internal + External Failure |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** In Part B 14-mark questions, students are typically given a scenario (e.g., a resistor with target 100 Ω, tolerance ±5 Ω, replacement cost ₹50) and asked to (a) derive K, (b) compute expected loss given process mean and sigma, (c) recommend whether to tighten tolerance. Memorize formulas 1, 2, 5, and 11/12 by heart.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Taguchi's Quadratic Loss Function from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A quality characteristic has target $m$. When the characteristic deviates by a small amount $\delta = (y - m)$, customers experience dissatisfaction proportional to the deviation squared. Show that the loss function is quadratic.

**Step 1 — Define the Loss Function as a Taylor Series**

The loss $L(y)$ is a smooth, continuous function of $y$ with a global minimum at $y = m$. By Taylor's theorem, we expand $L(y)$ around $y = m$:

$$L(y) = L(m) + L'(m)(y-m) + \frac{L''(m)}{2!}(y-m)^{2} + \frac{L'''(m)}{3!}(y-m)^{3} + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions**

*Condition (a):* The loss is *zero* at the target (lossless condition).

$$L(m) = 0$$

*Condition (b):* The first derivative at the target is *zero* (the target is a stationary point — a minimum).

$$L'(m) = 0$$

*Condition (c):* The second derivative at the target is a *positive constant* $2K$ (a stable minimum, not a saddle point).

$$L''(m) = 2K, \quad K > 0$$

**Step 3 — Truncate the Series**

By Taguchi's assumption (verified empirically for most quality characteristics), the higher-order terms are negligible for small deviations:

$$\frac{L'''(m)}{3!}(y-m)^{3} + \frac{L^{(4)}(m)}{4!}(y-m)^{4} + \cdots \approx 0$$

**Step 4 — Write the Final Form**

Substituting back:

$$L(y) = 0 + 0 \cdot (y-m) + \frac{2K}{2!}(y-m)^{2} + 0$$

$$L(y) = K(y - m)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,L(y) = K(y - m)^{2}\,}$$

This derivation proves the **quadratic form is the simplest non-trivial loss function** consistent with the physics of customer dissatisfaction near the target. The assumption of negligibility of higher-order terms is called the *small-deviation approximation* and is valid when $\vert y - m \vert \leq \Delta_0$ (i.e., within specification limits).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Determining the Engineering Constant K (Boundary-Value Method)

**Problem:** A voltage regulator has target output $m = 12.0$ V. The specification limit is $\Delta_0 = 0.5$ V (i.e., acceptable range is $11.5$ V to $12.5$ V). If the regulator fails, the customer suffers a loss of $A = ₹ 400$ (replacement + labor cost). Determine K.

**Step 1 — State the Known Boundary Condition**

At the specification boundary $y = m + \Delta_0 = 12.5$ V, the loss equals A.

**Step 2 — Apply the QLF**

$$A = K(y - m)^{2} = K \cdot \Delta_0^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$400 = K \cdot (0.5)^{2}$$

$$400 = K \cdot 0.25$$

**Step 4 — Solve for K**

$$K = \frac{400}{0.25} = 1600 \;\; \text{₹/V}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,K = 1600 \; \text{₹/V}^{2}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Compute Loss at Boundary)**

$$L(12.5) = 1600 \times (0.5)^{2} = 1600 \times 0.25 = ₹ 400 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute Loss at a Mid-Spec Point (e.g., y = 12.25 V)**

Deviation: $y - m = 0.25$ V.

$$L(12.25) = 1600 \times (0.25)^{2} = 1600 \times 0.0625 = ₹ 100$$

This shows the loss at $y = 12.25$ V (still "in spec") is **₹100**, demonstrating Taguchi's point that even in-spec parts generate loss.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Expected Loss for a Production Run (Statistical)

**Problem:** The above voltage regulator is produced in batches. A process has mean $\mu = 12.05$ V and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.10$ V. Compute the expected loss per unit.

**Step 1 — Use the Expected Value Operator**

The expected loss is the mean of $L(y)$ over the distribution of $y$:

$$E[L] = E[K(y - m)^{2}] = K \cdot E[(y - m)^{2}]$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Square**

$$E[(y - m)^{2}] = E[(y - \mu + \mu - m)^{2}]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Variance Identity**

For any random variable $X$ with mean $\mu_X$, $E[(X - c)^{2}] = \text{Var}(X) + (\mu_X - c)^{2}$.

Let $X = y$, $c = m$. Then $\text{Var}(y) = \sigma^{2}$ and $\mu_y = \mu$.

$$E[(y - m)^{2}] = \sigma^{2} + (\mu - m)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Variance Identity into the Expected Loss**

$$E[L] = K[\sigma^{2} + (\mu - m)^{2}]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Values**

Given: $K = 1600$ ₹/V², $\sigma = 0.10$ V, $\mu = 12.05$ V, $m = 12.0$ V.

Bias deviation: $\mu - m = 0.05$ V.

$$E[L] = 1600 \times [(0.10)^{2} + (0.05)^{2}]$$

$$E[L] = 1600 \times [0.01 + 0.0025]$$

$$E[L] = 1600 \times 0.0125$$

$$\boxed{\,E[L] = ₹ 20 \; \text{per unit}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

- Variance loss: $1600 \times 0.01 = ₹ 16$ per unit.
- Bias loss: $1600 \times 0.0025 = ₹ 4$ per unit.
- Total ₹20 per unit. If the manufacturer produces 1,00,000 units per year, the **annual expected loss** is $₹ 20 \times 1,00,000 = ₹ 20,00,000$ — a powerful argument for process improvement.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Process Capability $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ for the Same Regulator

**Given:** USL = 12.5 V, LSL = 11.5 V, $\mu = 12.05$ V, $\sigma = 0.10$ V.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{12.5 - 11.5}{6 \times 0.10} = \frac{1.0}{0.6}$$

$$C_p \approx 1.667$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (Upper)**

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.5 - 12.05}{0.30} = \frac{0.45}{0.30} = 1.50$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (Lower)**

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.05 - 11.5}{0.30} = \frac{0.55}{0.30} \approx 1.833$$

**Step 4 — Take the Minimum**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.50, \; 1.833) = 1.50$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

The process is **centered high** (closer to USL). $C_p = 1.667$ means the process *spread* alone fits within spec with 67% headroom. $C_{pk} = 1.50$ means the process is *performing* acceptably (industry threshold ≥ 1.33) but is **biased upward**. The corrective action is to shift the process mean to $\mu = 12.0$ V.

**Step 6 — Centered Performance Check (theoretical maximum)**

If $\mu$ were shifted to 12.0 V:

$$C_{pk} = \frac{0.5}{0.30} = 1.667 = C_p$$

Centered → $C_{pk} = C_p$ → process is fully capable.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Total Cost of Quality (PAF) Optimization

**Problem:** A company produces 10,000 units/month. Current cost structure:

| Category | Cost/Unit (₹) | Total (₹/month) |
|---|---|---|
| Prevention | 20 | 2,00,000 |
| Appraisal | 30 | 3,00,000 |
| Internal Failure | 50 | 5,00,000 |
| External Failure | 100 | 10,00,000 |
| **Total COQ** | **200** | **20,00,000** |

The manager proposes to **double prevention spending to ₹40/unit**. Historical data shows: every ₹1 increase in prevention reduces internal failure by ₹3 and external failure by ₹4, while appraisal drops by ₹0.50.

**Step 1 — Compute New Prevention Cost**

Increase in prevention: $20 \rightarrow 40$ ₹/unit, i.e., +₹20/unit.

**Step 2 — Compute New Internal Failure**

Reduction: $3 \times 20 = 60$ ₹/unit. New IF: $50 - 60 = -10$ ₹/unit.

**Step 3 — Compute New External Failure**

Reduction: $4 \times 20 = 80$ ₹/unit. New EF: $100 - 80 = 20$ ₹/unit.

**Step 4 — Compute New Appraisal**

Reduction: $0.5 \times 20 = 10$ ₹/unit. New A: $30 - 10 = 20$ ₹/unit.

**Step 5 — New Total COQ**

| Category | New Cost/Unit (₹) |
|---|---|
| Prevention | 40 |
| Appraisal | 20 |
| Internal Failure | 0 (clamped to non-negative) |
| External Failure | 20 |
| **New Total COQ** | **80** |

$$\Delta \text{COQ} = 200 - 80 = ₹120 \text{ per unit saved}$$

**Step 6 — Monthly Savings**

$$10,000 \times 120 = ₹ 12,00,000 \text{ per month saved}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{Monthly Savings} = ₹ 12 \; \text{lakhs}\,}$$

This is the **PAF optimization** in action — the empirical justification for "Quality is Free."

---

### 3.6 Master Analytical Matrix — Mapping Inputs to Outputs

| Given Quantity | Use Formula | Compute | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$, $\Delta_0$ | $K = A/\Delta_0^{2}$ | Engineering cost coefficient | ₹/unit² |
| $K$, $y$, $m$ | $L(y) = K(y-m)^{2}$ | Loss at a point | ₹ |
| $K$, $\mu$, $m$, $\sigma$ | $E[L] = K[(\mu-m)^{2}+\sigma^{2}]$ | Expected per-unit loss | ₹ |
| USL, LSL, $\sigma$ | $C_p = (\text{USL}-\text{LSL})/(6\sigma)$ | Process capability | dimensionless |
| USL, LSL, $\mu$, $\sigma$ | $C_{pk} = \min$ formula | Process performance | dimensionless |
| $\Delta_i$ (parts in series) | $\Delta_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum \Delta_i^{2}}$ | Statistical stack-up | engineering units |
| $n$, $y_i$ (SB) | $\eta = -10\log_{10}(\overline{y^{2}})$ | S/N ratio | dB |
