## ✅ PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between control charts for variables and control charts for attributes. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Variables Charts | Attributes Charts |
|---|---|---|
| Data type | Continuous (measurable) | Discrete (counts / classifications) |
| Information | Magnitude of quality characteristic | Conformity / non-conformity |
| Charts | $\bar{X}$–R, X–MR, $\bar{X}$–S | p, np, c, u |
| Computation | Uses mean and range | Uses proportions or counts |
| **Example** | Diameter of a piston (mm) | Number of defective bulbs in a lot of 100 |

(1 mark for table, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for distinguishing data type)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the formula for the Upper Control Limit of the $\bar{X}$ chart when subgroup size n = 5, and explain the role of the constant A₂.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$

For n = 5, **A₂ = 0.577**.

**Role of A₂ (1 mark):** A₂ is a tabulated constant that depends on subgroup size n. It equals $\dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$, where $d_2$ is the expected value of the range of n standard normal variables divided by σ. A₂ converts the average range $\bar{R}$ into the **3-sigma control limit width** for the subgroup mean, avoiding direct estimation of σ.

(1 mark formula, 1 mark value, 1 mark role of A₂)

---

## ✅ PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### **📝 Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** The following data represent the sample means and ranges for 8 subgroups of size n = 5:

| Subgroup | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$ | 28 | 30 | 27 | 32 | 31 | 29 | 30 | 31 |
| R | 4 | 5 | 3 | 6 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 |

Construct the $\bar{X}$ chart and R chart. (Use A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114 for n = 5). Comment on the state of control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process produces items in lots of 200. The number of defectives in 10 successive samples is:
8, 12, 15, 10, 9, 13, 11, 14, 12, 16. Construct an **np-chart** and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — $\bar{X}$–R Chart (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$ (2 marks)**
$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{28+30+27+32+31+29+30+31}{8} = \dfrac{238}{8} = 29.75
$$
$$
\bar{R} = \dfrac{4+5+3+6+4+5+4+3}{8} = \dfrac{34}{8} = 4.25
$$

**Step 2: $\bar{X}$ Chart Control Limits (2 marks)**
$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 29.75 + (0.577)(4.25) = 29.75 + 2.452 = 32.20
$$
$$
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 29.75
$$
$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 29.75 - 2.452 = 27.30
$$

**Step 3: R Chart Control Limits (1 mark)**
$$
\text{UCL}_R = (2.114)(4.25) = 8.98
$$
$$
\text{CL}_R = 4.25
$$
$$
\text{LCL}_R = 0
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (2 marks)**

| Subgroup | $\bar{X}_i$ | In [27.30, 32.20]? | $R_i$ | In [0, 8.98]? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 28 | ✓ | 4 | ✓ |
| 2 | 30 | ✓ | 5 | ✓ |
| 3 | 27 | ✓ (boundary) | 3 | ✓ |
| 4 | 32 | ✓ | 6 | ✓ |
| 5 | 31 | ✓ | 4 | ✓ |
| 6 | 29 | ✓ | 5 | ✓ |
| 7 | 30 | ✓ | 4 | ✓ |
| 8 | 31 | ✓ | 3 | ✓ |

**Conclusion:** All subgroup means lie within [27.30, 32.20] and all ranges within [0, 8.98]. The process is **in statistical control**. These trial limits are accepted as **revised control limits** for future monitoring.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — np-Chart (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute $\bar{p}$ (2 marks)**
$$
\sum D_i = 8+12+15+10+9+13+11+14+12+16 = 120
$$
$$
\bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum D_i}{kn} = \dfrac{120}{10 \times 200} = \dfrac{120}{2000} = 0.06
$$

**Step 2: Compute CL, UCL, LCL for np-chart (2 marks)**
$$
\text{CL}_{np} = n\bar{p} = (200)(0.06) = 12
$$
$$
\sigma_{np} = \sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} = \sqrt{(200)(0.06)(0.94)} = \sqrt{11.28} = 3.359
$$
$$
\text{UCL}_{np} = 12 + 3(3.359) = 12 + 10.08 = 22.08
$$
$$
\text{LCL}_{np} = 12 - 10.08 = 1.92
$$

**Step 3: Plot and Interpret (3 marks)**

| Sample | Dᵢ | Status (1.92, 22.08) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | In |
| 2 | 12 | In |
| 3 | 15 | In |
| 4 | 10 | In |
| 5 | 9 | In |
| 6 | 13 | In |
| 7 | 11 | In |
| 8 | 14 | In |
| 9 | 12 | In |
| 10 | 16 | In |

**Conclusion:** All points are within control limits → process is **in statistical control**. The average number of defectives per sample is 12 with limits 1.92 to 22.08.

---

### **📝 Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Define a control chart. Explain the procedure for constructing an $\bar{X}$–R chart with subgroup size n = 5. State the formulas for control limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fabric manufacturer inspects 15 rolls of cloth. The number of defects per roll observed is:
6, 8, 5, 7, 4, 9, 6, 7, 5, 8, 6, 4, 7, 5, 6. Construct a **c-chart** and comment on the state of the process. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Theory (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** A control chart is a time-sequenced plot of a sample statistic (e.g., $\bar{X}$, R, p) with a center line representing the process average and upper/lower control limits set at ±3σ from the center line. It is used to monitor whether a process is in statistical control.

**Procedure for $\bar{X}$–R chart with n = 5 (4 marks):**

1. **Collect data:** Take k = 20 to 25 subgroups, each of size n = 5, at regular time intervals (rational subgroups).
2. **Compute statistics:** For each subgroup, calculate $\bar{X}_i$ and $R_i$.
3. **Compute grand mean and mean range:** $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum\bar{X}_i$ and $\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum R_i$.
4. **Compute trial control limits:**
   - $\bar{X}$ chart: UCL = $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, CL = $\bar{\bar{X}}$, LCL = $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$.
   - R chart: UCL = $D_4 \bar{R}$, CL = $\bar{R}$, LCL = $D_3 \bar{R}$.
5. For n = 5: A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114.
6. **Plot** all $\bar{X}_i$ and $R_i$ on their respective charts.
7. **Investigate** any point outside the limits → identify special cause → remove it.
8. **Recompute** limits using only in-control data → these become the **revised control limits** for future monitoring.

**Formulas (1 mark):**
$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \quad ; \quad \text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}
$$
$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} \quad ; \quad \text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — c-Chart (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute $\bar{c}$ (2 marks)**
$$
\sum c_i = 6+8+5+7+4+9+6+7+5+8+6+4+7+5+6 = 93
$$
$$
\bar{c} = \dfrac{93}{15} = 6.2
$$

**Step 2: Compute control limits (2 marks)**
$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\bar{c}} = \sqrt{6.2} = 2.490
$$
$$
\text{UCL}_c = 6.2 + 3(2.490) = 6.2 + 7.47 = 13.67
$$
$$
\text{LCL}_c = 6.2 - 7.47 = -1.27 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0
$$
$$
\text{CL}_c = 6.2
$$

**Step 3: Plot and interpret (3 marks)**

All observed c values (max = 9) are between 0 and 13.67. Therefore, the process is **in statistical control**. The average number of defects per fabric roll is 6.2 with control limits [0, 13.67].

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS**
> 1. **Wrong constant selection:** Using A₂ = 0.729 (n = 4) instead of A₂ = 0.577 (n = 5). Always verify n first.
> 2. **Forgetting to round LCL to 0 for c-charts and u-charts:** Negative defect counts are physically meaningless. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 3. **Computing $\bar{p}$ using $\sum p_i / k$ instead of $\sum D_i / \sum n_i$** — this is a **fatal error** for variable-n p-charts. Always use the **weighted average**.
> 4. **Confusing p-chart with c-chart:** p-chart plots *fraction defective*; c-chart plots *number of defects*. The denominator and underlying distribution (binomial vs Poisson) differ.
> 5. **Forgetting the trial vs revised limit step:** If any point lies outside trial limits, KTU expects you to **revise** $\bar{p}$ or $\bar{c}$ after removing those points. Skipping this loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Using $\bar{p}$ for np-chart without multiplying by n:** $\text{CL}_{np} = n\bar{p}$, not $\bar{p}$.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember High-Yield Points for KTU Exam:**

- ⭐ **Control chart** = Shewhart's SPC tool, 3σ limits.
- ⭐ **$\bar{X}$ chart** monitors **mean**; **R chart** monitors **variability** (always plot and interpret R chart FIRST).
- ⭐ **A₂ = 3/(d₂√n)** is the multiplier for $\bar{X}$ chart limits.
- ⭐ **R chart LCL = D₃$\bar{R}$ = 0** for n ≤ 6.
- ⭐ **X–MR chart** is for **n = 1**; uses $d_2 = 1.128$ to estimate σ.
- ⭐ **p-chart** for **variable sample size**; **np-chart** for **constant n**.
- ⭐ **$\bar{p} = \sum D_i / \sum n_i$** (weighted, not simple average).
- ⭐ **c-chart** for **fixed inspection unit**; **u-chart** for **variable inspection unit**.
- ⭐ **LCL < 0 → set to 0** for c and u charts.
- ⭐ **Trial limits → revised limits** methodology is mandatory in long-answer questions.
- ⭐ **Out-of-control signals:** point beyond limits, 2-of-3 beyond 2σ, 4-of-5 beyond 1σ, 8-in-a-row on one side.
- ⭐ **Control limits ≠ Specification limits** (USL/LSL).
- ⭐ **Variables charts** give **more information per sample** → preferred when data is measurable.
- ⭐ **Attributes charts** are **simpler** but **less powerful** — they only distinguish good from bad.
- ⭐ The **R chart is always interpreted before** the $\bar{X}$ chart because if variability is out of control, the $\bar{X}$ chart interpretation is meaningless.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State of Control and Process Out of Control Identification in Control Charts, Patterns in Control Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** State of Control and Process Out of Control Identification in Control Charts, Patterns in Control Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 State of Statistical Control — Formal Definition

A process is said to be in a **State of Statistical Control** (also called *in-control*) when it operates under the influence of only **Common Cause Variation** (also termed *chance causes*, *random causes*, or *inherent variation*). In this state, the output variation is predictable, stable, and falls within established statistical limits known as **Control Limits**, conventionally set at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Verbatim Definition):**
> *"A process is considered to be in statistical control when the variation in its output is solely attributable to chance/random causes (common causes) and the plotted points on the control chart fall within the upper and lower control limits with no discernible non-random patterns."*

Conversely, an **Out-of-Control** state exists when the process exhibits **Special Cause Variation** (also known as *assignable causes* or *non-random variation*). These are external, identifiable, and removable sources of variability that disrupt the inherent process stability.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The River Flow Model

Imagine a river flowing naturally through a plain. The river's natural meandering, water ripples, and flow fluctuations represent **common cause variation** — these are inherent, expected, and cannot be eliminated without fundamentally changing the river itself. The river is *in control*.

Now, suppose a large boulder is dropped into the riverbed, or a dam is constructed upstream. The flow pattern dramatically changes — water splashes unusually, or the water level rises significantly. These represent **special cause variation** — they are *assignable* (we can point to the boulder or dam) and *removable* (remove the boulder, flow returns to natural state). The river is now *out of control*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** A process in statistical control is **not** necessarily a *capable* process. A process can be stable (in control) yet fail customer specifications. Stability and capability are two independent dimensions of process performance.

## 1.3 Common Cause vs Special Cause Variation — The Foundation

| Attribute | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Inherent to the process | External to the process |
| **Predictability** | Predictable, statistical | Unpredictable, sporadic |
| **Magnitude** | Small, consistent | Large, dramatic |
| **Removability** | Only via system redesign | Identifiable and removable |
| **Frequency** | Always present | Intermittent |
| **Statistical Term** | Noise (random) | Signal (non-random) |
| **Examples** | Tool wear, material inconsistency (baseline) | Machine breakdown, operator error, power surge |

## 1.4 Control Limits vs Specification Limits — A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *Control Limits* with *Specification Limits*. They are entirely different statistical entities.

- **Control Limits (UCL, LCL):** Determined purely by process data (voice of the process). Computed as $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$.
- **Specification Limits (USL, LSL):** Determined by customer requirements (voice of the customer). Set by design/engineering.

A process can be **in control** (points within control limits) but **incapable** (process spread exceeds specification range). This is detected through **Process Capability Indices** such as $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Sigma Control Limit Philosophy

The choice of $\pm 3\sigma$ as control limits is rooted in the **Normal Distribution** properties. For a process characterized by mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$$

This means that under purely common cause variation, only **0.27%** of points (roughly 3 out of 1000) are expected to fall outside the control limits. The probability of a point falling *beyond* either limit due to chance alone is:

$$P(X > \mu + 3\sigma) = P(X < \mu - 3\sigma) = 0.00135$$

This small false-alarm probability is called the **Type I Error** or **Producer's Risk** ($\alpha$).

## 2.2 Western Electric Rules — The 8 Pattern Detection Rules

The **Western Electric Statistical Quality Control Handbook (1956, revised 1985)** formalized a set of supplementary run rules to detect non-random behavior *within* the control limits. These are essential for KTU Part A and Part B questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Core:** Western Electric Rules are explicitly part of the syllabus under "Process Out of Control Identification."

### The 8 Western Electric Rules:

**Rule 1:** *One point beyond the 3-sigma control limit.*
$$\text{Any point} > UCL \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Any point} < LCL$$

**Rule 2:** *2 out of 3 successive points beyond the 2-sigma warning limit (on the same side).*
$$2 \text{ of } 3 \text{ points} > \mu + 2\sigma \quad \text{or} \quad 2 \text{ of } 3 \text{ points} < \mu - 2\sigma$$

**Rule 3:** *4 out of 5 successive points beyond the 1-sigma limit (on the same side).*
$$4 \text{ of } 5 \text{ points} > \mu + 1\sigma \quad \text{or} \quad 4 \text{ of } 5 \text{ points} < \mu - 1\sigma$$

**Rule 4:** *8 successive points on the same side of the centerline (a "shift").*

**Rule 5:** *6 successive points trending in one direction (increasing or decreasing) — a "trend."*

**Rule 6:** *15 successive points within the 1-sigma zone (on either side) — "stratification."*

**Rule 7:** *14 successive points alternating up and down — "systematic variation."*

**Rule 8:** *8 successive points beyond the 1-sigma zone on both sides — "mixture."*

## 2.3 Nelson Rules — 7 Additional Rules

**Lloyd S. Nelson (1984, 1985)** proposed additional rules. The most commonly cited are:

1. One point beyond Zone A (3σ)
2. 9 points in a row on one side of the centerline
3. 6 points in a row steadily increasing or decreasing
4. 14 points in a row alternating up and down
5. 2 out of 3 points in Zone A or beyond (on the same side)
6. 4 out of 5 points in Zone B or beyond (on the same side)
7. 15 points in a row in Zone C (within ±1σ, both sides)

Where **Zone A** = ±2σ to ±3σ, **Zone B** = ±1σ to ±2σ, **Zone C** = centerline to ±1σ.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Mandate:** This table uses `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table corruption. All formulas are mathematically verifiable against standard SPC textbooks.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | UCL (X-bar chart) | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper Control Limit for means |
| 2 | LCL (X-bar chart) | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower Control Limit for means |
| 3 | CL (X-bar chart) | $CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$ | Grand mean of sample means |
| 4 | UCL (R-chart) | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | Upper Control Limit for ranges |
| 5 | LCL (R-chart) | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Lower Control Limit for ranges |
| 6 | 3-Sigma Rule | $UCL = \mu + 3\sigma$ | Standard control limit definition |
| 7 | Zone A (Upper) | $\mu + 2\sigma \text{ to } \mu + 3\sigma$ | Western Electric zone designation |
| 8 | Zone B (Upper) | $\mu + 1\sigma \text{ to } \mu + 2\sigma$ | Middle warning zone |
| 9 | Zone C (Upper) | $\mu \text{ to } \mu + 1\sigma$ | Inner zone (stratification) |
| 10 | False Alarm Rate | $\alpha = 0.0027$ | Type I error at 3σ |
| 11 | Process Capability | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Capability index |
| 12 | Process Performance | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Centered capability index |
| 13 | Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Standard deviation of sample means |
| 14 | Average Run Length (In-control) | $ARL_0 = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370$ | Expected points before false alarm |
| 15 | ARL (Out-of-control) | $ARL_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 - \beta}$ | Expected points to detect shift |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application

Control chart pattern analysis is the **backbone of Statistical Process Control (SPC)** in:

- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Detecting micron-scale drifts in wafer etching
- **Automotive Industry:** Monitoring torque, dimensional tolerances on assembly lines
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Ensuring tablet weight, dissolution consistency per FDA/ICH-Q8-Q10 guidelines
- **Process Industries:** Chemical, oil & gas — flow rate, pressure, temperature monitoring
- **Six Sigma DMAIC:** Pattern detection is the "Measure & Control" phase cornerstone

The cost of ignoring out-of-control patterns is quantified via the **Taguchi Loss Function**:

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^2$$

where any deviation from target $m$ incurs a quadratic loss to society.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 3-Sigma Control Limits

### Step 1 — Establish the Statistical Foundation

For a process with output variable $X$ following a Normal Distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the probability density function is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

The control chart samples this process in subgroups of size $n$ at regular time intervals.

### Step 2 — Derive the Distribution of the Sample Mean

By the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, the sample mean $\bar{X}$ for samples of size $n$ drawn from a normal population also follows a normal distribution with:

$$E(\bar{X}) = \mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$$

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

Therefore, the standard error of the mean is:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the 3-Sigma Empirical Rule

Using the standard convention of $3\sigma$ limits to capture ~99.73% of the variation:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu_{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu + \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu_{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu - \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = \mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Estimable Quantities

Since $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown, we substitute the sample estimators $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}/d_2$:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$$

where the constant $A_2$ is derived as:

$$A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

with $d_2$ being a tabulated constant based on subgroup size $n$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Average Run Length (ARL)

### Step 1 — Define the Concept

The **Average Run Length (ARL)** is the average number of plotted points required before a control chart signals an out-of-control condition.

### Step 2 — ARL for an In-Control Process

The probability that any single point exceeds the control limits is the tail area under the standard normal curve:

$$p = P(\vert Z \vert > 3) = 0.0027$$

The ARL is the reciprocal of this probability:

$$ARL_0 = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370.37 \approx 370$$

This means a *false alarm* (a point outside limits even when the process is in control) occurs, on average, every 370 samples.

### Step 3 — ARL for an Out-of-Control Process

If the process mean shifts by $\delta\sigma$ (i.e., the new mean is $\mu + \delta\sigma$), the probability of detection becomes:

$$p_1 = P(Z > 3 - \delta) \quad \text{(for an upward shift)}$$

The corresponding ARL is:

$$ARL_1 = \frac{1}{p_1} = \frac{1}{1 - \beta}$$

where $\beta$ is the **Type II Error** (failure to detect a real shift).

### Step 4 — Worked Example Computation

**Problem:** A process shifts by $1.5\sigma$ (i.e., $\delta = 1.5$). Calculate $ARL_1$.

**Step 4a:** Compute the new detection probability:

$$p_1 = P(Z > 3 - 1.5) = P(Z > 1.5)$$

**Step 4b:** Look up the standard normal table:

$$P(Z > 1.5) = 0.0668$$

**Step 4c:** Calculate the ARL:

$$ARL_1 = \frac{1}{0.0668} \approx 14.97 \approx 15 \text{ samples}$$

This means a $1.5\sigma$ shift will be detected, on average, within 15 sample points.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Patterns and Their Root Causes

The following table maps **observable patterns** on a control chart to **probable physical causes**, a high-yield framework for KTU descriptive questions.

| Pattern | Visual Signature | Probable Assignable Causes | Remedial Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Out-of-Limits Point (Rule 1)** | Single point outside UCL/LCL | Machine breakdown, measurement error, raw material defect, operator error | Stop production, investigate, recalibrate |
| **Trend (Rule 5)** | 6+ points consistently increasing or decreasing | Tool wear, gradual temperature rise, progressive material degradation, operator fatigue | Schedule maintenance, replace tool, reset machine |
| **Shift (Rule 4)** | 8+ points on one side of centerline | New operator, new lot of raw material, method change, permanent fixture shift | Update procedures, retrain, implement SOP |
| **Cycle** | Regular, repeating up-down pattern | Operator shifts, environmental cycles (temperature), systematic rotation | Review shift schedules, climate control |
| **Mixture (Rule 8)** | Most points outside ±1σ on both sides | Two distinct populations being measured together, over-control | Separate data streams, identify subgroups |
| **Stratification (Rule 6)** | Most points within ±1σ (lack of spread) | Over-control, incorrect control limits, data stratification | Recalculate control limits, review sampling |
| **Systematic Variation (Rule 7)** | Alternating high-low pattern | Alternating machines/operators, systematic rotation | Balance workload, randomize order |
| **Freaks / Spurious Points** | Isolated, non-repeating outliers | Measurement error, recording error, one-time events | Verify data, retrain inspector |

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification for Western Electric Rule 4 (Shift)

To detect a shift of size $k\sigma$ efficiently, we use the **run test**. The probability that a point falls *above* the centerline is $p = 0.5$ for a centered process.

For a shift of $k\sigma$, the probability becomes:

$$p_{shift} = P\left(X > \mu \vert \mu_{new} = \mu + k\sigma\right) = P\left(Z > -\frac{k\sigma}{\sigma}\right) = P(Z > -k)$$

For $k = 1.5$, $p_{shift} \approx 0.9332$. The probability of 8 successive points all being above the centerline is:

$$P(\text{8 above}) = (0.9332)^8 \approx 0.557$$

For Rule 2 (2 of 3 beyond 2σ), the probability of being beyond 2σ for a 1.5σ shift is:

$$p_{2\sigma} = P(Z > 2 - 1.5) = P(Z > 0.5) = 0.3085$$

$$\binom{3}{2}(0.3085)^2(0.6915)^1 \approx 0.197$$

This justifies Rule 2 as a sensitive detector of moderate shifts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Identifying Out-of-Control Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plotted Point on Control Chart] --> B{Point within UCL and LCL?}
    B -- No --> C[Rule 1 Violated: OUT OF CONTROL]
    C --> C1[Investigate Special Cause]
    B -- Yes --> D{2 of 3 points beyond 2 sigma zone?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Rule 2 Violated: OUT OF CONTROL]
    D -- No --> F{4 of 5 points beyond 1 sigma zone?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Rule 3 Violated: OUT OF CONTROL]
    F -- No --> H{8 points on one side of centerline?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Rule 4 Violated: SHIFT detected]
    I --> J[Investigate Process Change]
    H -- No --> K{6 points trending in one direction?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Rule 5 Violated: TREND detected]
    L --> M[Investigate Tool Wear or Drift]
    K -- No --> N{15 points within 1 sigma zone?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Rule 6 Violated: STRATIFICATION]
    O --> P[Recalculate Control Limits]
    N -- No --> Q{14 points alternating up-down?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Rule 7 Violated: SYSTEMATIC VARIATION]
    R --> S[Check for Rotational Patterns]
    Q -- No --> T[Process is IN STATISTICAL CONTROL]
    T --> U[Continue Monitoring]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Pattern Detection in SPC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Data Acquisition Layer]
        node1[Process Sensors]
        node2[Manual Measurement]
        node3[Automated Gauges]
    end
    subgraph ComputeLayer[Statistical Computation Engine]
        node4[Mean Calculator]
        node5[Standard Deviation Module]
        node6[Range Calculator]
    end
    subgraph ControlLayer[Control Limit Engine]
        node7[UCL Generator]
        node8[LCL Generator]
        node9[Centerline Generator]
    end
    subgraph PatternLayer[Pattern Recognition Module]
        node10[Zone Detector]
        node11[Run Rules Engine]
        node12[Western Electric Module]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Decision and Action]
        node13[In Control Indicator]
        node14[Out of Control Alarm]
        node15[Root Cause Trigger]
    end
    node1 --> node4
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node10
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13
    node12 --> node14
    node12 --> node15
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Visual Pattern Map — Seven Common Control Chart Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PatternLibrary[Control Chart Pattern Library]
        pat1[Pattern 1: Normal Random Scatter]
        pat2[Pattern 2: Trend Six Consecutive Points]
        pat3[Pattern 3: Shift Eight Points on One Side]
        pat4[Pattern 4: Cycle Regular Oscillation]
        pat5[Pattern 5: Mixture Two Overlapping Populations]
        pat6[Pattern 6: Stratification Banded Within Sigma]
        pat7[Pattern 7: Systematic Alternating High Low]
    end
    pat1 --> root1[Cause: Common Cause Variation Only]
    pat2 --> root2[Cause: Tool Wear or Drift]
    pat3 --> root3[Cause: Process Step Change]
    pat4 --> root4[Cause: Environmental Cyclicity]
    pat5 --> root5[Cause: Data Pooling Error]
    pat6 --> root6[Cause: Over Control]
    pat7 --> root7[Cause: Systematic Rotation]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual State Transition Diagram — Process State Evolution

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stable: Process Commissioned
    Stable --> OutOfControl: Special Cause Introduced
    OutOfControl --> Stable: Cause Identified and Removed
    OutOfControl --> Investigate: Pattern Detected by Rules
    Investigate --> Stable: Root Cause Eliminated
    Stable --> Capable: Capability Confirmed
    Capable --> Stable: Continuous Monitoring
    Stable --> Incapable: Capability Below Threshold
    Incapable --> Redesign: System Level Improvement
    Redesign --> Stable: Process Recommissioned
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the State of Statistical Control. Differentiate between common cause and special cause variation.**

**Model Answer:**

A process is said to be in a **State of Statistical Control** when its output variation is solely due to **chance/common causes** and the plotted points on the control chart fall within the $\pm 3\sigma$ control limits with no non-random patterns.

**Difference Table (Mandatory for KTU Valuation):**

| Parameter | Common Cause | Special Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Inherent, random | External, identifiable |
| Predictability | Predictable statistically | Unpredictable |
| Action | Requires system change | Investigate and remove |
| Examples | Tool wear (baseline) | Machine breakdown |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Tip:** A bare 2-line answer will fetch only 1.5 marks. KTU examiners expect a *definition* (1 mark) + a *structured comparison* (1.5 marks) + at least one example of each (0.5 marks).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain any FOUR Western Electric Rules for detecting out-of-control conditions on a control chart.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Rule 1:** One point beyond the 3σ control limit — indicates an immediate special cause.
2. **Rule 2:** 2 of 3 successive points beyond the 2σ warning limit (same side) — suggests a developing shift.
3. **Rule 4:** 8 successive points on one side of the centerline — indicates a permanent process shift.
4. **Rule 5:** 6 successive points trending in one direction — indicates a trend (e.g., tool wear).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern]

**a)** Explain the concept of "State of Statistical Control" in detail. Discuss the role of common cause and special cause variation with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A process is in statistical control when it operates under only common cause variation, with all output points falling within $\pm 3\sigma$ control limits and exhibiting random behavior.

**Common Cause Variation (2 marks):**
- Also called chance, random, or inherent variation
- Represents the natural noise of the process
- Cannot be eliminated without changing the fundamental process design
- Example: Slight variations in bolt diameter due to microscopic tool chatter

**Special Cause Variation (2 marks):**
- Also called assignable cause variation
- External, identifiable, removable
- Example: Sudden machine breakdown causing out-of-spec parts

**Implication (1 mark):** An in-control process is stable but may not be capable. Stability must precede capability studies.

---

**b)** With neat sketches, describe SEVEN common patterns that can be observed on a control chart. For each pattern, state a likely assignable cause. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Pattern | Description (1 mark each × 4 = 4 marks) | Assignable Cause (0.5 mark each × 6 = 3 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Normal Scatter | Random points within limits | Common cause only |
| 2. Trend | 6+ points increasing/decreasing | Tool wear, temperature drift |
| 3. Shift | 8+ points on one side of CL | New operator, new material lot |
| 4. Cycle | Repeating wave-like pattern | Shift rotation, environmental cycle |
| 5. Mixture | Points hugging the limits | Two populations mixed |
| 6. Stratification | Points within ±1σ only | Over-control, wrong subgrouping |
| 7. Systematic Variation | Alternating high-low | Rotational machine/operator cycle |

*(Sketches should be drawn on a coordinate plane showing the centerline and control limits, with characteristic point distribution for each pattern.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Internal Choice Alternative]

**a)** Define the following terms with respect to control charts: (i) Control Limits, (ii) Specification Limits, (iii) Zone A, Zone B, Zone C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Control Limits (2 marks):** Statistical boundaries set at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (i.e., UCL = $\mu + 3\sigma$ and LCL = $\mu - 3\sigma$). They represent the "voice of the process" and are derived from the data itself. They are NOT customer specifications.

**(ii) Specification Limits (2 marks):** Engineering tolerances set by design or customer requirement (USL and LSL). They represent the "voice of the customer" and define acceptable product performance. A product within specification limits is acceptable to the customer.

**(iii) Zone A, Zone B, Zone C (3 marks — 1 each):**
- **Zone A:** The region between $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ and $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (outer zones). Western Electric Rule 2 uses this zone.
- **Zone B:** The region between $\mu \pm 1\sigma$ and $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ (middle zones). Western Electric Rule 3 uses this zone.
- **Zone C:** The region between $\mu$ and $\mu \pm 1\sigma$ (inner zones). Western Electric Rule 6 uses this zone.

---

**b)** A process has a mean of 50 grams and standard deviation of 2 grams. The sample size is n = 5. Calculate the control limits for the X-bar and R-charts, given that for n = 5, A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114, and d₂ = 2.326. The average range is 4.6 grams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Process mean: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50$ g
- Standard deviation: $\sigma = 2$ g
- Sample size: $n = 5$
- Average range: $\bar{R} = 4.6$ g
- Constants: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$

**X-bar Chart (3 marks):**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50 + (0.577)(4.6)$$

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 50 + 2.6542 = 52.6542 \text{ g}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 50 - (0.577)(4.6)$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 50 - 2.6542 = 47.3458 \text{ g}$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 50 \text{ g}$$

**R-Chart (3 marks):**

$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(4.6) = 9.7244 \text{ g}$$

$$LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(4.6) = 0 \text{ g}$$

$$CL_R = \bar{R} = 4.6 \text{ g}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer (1 mark):**

| Chart | UCL | CL | LCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| X-bar | 52.65 g | 50.00 g | 47.35 g |
| R | 9.72 g | 4.60 g | 0.00 g |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** If the problem gives data in grams, *every* limit must be in grams. (0.5 mark deduction per inconsistency)
> 2. **Wrong Constants:** Using $A_2 = 0.577$ for $n = 5$ is correct; mixing with $n = 4$ constants is a common error. Always verify the subgroup size $n$ before table lookup.
> 3. **LCL for R-Chart:** Many students forget that $D_3$ is often zero. The R-chart can have a zero LCL — this is **mathematically valid**, not an error.
> 4. **Skipping the Centerline:** Every chart must explicitly state CL.
> 5. **Rounding:** KTU expects answers to 2–4 decimal places; do not truncate to whole numbers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Month KTU Exam Revision — High-Density Summary**

- [x] **In-Control** = Only common cause variation + points within $\pm 3\sigma$ limits + no non-random patterns.
- [x] **Out-of-Control** = Special cause variation present, detected by Western Electric or Nelson Rules.
- [x] **$3\sigma$ Limits** = ~99.73% coverage = 0.27% Type I error = ARL₀ ≈ 370 points.
- [x] **Control Limits ≠ Specification Limits** — Voice of Process vs Voice of Customer.
- [x] **Common patterns to recognize (7 types):** Trend, Shift, Cycle, Mixture, Stratification, Systematic Variation, Freaks.
- [x] **Western Electric Rule 1:** 1 point beyond 3σ — most basic rule.
- [x] **Western Electric Rule 2:** 2 of 3 points beyond 2σ — early shift detector.
- [x] **Western Electric Rule 3:** 4 of 5 points beyond 1σ — moderate shift detector.
- [x] **Western Electric Rule 4:** 8 points on one side — shift indicator.
- [x] **Western Electric Rule 5:** 6 points trending — trend (tool wear).
- [x] **Western Electric Rule 6:** 15 points within 1σ — stratification/over-control.
- [x] **Western Electric Rule 7:** 14 points alternating — systematic variation.
- [x] **X-bar Chart Formula:** $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$.
- [x] **R-Chart Formula:** $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$, $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$.
- [x] **Stability ≠ Capability:** A process can be stable but incapable (and vice versa).
- [x] **ARL Formula:** $ARL_0 = 1/\alpha = 1/0.0027 \approx 370$ for in-control; $ARL_1 = 1/(1-\beta)$ for out-of-control.
- [x] **Stabilize before improving:** Always achieve statistical control first, then pursue capability improvements (Deming's principle).
- [x] **Zone Definitions:** Zone A = ±2σ to ±3σ; Zone B = ±1σ to ±2σ; Zone C = 0 to ±1σ.
- [x] **2 of 3 beyond 2σ** is a stronger signal than **4 of 5 beyond 1σ** in terms of statistical confidence for a 1.5σ shift.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Pattern Detection in Control Charts**
