topics.title: Other Control Charts: Cumulative Sum Control Chart for Process Mean and Exponentially Weighted Moving Average Control Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Other Control Charts: Cumulative Sum Control Chart for Process Mean and Exponentially Weighted Moving Average Control Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) Control Chart

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> A **Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) Control Chart** is a time-weighted control chart that plots the cumulative sum of deviations of sample values from a target value, designed to detect small, sustained shifts in the process mean (typically 0.5σ to 2.5σ). It is superior to the Shewhart $\bar{X}$ chart for detecting small mean shifts because it *accumulates* information across multiple subgroups.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Snowball" Effect

Imagine you are walking a long, straight corridor carrying a **snowball**. With every step:
- If you walk perfectly straight (no shift), the snowball neither grows nor shrinks — it stays the same.
- If you drift *slightly* to the left or right, even by an imperceptible amount, **the snowball rolls into the snow and grows**. Over time, this tiny drift produces a giant snowball that cannot be ignored.

The **CUSUM chart works exactly the same way**. A Shewhart chart looks at *one* subgroup at a time and "forgets" the past. The CUSUM chart **remembers every subgroup** and sums up the small drifts. A shift of even 0.5σ, invisible on a Shewhart chart, becomes obvious on a CUSUM chart because the cumulative deviation grows steadily.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Inventor:** The CUSUM chart was popularized by **E.S. Page (1954)** at the University of Manchester. It is the standard for detecting shifts between **0.5σ and 2.5σ**, which are the hardest to detect and the most economically damaging in modern manufacturing.

## 1.2 Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) Control Chart

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> An **Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) Control Chart** is a time-weighted control chart that plots a weighted average of all past and current sample observations, where the weight assigned to past observations *decreases exponentially* with their age. It is highly effective in detecting small-to-moderate process mean shifts (0.5σ to 2σ) and is mathematically equivalent to a CUSUM chart for certain parameter choices.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Memory Decay" Analogy

Think of the EWMA chart as a **person with fading memory**:
- Yesterday's event is vivid (high weight $\lambda$).
- Last week's event is fuzzy (medium weight).
- Last year's event is barely a whisper (very tiny weight, but **never zero**).

Every new data point is mixed with this fading memory to form a new weighted average called the **EWMA statistic** ($Z_i$). When the process suddenly shifts, the new measurements carry more weight, and $Z_i$ gradually drifts toward the new mean, signaling a problem long before a Shewhart chart would.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The EWMA chart was first proposed by **Roberts (1959)** in his seminal AT&T Bell Labs technical report. It is statistically optimal (minimum mean square error) for tracking the mean of a process with **autocorrelated** or **slowly drifting** characteristics.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in CUSUM and EWMA design:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Range (KTU) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Target Mean | $\mu_0$ | — | Reference value (in-control center) |
| Process Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ | — | Inherent variability |
| Allowable Slack/Reference Value (CUSUM) | $K$ or $k$ | 0.25σ to 1.0σ | Half the shift to detect |
| Decision Interval (CUSUM) | $H$ or $h$ | 4σ to 5σ | Threshold for action |
| Smoothing Constant (EWMA) | $\lambda$ | 0.05 ≤ λ ≤ 0.30 (typical) | Weight on most recent sample |
| Control Limit Multiplier (EWMA) | $L$ | 2.614 to 3.054 | 3-sigma equivalent factor |
| Average Run Length | ARL | — | Expected number of points before out-of-control signal |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) — Operational Logic

The CUSUM chart works in **two parallel cumulative chains** — one for detecting *increases* and one for detecting *decreases* in the process mean.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Take a subgroup** of size $n$ and compute the sample mean $\bar{X}_i$.
2. **Standardize** the subgroup mean: $y_i = \bar{X}_i - \mu_0$ (deviation from target).
3. **Define the Reference Value** $K$ (also called the *allowable slack* or *slip* value). The standard choice is:
$$K = \frac{\delta}{2}\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$
   where $\delta$ is the smallest shift (in units of $\sigma$) we wish to detect quickly.
4. **Compute the one-sided upper CUSUM** (detects upward shift):
$$C_i^+ = \max\left[0,\ \bar{X}_i - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{i-1}^+\right]$$
5. **Compute the one-sided lower CUSUM** (detects downward shift):
$$C_i^- = \max\left[0,\ (\mu_0 - K) - \bar{X}_i + C_{i-1}^-\right]$$
6. **Decision Rule:** The process is *out-of-control* if either $C_i^+ > H$ or $C_i^- > H$.

### Why Two CUSUMs?

A single CUSUM only detects shifts in *one* direction. The pair $C_i^+$ and $C_i^-$ simultaneously monitors both directions — a scheme called the **Two-Sided Tabular CUSUM**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Initial Conditions:** The recursion starts at $C_0^+ = C_0^- = 0$. The minimum value of any CUSUM is reset to zero when the accumulated sum becomes negative (the $\max[0, \cdot]$ function). This is called the **"non-negative reset"** and it makes the chart insensitive to past out-of-control conditions that have been corrected.

### The V-Mask Procedure (Graphical CUSUM)

An alternative, older graphical method uses a **V-shaped mask** placed over the plotted cumulative sums:

- The vertex of the V is placed on the most recent cumulative sum.
- The two arms of the V are extended backward at angles $\pm\theta$ from the horizontal.
- If any **previously plotted** cumulative sum falls *outside* (above the upper arm or below the lower arm), the process is signaled out-of-control.

The angle $\theta$ is related to the decision interval $H$ and the lead distance $d$:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{H}{2d}$$

A more practical form using standardized parameters:

$$d = \frac{2H}{\delta^2}, \quad \tan(\theta) = \frac{\delta}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **V-mask** is rarely used in modern industry because the **tabular CUSUM** is easier to implement in software. However, KTU examiners often ask it because of its historical importance and visual intuition.

## 2.2 Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) — Operational Logic

The EWMA chart maintains a single statistic $Z_i$ that smoothly averages past data with exponentially decaying weights.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Initialize** the EWMA statistic with the target mean:
$$Z_0 = \mu_0$$
2. **Compute the new EWMA** for subgroup $i$ using the smoothing constant $\lambda$ ($0 < \lambda \le 1$):
$$Z_i = \lambda \bar{X}_i + (1 - \lambda) Z_{i-1}$$
3. **Compute the time-varying control limits:**
$$UCL_i = \mu_0 + L \cdot \sigma \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{n(2-\lambda)}\left[1 - (1-\lambda)^{2i}\right]}$$
$$LCL_i = \mu_0 - L \cdot \sigma \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{n(2-\lambda)}\left[1 - (1-\lambda)^{2i}\right]}$$
4. **Decision Rule:** If $Z_i$ falls outside $[LCL_i, UCL_i]$, the process is out-of-control.

### Why the Asymptote Matters:

As $i \to \infty$, the term $(1-\lambda)^{2i} \to 0$, and the limits stabilize at the **steady-state limits**:

$$UCL_\infty = \mu_0 + L \cdot \sigma \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{n(2-\lambda)}}$$

$$LCL_\infty = \mu_0 - L \cdot \sigma \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{n(2-\lambda)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Insight:** The factor $\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}}$ is the **standard deviation of the EWMA statistic** at steady state, scaled by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. A larger $\lambda$ gives *faster* detection of large shifts; a smaller $\lambda$ gives *slower but steadier* detection of tiny shifts. **There is no single "best" $\lambda$** — it must be tuned to the engineering application.

## 2.3 Average Run Length (ARL) — The Common Performance Metric

The **Average Run Length (ARL)** is the average number of subgroups plotted before a chart signals out-of-control.

- **$ARL_0$** = ARL when the process is in control (should be LARGE, typically 200–500).
- **$ARL_1$** = ARL when the process is out of control by a specific shift (should be SMALL, typically 3–10).

The relationship between in-control and out-of-control ARL is:

$$ARL_0 = \frac{1}{P(\text{false alarm})}$$

$$ARL_1 = \frac{1}{P(\text{detection on a single point})}$$

| Chart Type | $ARL_0$ | Detects 1σ Shift ($ARL_1$) | Detects 2σ Shift ($ARL_1$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shewhart $\bar{X}$ | 370.4 | 43.9 | 6.3 |
| CUSUM ($K=0.5\sigma$, $H=5\sigma$) | ~340 | ~10.4 | ~3.0 |
| EWMA ($\lambda=0.2$, $L=3$) | ~500 | ~11.4 | ~3.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CUSUM and EWMA both have $ARL_0 \approx 500$ for equivalent parameter choices**, matching a **4.5σ** Shewhart chart. This means they offer the *same low false-alarm rate* but *dramatically faster detection of small shifts*.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tabular Upper CUSUM | $C_i^+ = \max[0, \bar{X}_i - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{i-1}^+]$ | Detects upward shift |
| 2 | Tabular Lower CUSUM | $C_i^- = \max[0, (\mu_0 - K) - \bar{X}_i + C_{i-1}^-]$ | Detects downward shift |
| 3 | CUSUM Reference Value | $K = \dfrac{\delta \sigma}{2\sqrt{n}}$ (or $\dfrac{\delta\sigma}{2}$ for $n=1$) | $\delta$ = shift to detect in $\sigma$ units |
| 4 | Standardized CUSUM | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} (Z_j - k\sigma_Z)$ | Uses standardized residuals $Z_j = (\bar{X}_j - \mu_0)/\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ |
| 5 | V-Mask Lead Distance | $d = \dfrac{2H}{\delta^2}$ (standardized units) | Distance from latest point to V-vertex |
| 6 | V-Mask Angle | $\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{\delta}{2}$ | $\delta$ in sigma units |
| 7 | EWMA Statistic | $Z_i = \lambda \bar{X}_i + (1-\lambda) Z_{i-1}$ | $0 < \lambda \le 1$, $Z_0 = \mu_0$ |
| 8 | EWMA Variance Factor | $\mathrm{Var}(Z_i) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}\left[1 - (1-\lambda)^{2i}\right]$ | Time-varying |
| 9 | EWMA Steady-State UCL | $UCL = \mu_0 + L\sigma\sqrt{\dfrac{\lambda}{n(2-\lambda)}}$ | As $i \to \infty$ |
| 10 | Average Run Length | $ARL = \dfrac{1}{p}$ | $p$ = probability of signal at a given point |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** CUSUM charts are used at Intel and TSMC to detect drift in chip etching thickness (shifts as small as 5 nm in a 50 nm process).
- **Pharmaceutical Vial Filling:** EWMA monitors fill volumes — the gradual nature of EWMA matches the gradual drift of pump wear.
- **Automotive Paint Thickness:** Both charts detect slow degradation in spray nozzle performance.
- **Chemical Process Industries (KTU Kerala Context):** Kerala's industrial belt (Cochin, Palakkad, Aluva) uses CUSUM/EWMA in spice-processing and cashew-export QC to monitor moisture and oil content.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tabular CUSUM Recurrence

The **CUSUM statistic** $C_i$ is defined as the cumulative sum of deviations from a target value, but with a **one-sided hypothesis test** interpretation.

### Derivation Walkthrough:

**Step 1:** Define the one-sided likelihood ratio test. We want to test the null hypothesis $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ against the alternative $H_1: \mu = \mu_0 + \delta$ (for detecting upward shifts).

**Step 2:** For a normal sample with known variance, the log-likelihood ratio after $i$ samples is:

$$L_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i}\left[\frac{(\bar{X}_j - \mu_0)\delta}{\sigma^2/n} - \frac{\delta^2}{2\sigma^2/n}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Factor out constants and substitute $K = \delta\sigma/(2\sqrt{n})$:

$$L_i = \frac{2\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\sum_{j=1}^{i}(\bar{X}_j - \mu_0) - \frac{n\delta^2}{\sigma^2}\cdot i$$

**Step 4:** Divide by the constant $2\sqrt{n}/\sigma$ to obtain the **standardized CUSUM statistic** $S_i$:

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i}(\bar{X}_j - \mu_0) - K \cdot i = \sum_{j=1}^{i}(\bar{X}_j - \mu_0 - K)$$

**Step 5:** Convert to the recursive (memory-efficient) form:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_i &= S_{i-1} + (\bar{X}_i - \mu_0 - K) \\
S_i &= \max\left[0,\ S_{i-1} + \bar{X}_i - (\mu_0 + K)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** This recursive form is precisely the tabular upper CUSUM:

$$C_i^+ = \max\left[0,\ \bar{X}_i - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{i-1}^+\right]$$

**The negative reset (clamping at zero)** comes from the **sequential probability ratio test (SPRT)**: when evidence against $H_0$ is weaker than evidence for $H_0$, the test statistic is reset to zero, effectively "starting over."

## 3.2 Derivation 2: EWMA Variance

The **EWMA statistic** $Z_i$ is a weighted sum of all past observations. We compute its variance to find the control limits.

### Derivation Walkthrough:

**Step 1:** Unroll the recursion explicitly:

$$Z_i = \lambda \bar{X}_i + (1-\lambda)\lambda \bar{X}_{i-1} + (1-\lambda)^2\lambda \bar{X}_{i-2} + \cdots + (1-\lambda)^i \mu_0$$

**Step 2:** The weight on $\bar{X}_{i-k}$ is $w_k = \lambda(1-\lambda)^k$. Verify weights sum to 1:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{i} w_k = \lambda \sum_{k=0}^{i}(1-\lambda)^k = 1 - (1-\lambda)^{i+1}$$

**Step 3:** Since all $\bar{X}_j$ are independent with variance $\sigma^2/n$, the variance of $Z_i$ is:

$$\mathrm{Var}(Z_i) = \sum_{k=0}^{i} w_k^2 \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the sum of squared weights:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{i} w_k^2 = \lambda^2 \sum_{k=0}^{i}(1-\lambda)^{2k} = \frac{\lambda\left[1 - (1-\lambda)^{2(i+1)}\right]}{2-\lambda}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the variance expression:

$$\mathrm{Var}(Z_i) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}\left[1 - (1-\lambda)^{2(i+1)}\right]$$

**Step 6:** For large $i$ (steady state), $(1-\lambda)^{2(i+1)} \to 0$, giving:

$$\mathrm{Var}(Z_\infty) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}$$

The **standard deviation** used in the control limits is:

$$\sigma_{Z_i} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}\left[1 - (1-\lambda)^{2i}\right]}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: CUSUM Chart

**Problem:** A process has $\mu_0 = 100$, $\sigma = 5$, and subgroup size $n = 4$. We want to detect a shift of $\delta = 1\sigma$. Design the CUSUM chart with $K = 0.5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ and $H = 5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n} = 5/2 = 2.5$.

**Step 2:** Set $K = 0.5 \times 2.5 = 1.25$. Set $H = 5 \times 2.5 = 12.5$.

**Step 3:** Given 10 subgroup means: 100.2, 99.8, 101.5, 102.3, 101.8, 102.9, 103.4, 103.1, 102.7, 103.6.

**Step 4:** Compute the upper CUSUM using the table format:

| $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $\bar{X}_i - (\mu_0 + K)$ | $C_{i-1}^+$ | $C_i^+ = \max[0, \cdot]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100.2 | 100.2 − 101.25 = −1.05 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 99.8 | 99.8 − 101.25 = −1.45 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 101.5 | 101.5 − 101.25 = 0.25 | 0 | 0.25 |
| 4 | 102.3 | 102.3 − 101.25 = 1.05 | 0.25 | 1.30 |
| 5 | 101.8 | 101.8 − 101.25 = 0.55 | 1.30 | 1.85 |
| 6 | 102.9 | 102.9 − 101.25 = 1.65 | 1.85 | 3.50 |
| 7 | 103.4 | 103.4 − 101.25 = 2.15 | 3.50 | 5.65 |
| 8 | 103.1 | 103.1 − 101.25 = 1.85 | 5.65 | 7.50 |
| 9 | 102.7 | 102.7 − 101.25 = 1.45 | 7.50 | 8.95 |
| 10 | 103.6 | 103.6 − 101.25 = 2.35 | 8.95 | **11.30** |

**Step 5:** At $i = 10$, $C_{10}^+ = 11.30 < 12.5$ — the process is **still in control** under this decision interval, but it is *trending toward an out-of-control signal*. A few more subgroups at this shifted level would trigger the alarm.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: EWMA Chart

**Problem:** A process has $\mu_0 = 50$, $\sigma = 2$, $n = 5$, $\lambda = 0.2$, $L = 3$. Compute the first 5 EWMA values and the control limits for a dataset with sample means: 50.1, 50.3, 50.2, 50.5, 50.4.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 2/\sqrt{5} = 0.8944$.

**Step 2:** Initialize $Z_0 = 50.0$.

**Step 3:** Compute the steady-state standard deviation of $Z$:

$$\sigma_Z = \sigma_{\bar{X}} \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}} = 0.8944 \sqrt{\frac{0.2}{1.8}} = 0.8944 \times 0.3333 = 0.2981$$

**Step 4:** Steady-state limits:
$$UCL = 50 + 3 \times 0.2981 = 50.894$$
$$LCL = 50 - 3 \times 0.2981 = 49.106$$

**Step 5:** Recursion:

| $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $Z_i = 0.2\bar{X}_i + 0.8 Z_{i-1}$ | $UCL_i$ (time-varying) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50.1 | $0.2(50.1) + 0.8(50.0) = 50.02$ | 50.288 |
| 2 | 50.3 | $0.2(50.3) + 0.8(50.02) = 50.076$ | 50.532 |
| 3 | 50.2 | $0.2(50.2) + 0.8(50.076) = 50.101$ | 50.683 |
| 4 | 50.5 | $0.2(50.5) + 0.8(50.101) = 50.181$ | 50.778 |
| 5 | 50.4 | $0.2(50.4) + 0.8(50.181) = 50.225$ | 50.837 |

All $Z_i$ lie within their respective time-varying limits — process is **in control**.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: CUSUM vs. EWMA

| Criterion | CUSUM | EWMA |
|---|---|---|
| Optimal shift range | 0.5σ to 2.5σ | 0.5σ to 2.0σ |
| Design parameters | $K$ and $H$ | $\lambda$ and $L$ |
| Computational complexity | Two parallel recursions | Single recursion |
| Visual interpretation | Two-sided plot of $C^+, C^-$ | Single statistic with banded limits |
| Sensitivity tuning | Choice of $K$ (shift to detect) | Choice of $\lambda$ (memory span) |
| Steady-state behavior | Independent of $i$ (clamped) | Time-varying limits that asymptote |
| Best for | Detecting *known* shift size | Detecting *uncertain* shift size |
| Industry preference | Pharmaceutical, steel | Semiconductor, chemical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CUSUM Detection Mechanism — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Take Subgroup i"] --> B["Compute Sample Mean X-bar-i"]
    B --> C["Compute Deviation from Target Mu-0"]
    C --> D["Upper Branch: X-bar-i minus Mu-0 plus K"]
    C --> E["Lower Branch: Mu-0 minus K minus X-bar-i"]
    D --> F["Add Previous C-plus minus 1"]
    E --> G["Add Previous C-minus minus 1"]
    F --> H["Apply Non-Negative Reset max 0"]
    G --> I["Apply Non-Negative Reset max 0"]
    H --> J["Check C-plus against H"]
    I --> K["Check C-minus against H"]
    J --> L["C-plus greater than H equals ALARM UP"]
    K --> M["C-minus greater than H equals ALARM DOWN"]
    L --> N["Process Out of Control"]
    M --> N
    H --> O["C-plus less than H equals Continue"]
    I --> P["C-minus less than H equals Continue"]
    O --> Q["Plot and Wait for Next Subgroup"]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 EWMA Smoothing Process — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initialize Z-0 equals Mu-0"] --> B["New Subgroup arrives with X-bar-i"]
    B --> C["Compute Z-i equals Lambda X-bar-i plus 1 minus Lambda Z-minus-1"]
    C --> D["Compute Time Varying Standard Deviation"]
    D --> E["Compute UCL-i equals Mu-0 plus L sigma Z-i"]
    D --> F["Compute LCL-i equals Mu-0 minus L sigma Z-i"]
    E --> G["Check if Z-i exceeds UCL-i"]
    F --> H["Check if Z-i falls below LCL-i"]
    G --> I["ALARM Upper Out of Control"]
    H --> J["ALARM Lower Out of Control"]
    I --> K["Process Out of Control"]
    J --> K
    G --> L["Z-i within limits equals In Control"]
    H --> L
    L --> M["Store Z-i for Next Iteration"]
    M --> B
```

## 4.3 ARL Performance Comparison — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHE["Shewhart X-bar Chart"]
        S1["3-sigma Limits"]
        S2["ARL-0 equals 370"]
        S3["Slow for shifts less than 1.5 sigma"]
    end
    subgraph CUS["CUSUM Chart"]
        C1["K plus H design"]
        C2["ARL-0 equals 340 to 500"]
        C3["Fast for 0.5 to 2.5 sigma shifts"]
    end
    subgraph EWM["EWMA Chart"]
        E1["Lambda plus L design"]
        E2["ARL-0 equals 500"]
        E3["Fast for 0.5 to 2.0 sigma shifts"]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    E1 --> E2 --> E3
```

## 4.4 Decision-Making Workflow — When to Use Which Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["New Quality Monitoring Task"] --> B{"What is the smallest shift of practical interest?"}
    B -->|"Larger than 2.5 sigma"| C["Use Shewhart X-bar Chart"]
    B -->|"Between 0.5 and 2.5 sigma"| D{"Is the shift size known in advance?"}
    B -->|"Less than 0.5 sigma"| E["Use Process Capability Indices Cp Cpk"]
    D -->|"Yes known shift size"| F["Use CUSUM with K equals delta over 2"]
    D -->|"No uncertain shift size"| G["Use EWMA with lambda equals 0.2 typical"]
    F --> H["Detect specific shift efficiently"]
    G --> I["Smooth detection across all shift sizes"]
    H --> J["Production Process Monitoring"]
    I --> J
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Q:** Define the Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) control chart. State the typical range of process mean shifts for which CUSUM is preferred over the Shewhart $\bar{X}$ chart.

**Model Answer:**
The CUSUM control chart is a time-weighted control procedure that plots the cumulative sum of deviations of sample means from a target value, accumulating small drifts across subgroups. It is preferred over the Shewhart $\bar{X}$ chart for detecting small-to-moderate process mean shifts in the range of **0.5σ to 2.5σ**, where the Shewhart chart has poor sensitivity. The CUSUM chart uses two parallel cumulative sums ($C_i^+$ and $C_i^-$) with a reference value $K$ and decision interval $H$ to detect upward and downward shifts respectively.

### Question 2: Distinction
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Q:** Differentiate between a CUSUM chart and an EWMA chart. Mention the key design parameters for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CUSUM Chart | EWMA Chart |
|---|---|---|
| Plotting Statistic | Cumulative sums $C_i^+, C_i^-$ | Smoothed average $Z_i$ |
| Design Parameters | Reference value $K$, Decision interval $H$ | Smoothing constant $\lambda$, Limit factor $L$ |
| Optimal Range | 0.5σ to 2.5σ shifts | 0.5σ to 2.0σ shifts |
| Number of Curves | Two (one-sided each direction) | One (with time-varying limits) |
| Reset Mechanism | Non-negative clamping at zero | Continuous exponential decay |
| Best Application | Known shift size detection | Unknown/varying shift size detection |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — CUSUM Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Q (a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the working of the **Tabular CUSUM** procedure for monitoring the process mean. Clearly state the decision rule.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] The diameter of pistons manufactured in a process has mean $\mu_0 = 50$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.8$ mm. Subgroups of size $n = 4$ are taken every hour. Design a CUSUM chart to detect a shift of $\delta = 1\sigma$ in the process mean. Use $K = 0.5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ and $H = 5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$. The last 8 subgroup means (in mm) are: 50.10, 50.30, 50.45, 50.60, 50.80, 51.10, 50.95, 51.30. Plot the CUSUM statistic and check whether the process is in control.

**Model Solution (a):**

The Tabular CUSUM uses two parallel cumulative chains, initialized at zero:

**Upper CUSUM (detects upward shift):**
$$C_i^+ = \max[0,\ \bar{X}_i - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{i-1}^+]$$

**Lower CUSUM (detects downward shift):**
$$C_i^- = \max[0,\ (\mu_0 - K) - \bar{X}_i + C_{i-1}^-]$$

**Decision Rule:** The process is signaled *out of control* if either $C_i^+ > H$ or $C_i^- > H$. The non-negative reset ($\max[0, \cdot]$) prevents the chart from being misled by past large shifts that have been corrected.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 0.8/\sqrt{4} = 0.4$ mm.

**Step 2:** Set parameters:
$$K = 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.20 \text{ mm}, \quad H = 5 \times 0.4 = 2.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Upper CUSUM (only the upward direction is needed, since data is trending upward):

| $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $\bar{X}_i - (50 + 0.2) = \bar{X}_i - 50.2$ | $C_{i-1}^+$ | $C_i^+$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50.10 | −0.10 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 50.30 | +0.10 | 0 | 0.10 |
| 3 | 50.45 | +0.25 | 0.10 | 0.35 |
| 4 | 50.60 | +0.40 | 0.35 | 0.75 |
| 5 | 50.80 | +0.60 | 0.75 | 1.35 |
| 6 | 51.10 | +0.90 | 1.35 | 2.25 |
| 7 | 50.95 | +0.75 | 2.25 | **3.00** |
| 8 | 51.30 | +1.10 | 3.00 | **4.10** |

**Step 4:** At $i = 7$, $C_7^+ = 3.00 > H = 2.00$. **ALARM TRIGGERED.** The process is **OUT OF CONTROL** with an upward shift detected starting from subgroup 7.

### Question B (14 Marks) — EWMA Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the construction and working of an **EWMA control chart** for monitoring the process mean. Define the smoothing constant $\lambda$ and the limit factor $L$. Discuss the effect of $\lambda$ on the chart's performance.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] The viscosity of a chemical product has target mean $\mu_0 = 100$ cP, standard deviation $\sigma = 4$ cP, and subgroup size $n = 5$. Design an EWMA chart with $\lambda = 0.3$ and $L = 3$. Compute the steady-state control limits and the first 4 EWMA values for the following subgroup means: 100.5, 101.2, 100.8, 102.0.

**Model Solution (a):**

The **EWMA chart** plots the exponentially weighted moving average statistic:
$$Z_i = \lambda \bar{X}_i + (1-\lambda) Z_{i-1}, \quad Z_0 = \mu_0$$

**Smoothing constant $\lambda$** ($0 < \lambda \le 1$) is the weight assigned to the most recent sample mean. A smaller $\lambda$ gives more weight to past history (slower response, better for small shifts); a larger $\lambda$ gives more weight to the current sample (faster response, better for large shifts).

**Limit factor $L$** (typically 3) is the multiplier that defines the control limits as a multiple of the standard deviation of $Z_i$. The time-varying limits are:

$$UCL_i = \mu_0 + L\sigma\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{n(2-\lambda)}\left[1-(1-\lambda)^{2i}\right]}$$

$$LCL_i = \mu_0 - L\sigma\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{n(2-\lambda)}\left[1-(1-\lambda)^{2i}\right]}$$

**Effect of $\lambda$ on performance:** Smaller $\lambda$ (e.g., 0.05) is excellent for detecting very small shifts (0.5σ) but slow for large ones. Larger $\lambda$ (e.g., 0.5) detects large shifts quickly but behaves like a Shewhart chart for small shifts. The "best" $\lambda$ is typically in the range **0.1 to 0.3** for general-purpose use.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 4/\sqrt{5} = 1.7889$ cP.

**Step 2:** Compute steady-state standard deviation of $Z$:

$$\sigma_Z = \sigma_{\bar{X}}\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}} = 1.7889 \sqrt{\frac{0.3}{1.7}} = 1.7889 \times 0.4201 = 0.7514 \text{ cP}$$

**Step 3:** Compute steady-state limits:

$$UCL = 100 + 3 \times 0.7514 = 102.25 \text{ cP}$$
$$LCL = 100 - 3 \times 0.7514 = 97.75 \text{ cP}$$

**Step 4:** Recursion (start with $Z_0 = 100$):

| $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $Z_i = 0.3 \bar{X}_i + 0.7 Z_{i-1}$ | $UCL_i$ (time-varying) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100.5 | $0.3(100.5) + 0.7(100) = 100.15$ | 100.50 | In Control |
| 2 | 101.2 | $0.3(101.2) + 0.7(100.15) = 100.465$ | 100.97 | In Control |
| 3 | 100.8 | $0.3(100.8) + 0.7(100.465) = 100.566$ | 101.31 | In Control |
| 4 | 102.0 | $0.3(102.0) + 0.7(100.566) = 100.996$ | 101.56 | In Control |

**Step 5:** All $Z_i$ values lie within their respective time-varying limits. The process is **in control** for these four subgroups. However, $Z_4 = 100.996$ is approaching the asymptote of $UCL = 102.25$ — continued upward trend would trigger an alarm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the non-negative reset** in CUSUM: Many students compute $C_i^+$ as a simple cumulative sum without applying $\max[0, \cdot]$. This is **WRONG** and costs 2–3 marks per question.
> 2. **Using $\sigma$ instead of $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ in CUSUM/EWMA formulas**: The decision interval $H$ and reference value $K$ must be expressed in terms of $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$, not $\sigma$. This is the **#1 marking trap** in KTU papers.
> 3. **Initial condition error in EWMA**: Students often start the EWMA at $Z_0 = 0$ instead of $Z_0 = \mu_0$. This produces a misleading initial drop in the chart and costs full marks.
> 4. **Time-varying vs. steady-state limits confusion**: For early subgroups ($i < 10$), students must use the time-varying UCL/LCL formula. Using the steady-state limit for $i = 1$ produces a much tighter limit than reality.
> 5. **Forgetting to compute both upper AND lower CUSUMs**: KTU often asks for "two-sided CUSUM"; a single-sided answer loses 3 marks immediately.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **CUSUM** detects shifts of **0.5σ to 2.5σ**; EWMA detects **0.5σ to 2.0σ** — both are *far superior* to Shewhart for small shifts.
- [x] The CUSUM uses **two parameters**: $K$ (reference/slack value) and $H$ (decision interval). Standard choice: $K = 0.5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$, $H = 4\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ to $5\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.
- [x] The **non-negative reset** in CUSUM ($\max[0, \cdot]$) is the heart of the chart — never omit it.
- [x] The EWMA uses **two parameters**: $\lambda$ (smoothing constant, typically 0.1–0.3) and $L$ (limit factor, typically 3).
- [x] The EWMA statistic is initialized at $Z_0 = \mu_0$, **not zero**.
- [x] EWMA control limits are **time-varying** for the first ~20 subgroups and asymptote to a steady-state value.
- [x] **$ARL_0$** (in-control) should be **large (200–500)**; **$ARL_1$** (out-of-control) should be **small (3–10)**.
- [x] The **V-mask** is a graphical alternative to the tabular CUSUM; modern practice uses the tabular form.
- [x] The two **parallel CUSUMs** ($C_i^+$ and $C_i^-$) are independent; always compute both for a two-sided test.
- [x] Both charts use $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ — never confuse with $\sigma$ in formula substitution.
- [x] The EWMA chart is mathematically equivalent to a CUSUM chart under specific parameter equivalences: $\lambda = 2k$ and $L = h$.
- [x] For **autocorrelated processes** (common in chemical industries), EWMA outperforms CUSUM.
- [x] For **discrete and rare events**, CUSUM is preferred (e.g., in healthcare adverse-event monitoring).
- [x] KTU examiners often test: derivation of CUSUM recurrence, EWMA variance formula, and numerical plotting from a given dataset.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specifications and Process Capability: Process Capability Indices, Process Capability Analysis, Tolerances for Assemblies and Subassemblies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specifications and Process Capability: Process Capability Indices, Process Capability Analysis, Tolerances for Assemblies and Subassemblies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Process Capability (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability** is defined as the inherent, natural ability of a stable, in-control process to produce output that conforms to engineering specifications. It is a **statistical measure** of process performance expressed as a ratio of the **specification spread (tolerance band)** to the **process spread (6σ voice of the process)**.

In KTU 2024 terminology, *Process Capability Analysis* is the systematic engineering study that quantifies the **Voice of the Process (VOP)** relative to the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)**, where the VOP is represented by ±3σ around the process mean and the VOC is bounded by the **Upper Specification Limit (USL)** and **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)**.

### 1.2 Process Capability Indices (PCI) — Overview

A **Process Capability Index (PCI)** is a dimensionless numerical value that quantifies the relationship between process performance and design tolerances. The principal indices evaluated under KTU Module 2 are:

| Index | Name | Purpose |
|:------|:-----|:--------|
| **Cp** | Process Capability Index | Measures *potential* capability (centered processes) |
| **Cpk** | Process Capability Index (Adjusted) | Measures *actual* capability (accounts for off-centering) |
| **Pp** | Process Performance Index | Long-term / overall performance |
| **Ppk** | Process Performance Index (Adjusted) | Long-term performance, off-center adjusted |
| **Cpm** | Taguchi Capability Index | Includes target value penalty |
| **Cr** | Capability Ratio | Reciprocal of Cp |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Highway and the Road Width:**
> 
> Imagine your manufacturing process is a **car driving down a highway**. The car naturally weaves within a lane whose width equals **6σ** (the process spread). The **road's edge lines** represent the **USL and LSL** (the customer's specification limits). 
> 
> - **Cp** asks: *"Is my weaving lane narrow enough to fit comfortably inside the road markings?"* (Potential capability)
> - **Cpk** asks: *"Is my weaving lane narrow enough AND am I driving in the middle of the road, or hugging one edge?"* (Actual capability)
> - **A high Cp but low Cpk** = a precise driver who insists on driving in the wrong lane. **A high Cpk** = a precise driver who stays perfectly centered.

### 1.4 Tolerances for Assemblies and Subassemblies — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tolerance Stack-up** is the cumulative effect of part-to-part dimensional variation on the assembly's critical functional dimensions. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes two primary methodologies:
> 
> 1. **Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Tolerancing** — Sum of absolute tolerances
> 2. **Statistical (RSS — Root Sum of Squares) Tolerancing** — Quadratic summation of standard deviations
> 
> The choice between these methods fundamentally alters achievable assembly yield and manufacturing cost.

**Real-world context:** In automotive transmission assemblies, a critical clearance of **0.50 ± 0.05 mm** must be achieved despite individual shaft, bearing, and housing tolerances each being ±0.10 mm. The tolerance stack-up analysis determines whether 100% assembly yield is feasible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Assumptions of Capability Analysis

For all capability indices to be **mathematically valid and KTU-exam ready**, the following conditions must hold:

1. **Statistical Stability**: The process must be in a state of *statistical control* (no special-cause variation, verified by **X-bar and R control charts**).
2. **Normality**: Output must follow a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution** N(μ, σ²). Verify via histogram, probability plot, or **Anderson–Darling test**.
3. **Independence**: Observations must be statistically independent (no autocorrelation).
4. **Continuous Data**: Measurements must be on a continuous ratio/interval scale.

### 2.2 Process Capability Index — Cp (Potential Capability)

**Definition:** Cp measures the ability of a process to meet specifications **assuming the process mean is perfectly centered** between the specification limits.

**Why it exists:** Cp ignores location; it only measures dispersion. It represents the **best-case scenario** where centering has been optimized.

**Logical derivation steps:**
- Total specification width = USL − LSL
- Natural process spread (±3σ) = 6σ
- Cp = (Allowable width) / (Actual width)
- **Higher Cp = Better process relative to specifications**
- Cp = 1.00 means the process barely fits (theoretical zero defect level of 0.27% or 2700 ppm)
- Cp = 1.33 is the KTU industry benchmark for existing processes
- Cp = 1.67 is required for new processes (Six Sigma target)

### 2.3 Process Capability Index — Cpk (Actual Capability)

**Definition:** Cpk measures the actual capability of a process **incorporating both dispersion AND centering**.

**Why it exists:** Real processes are rarely perfectly centered. Cpk penalizes off-center conditions, giving the *minimum* of two one-sided capability values:

**Logic of min function:**
- Upper capability: (USL − μ) / 3σ — distance from mean to USL
- Lower capability: (μ − LSL) / 3σ — distance from mean to LSL
- Cpk = minimum of these two values (the *worse* side governs)

**Relationship:** Cp ≥ Cpk always. If Cp = Cpk, the process is perfectly centered.

### 2.4 Process Performance Indices — Pp and Ppk

**Pp and Ppk** use the **overall standard deviation (σ_overall)** computed from all data including between-subgroup variation, while Cp and Cpk use the **within-subgroup standard deviation (σ_within)** estimated from the average of subgroup ranges.

**Why two versions exist:**
- **Cp/Cpk** → Short-term, "potential" capability, free of drift effects
- **Pp/Ppk** → Long-term, "actual" performance including all sources of variation
- **Ppk ≤ Cpk** generally; the gap indicates the magnitude of process drift

### 2.5 Taguchi Capability Index — Cpm

Cpk does not penalize deviation from the **Target Value (T)**. The **Taguchi Cpm** index addresses this by including a squared deviation term:

$$C_{pm} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$$

### 2.6 Capability Ratio — Cr

Cr is simply the reciprocal of Cp: **Cr = 1/Cp**, sometimes used in older European quality standards. A Cr < 0.75 corresponds to Cp > 1.33.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Pin this for your KTU exam preparation.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Range | KTU Use |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------|:--------------|:--------|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | σ = within-std dev | Dimensionless; Cp ≥ 1 ideal | Module 2 — most frequent |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | μ = process mean | Cpk ≤ Cp; ≥ 1.33 desired | Module 2 — paired with Cp |
| 3 | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided upper | Always ≥ 0 | Sub-derivation |
| 4 | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided lower | Always ≥ 0 | Sub-derivation |
| 5 | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2+(\mu-T)^2}}$ | T = target | Penalizes off-target | Taguchi-focused Q |
| 6 | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{overall}}$ | Overall σ | Long-term metric | Distinguish from Cp |
| 7 | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left[\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma_{overall}},\; \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma_{overall}}\right]$ | Overall version | Long-term | Distinguish from Cpk |
| 8 | $C_r = \dfrac{1}{C_p}$ | Reciprocal | Legacy metric | Rarely asked |
| 9 | $\sigma_{within} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | $\bar{R}$ = avg range; d₂ = control-chart constant | Estimate of short-term σ | Computational setup |
| 10 | $T_{Worst} = \sum \vert t_i \vert$ | Absolute sum | Conservative stack-up | Tolerance analysis |
| 11 | $T_{Stat} = \sqrt{\sum t_i^2}$ | RSS / Quadratic sum | Realistic stack-up | Tolerance analysis |
| 12 | $\sigma_{Assembly} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ | σ for each component | Statistical stack-up | Tolerance analysis |

**Control Chart Constants (d₂) — KTU-referenced values:**

| Subgroup Size n | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 10 |
|:---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| d₂ | 1.128 | 1.693 | 2.059 | 2.326 | 2.534 | 2.704 | 3.078 |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry**: Cpk ≥ 1.33 mandatory for safety-critical components (brake discs, steering linkages).
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing**: Cpk ≥ 1.67 for sub-10nm lithography processes.
- **Aerospace (AS9100)**: Capability indices are auditable metrics; Cpk < 1.0 triggers non-conformance reports.
- **Tolerance Stack-up in Gears**: Statistical tolerancing of pinion-shaft-bearing-housing stack determines gearbox NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) performance.
- **Biomedical Devices**: Insulin pen dosing accuracy requires capability analysis on plunger travel tolerances.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cp from the Normal Distribution Framework

**Step 1 — Establish the process spread.**
For a normal distribution N(μ, σ²), the total spread covering **99.73% of all measurements** is:

$$\text{Process Spread} = \mu + 3\sigma - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma$$

**Step 2 — Establish the specification spread.**
The total specification width is:

$$\text{Specification Spread} = USL - LSL$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**
Capability is the ratio of allowable width to actual width:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Width}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
A Cp of 1.00 means the process barely meets specs; Cp = 1.33 leaves room for ordinary variation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cpk as the Minimum of Two One-Sided Indices

**Step 1 — Define the upper one-sided index.**
The distance from the process mean to the USL, scaled by 3σ:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Define the lower one-sided index.**
The distance from the process mean to the LSL, scaled by 3σ:

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the minimum operator.**
The *worse-performing side* determines actual capability:

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\; C_{pl})$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent single-form expression.**

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\frac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Relationship to Cp.**
Note that:

$$\frac{C_{pu} + C_{pl}}{2} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = C_p$$

So **Cpk = Cp − k**, where k = |μ − midpoint|/σ, quantifying the centering penalty.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Process Standard Deviation from Range Statistics

**Step 1 — Sample mean of subgroup ranges.**
For k subgroups of size n, the average range is:

$$\bar{R} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply the d₂ correction factor.**
The expected value of the range of n normal samples is d₂·σ. Therefore, an unbiased estimator of σ_within is:

$$\sigma_{within} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cp.**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6 \cdot (\bar{R}/d_2)} = \frac{d_2(USL - LSL)}{6\bar{R}}$$

**Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**
Given: n = 5, k = 25 subgroups, $\bar{R}$ = 0.42 mm, USL = 12.00 mm, LSL = 11.80 mm, mean μ = 11.92 mm.

Substitute d₂ = 2.326 (for n = 5):

$$\sigma = \frac{0.42}{2.326} = 0.1806\;\text{mm}$$

$$C_p = \frac{12.00 - 11.80}{6 \times 0.1806} = \frac{0.20}{1.0834} = 0.1846$$

$$C_{pu} = \frac{12.00 - 11.92}{3 \times 0.1806} = \frac{0.08}{0.5417} = 0.1477$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{11.92 - 11.80}{3 \times 0.1806} = \frac{0.12}{0.5417} = 0.2216$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.1477,\; 0.2216) = 0.1477$$

**Conclusion:** The process is **incapable** (Cpk ≪ 1.0) and the upper side is the limiting factor. Immediate corrective action and process re-centering are required.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Worst-Case Tolerance Stack-Up

**Problem setup:** An assembly has n components stacked in a series path. Each component i has a tolerance t_i (often ±t_i/2 deviation).

**Step 1 — Define the assembly nominal dimension.**

$$Y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 2 — Define the assembly tolerance bound.**
Taking absolute values of all extreme conditions:

$$T_{Assembly,\,Worst} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert t_i \vert$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
Worst-case guarantees **100% interchangeability**: every assembly will satisfy specs as long as each part is within its own tolerance. This is conservative and increases manufacturing cost.

**Worked example:** A shaft assembly has three subassemblies with tolerances ±0.05 mm, ±0.08 mm, and ±0.04 mm.

$$T_{Worst} = 0.05 + 0.08 + 0.04 = 0.17\;\text{mm}$$

This means the assembly gap can vary by **0.34 mm total** in the worst case.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Statistical (RSS) Tolerance Stack-Up

**Step 1 — Assume independent normal distributions.**
Each component dimension x_i ~ N(μ_i, σ_i²).

**Step 2 — Variance of the sum.**
For independent random variables, variances add:

$$\sigma_Y^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2$$

**Step 3 — Tolerance corresponding to ±3σ.**

$$T_{Assembly,\,Stat} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^2}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with worst-case.**
Using the inequality of norms: $\sqrt{\sum t_i^2} \leq \sum \vert t_i \vert$. Statistical tolerancing gives a **tighter, more realistic** tolerance band.

**Worked example (same assembly):**
Assume each tolerance is ±3σ_i, so σ_i = t_i/3.

$$\sigma_Y^2 = \left(\frac{0.05}{3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{0.08}{3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{0.04}{3}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_Y^2 = \frac{0.0025 + 0.0064 + 0.0016}{9} = \frac{0.0105}{9} = 0.001167\;\text{mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_Y = 0.0341\;\text{mm}$$

$$T_{Stat} = 6\sigma_Y = 6 \times 0.0341 = 0.2049\;\text{mm}\;\text{(total band)}$$

Or as a ± tolerance: ±0.1025 mm. The **statistical assembly tolerance is significantly tighter** than the worst-case 0.17 mm half-width.

**Key insight for KTU:** Statistical tolerancing assumes only **0.27% probability** of exceeding limits per side (3σ), whereas worst-case tolerancing assumes **0% probability** of exceeding limits. The trade-off is yield vs. risk.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Capability vs. Action

| Cpk Value | Process Status | KTU-Expected Action |
|:---------:|:---------------|:--------------------|
| Cpk < 1.00 | **Incapable** | Immediate process modification, 100% inspection |
| 1.00 ≤ Cpk < 1.33 | **Marginally capable** | Tighten controls, implement SPC |
| 1.33 ≤ Cpk < 1.50 | **Capable** | Maintain controls, periodic monitoring |
| 1.50 ≤ Cpk < 1.67 | **Highly capable** | Reduce inspection frequency, consider skip-lot |
| Cpk ≥ 1.67 | **Six-Sigma capable** | World-class performance, minimal inspection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Process Capability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define CTQ Critical to Quality Characteristic] --> B[Step 2: Plan Data Collection n subgroups of size 5]
    B --> C[Step 3: Collect Measurements in Rational Subgroups]
    C --> D[Step 4: Test for Stability using X-bar and R Control Charts]
    D --> E{Is Process in Statistical Control?}
    E -- No --> F[Identify and Remove Special Causes Then Re-collect Data]
    F --> C
    E -- Yes --> G[Step 5: Test for Normality Histogram and Probability Plot]
    G --> H{Is Data Approximately Normal?}
    H -- No --> I[Apply Data Transformation or Use Non parametric Methods]
    H -- Yes --> J[Step 6: Compute mu and sigma_within from X-double-bar and R-bar]
    J --> K[Step 7: Calculate Cp and Cpk]
    K --> L[Step 8: Calculate Pp and Ppk using Overall Std Dev]
    L --> M[Step 9: Compare Cpk with Specification Cpk greater than 1.33]
    M --> N[Step 10: Decide Action Continue, Improve, or Redesign]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capability Index Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Spread 6 sigma] --> B[Cp Computation]
    C[Specification Width USL minus LSL] --> B
    B --> D[Cp Value]
    E[Process Mean mu] --> F[One Sided Upper Cpu]
    A --> F
    C --> F
    E --> G[One Sided Lower Cpl]
    A --> G
    C --> G
    F --> H[Min Function]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Cpk Value]
    J[Target Value T] --> K[Taguchi Cpm]
    A --> K
    C --> K
    E --> K
    L[Overall Std Dev sigma_overall] --> M[Pp and Ppk Indices]
    C --> M
    E --> M
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Tolerance Stack-Up Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assembly with N Series Components] --> B{Choose Tolerancing Method}
    B -- Worst Case --> C[Sum Absolute Tolerances T equals Sum t_i]
    C --> D[Result Conservative, 100 percent Interchangeability]
    B -- Statistical RSS --> E[Sum Variances sigma_y squared equals Sum sigma_i squared]
    E --> F[T equals 6 times Square Root of Sum sigma_i squared]
    F --> G[Result Tighter, Realistic, 99.73 percent Yield]
    D --> H[High Manufacturing Cost, Guaranteed Fit]
    G --> I[Lower Cost, Small Risk of Defect]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Capability Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Computed Cpk Value] --> B{Cpk less than 1.00}
    B -- Yes --> C[Process is Incapable Investigate Immediately]
    B -- No --> D{Cpk between 1.00 and 1.33}
    D -- Yes --> E[Marginally Capable Implement SPC Tighten Controls]
    D -- No --> F{Cpk greater than 1.33}
    F -- Yes --> G[Process is Capable Maintain and Monitor]
    F -- No --> H[Outlier Check Data Integrity]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Process Capability Index (Cp) and Process Capability Index adjusted for centering (Cpk). Why is Cpk considered a more realistic measure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Cp** is the *potential* capability of a process, defined as $C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6\sigma)$. It assumes the process mean is centered at the midpoint of the specification limits. It measures only the **dispersion** of the process relative to the specification width. **(1 mark)**
- **Cpk** is the *actual* capability, defined as $C_{pk} = \min[(USL-\mu)/(3\sigma), (\mu-LSL)/(3\sigma)]$. It accounts for both dispersion **and** the location of the process mean relative to the specification limits. **(1 mark)**
- **Why Cpk is more realistic:** In real production environments, processes are rarely perfectly centered. Cp may be high even when the mean drifts toward a specification limit, producing defects. Cpk captures this real-world off-centering by taking the minimum of the upper and lower one-sided capabilities, providing a true measure of defect potential. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**With a neat sketch, explain the concept of statistical (RSS) tolerance stack-up. State one key assumption.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Concept:** In an assembly with n components, when each component dimension follows an independent normal distribution with standard deviation σ_i, the assembly variation is computed using the **Root Sum of Squares (RSS)** method: $T_{Assembly} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^2}$ **(1.5 marks)**
- **Sketch description:** Component tolerances shown as ±t_i values for stacked dimensions, with the assembly tolerance band tighter than the arithmetic sum. **(1 mark)**
- **Key assumption:** The component dimensions must be **statistically independent** and approximately **normally distributed**. Under this assumption, only 0.27% of assemblies will fall outside ±3σ limits. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]

**(a)** A CNC turning process produces shafts with **USL = 25.04 mm** and **LSL = 24.96 mm**. Twenty-five subgroups of size 5 were collected. The average of subgroup means is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.005$ mm, and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.012$ mm. **Calculate Cp and Cpk.** Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Process Capability Analysis**. List and briefly explain the **five sequential steps** involved in performing a capability study. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Look up the d₂ constant for subgroup size n = 5.
From the standard table: **d₂ = 2.326**. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 2:** Calculate the within-subgroup standard deviation. **(1 mark)**

$$\sigma_{within} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.012}{2.326} = 0.005159\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Cp. **(1.5 marks)**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.04 - 24.96}{6 \times 0.005159} = \frac{0.08}{0.03096} = 2.584$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the one-sided capabilities. **(1.5 marks)**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.04 - 25.005}{3 \times 0.005159} = \frac{0.035}{0.01548} = 2.262$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.005 - 24.96}{3 \times 0.005159} = \frac{0.045}{0.01548} = 2.908$$

**Step 5:** Determine Cpk. **(1 mark)**

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(2.262,\; 2.908) = 2.262$$

**Step 6:** Comment on the result. **(1.5 marks)**

Both Cp = 2.584 and Cpk = 2.262 are well above the **Cpk = 1.33** industry benchmark. The process is **highly capable** and approaches **Six-Sigma levels** (Cpk ≥ 1.67). The minor off-centering (mean = 25.005 vs. midpoint = 25.000) causes Cpk to be slightly lower than Cp, but the process comfortably meets specifications. The manufacturing cost may be reduced by **relaxing incoming inspection frequency** (skip-lot or skip-batch inspection schemes).

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Process Capability Analysis** is a structured statistical study that quantifies the ability of a stable manufacturing process to produce output conforming to engineering specifications. **(1 mark)**

**The five sequential steps are:**

1. **Planning and Preparation (1 mark):** Identify the *Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)* characteristic, define specifications (USL, LSL, Target), determine sample size (typically 25 subgroups of 5 samples each = 125 measurements, sufficient to detect a 1.5σ shift with 90% power), and establish data collection procedures (use of rational subgroups at uniform time intervals).

2. **Data Collection (1 mark):** Take measurements in **rational subgroups** — small samples (n = 2 to 5) collected over a short period to capture only common-cause variation. Use calibrated instruments, randomized sampling, and record time/order to enable trend analysis.

3. **Stability Verification (1.5 marks):** Construct **X-bar and R control charts** and verify all plotted points lie within control limits. If any points fall outside, the process is *out of statistical control*; special causes must be identified, removed, and data re-collected. Stability is the **prerequisite** for all capability calculations.

4. **Normality Assessment and Parameter Estimation (1.5 marks):** Test whether data follows a normal distribution using histograms, normal probability plots, or formal tests (Anderson–Darling, Shapiro–Wilk). If non-normal, apply transformations (Box-Cox) or non-parametric methods. Estimate μ (process mean) and σ (process standard deviation, typically using $\bar{R}/d_2$).

5. **Computation of Capability Indices and Interpretation (1 mark):** Calculate Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk and compare with target values. Cp/Cpk ≥ 1.33 indicates capability; Cpk ≥ 1.67 indicates Six-Sigma capability. Decide between **continue, improve, or redesign** actions, and document the study in a formal report.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Worst-Case** and **Statistical (RSS)** methods of tolerance stack-up analysis. A shaft assembly consists of three components with tolerances **±0.10 mm, ±0.15 mm, and ±0.08 mm** respectively. Compute the assembly tolerance using both methods and comment. **(8 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Taguchi Capability Index (Cpm)**? How does it differ from Cpk? Under what conditions is Cpm particularly useful? **(6 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a) — 8 Marks**

**Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Tolerance Stack-Up:** **(2.5 marks)**

This method assumes that **all components simultaneously reach their extreme tolerance limits** in the worst possible direction. It is also called the **arithmetic method** or **100% interchangeability method**.

$$T_{Worst} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert t_i \vert$$

- **Advantages:** Guarantees 100% assembly yield, every assembly will be within the calculated tolerance. Simple to compute and verify.
- **Disadvantages:** Highly conservative, leads to **tight component tolerances**, which increase manufacturing cost substantially. May force precision machining where standard tolerances would suffice.

**Statistical (RSS) Tolerance Stack-Up:** **(2.5 marks)**

This method uses the **Root Sum of Squares** of component tolerances, assuming independent normal distributions. For ±3σ tolerances:

$$T_{Stat} = 3\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{t_i}{3}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^2}$$

- **Advantages:** Realistic — assumes extreme conditions are statistically unlikely. Allows looser component tolerances, reducing cost. Typical industrial yield: 99.73% within band.
- **Disadvantages:** Risk of defects (~0.27%), 100% inspection may be required for safety-critical assemblies.

**Numerical Computation:** **(2 marks)**

Given: t₁ = ±0.10 mm, t₂ = ±0.15 mm, t₃ = ±0.08 mm

**Worst-case:**
$$T_{Worst} = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.08 = 0.33\;\text{mm}\;\text{(half-width)}$$

**Statistical (RSS):**
$$T_{Stat} = \sqrt{0.10^2 + 0.15^2 + 0.08^2} = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.0225 + 0.0064} = \sqrt{0.0389} = 0.1973\;\text{mm}$$

**Comment:** **(1 mark)**

The statistical method gives a tolerance band **0.1973 mm** vs. the worst-case **0.33 mm** — a **40% reduction** in tolerance band. The statistical method is acceptable provided the three component dimensions are independent and the 0.27% defect risk is acceptable. For safety-critical applications (aerospace), worst-case is mandated.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b) — 6 Marks**

**Taguchi Capability Index Cpm:** **(2 marks)**

The **Cpm index**, developed by Genichi Taguchi, is an enhanced capability index that **penalizes deviation from a target value (T)**, not just the specification limits. It is defined as:

$$C_{pm} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$$

**Difference from Cpk:** **(2 marks)**

| Aspect | Cpk | Cpm |
|:-------|:----|:----|
| Penalizes off-centering | Yes (from midpoint) | Yes (from **target**) |
| Symmetric spec assumed | No (uses both limits) | **Yes** (assumes T = midpoint) |
| Off-target penalty | Ignored | Explicitly included |
| Quadratic loss | No | Yes (Taguchi philosophy) |

When μ = T = midpoint, Cpm = Cp. As the mean deviates from target, Cpm drops faster than Cpk.

**When Cpm is useful:** **(2 marks)**

1. **Asymmetric quality loss situations** — when deviation from target causes progressive quality loss (e.g., a resistor whose resistance deviates from nominal causes circuit performance degradation).
2. **Target-oriented processes** — e.g., a piston diameter where target performance depends on the diameter being exactly at nominal, not merely within limits.
3. **Robust design validation** — Taguchi's quality engineering philosophy emphasizes *minimum loss to society*, and Cpm operationalizes this principle.
4. **Pharmaceutical and chemical industries** — where the target value is critical for product efficacy (e.g., drug concentration).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Process Capability Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to look up d₂ correctly** — students often use d₂ = √n or d₂ = 1.0, leading to wrong σ estimates. **KTU board examiners specifically check this table lookup.**
> 2. **Confusing Cp with Cpk calculation** — Cp uses midpoint of specs, Cpk uses process mean. Many students apply the same formula. This is a **2-mark loss per error**.
> 3. **Not stating normality assumption** — all capability indices are *mathematically invalid* without normality. Always mention "assuming the data is approximately normally distributed" — **0.5 to 1 mark lost if omitted**.
> 4. **Mixing up Pp with Cp** — Pp uses *overall* standard deviation; Cp uses *within-subgroup* standard deviation (via $\bar{R}/d_2$). This is a frequent **3-mark trap**.
> 5. **Using wrong RSS formula** — students often write $\sqrt{\sum t_i}$ instead of $\sqrt{\sum t_i^2}$. Always square each term before summing.
> 6. **Not interpreting the result** — computing Cpk without concluding "the process is capable/incapable" loses **1 to 2 marks** for incomplete analysis.
> 7. **Worst-case vs. statistical confusion** — the question wording matters: "guaranteed interchangeability" → worst-case; "statistical yield" → RSS.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Sub-Topic**

- ☐ **Cp** = (USL − LSL) / 6σ — measures **dispersion only**, assumes perfect centering.
- ☐ **Cpk** = min[(USL − μ)/3σ, (μ − LSL)/3σ] — measures **dispersion AND centering**.
- ☐ **Cp ≥ Cpk** always; equality implies perfect centering.
- ☐ **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the **industry benchmark** for existing processes.
- ☐ **Cpk ≥ 1.67** is the **Six-Sigma target** for new processes.
- ☐ **Pp and Ppk** use σ_overall (long-term), giving a more pessimistic view than Cp/Cpk.
- ☐ **Cpm** is the Taguchi index that penalizes deviation from the **target** T, not just the midpoint.
- ☐ **σ_within** is estimated from the average range: σ = $\bar{R}$/d₂ — **must use correct d₂**.
- ☐ **Worst-case tolerance** = $\sum \vert t_i \vert$ — guarantees 100% yield, very conservative.
- ☐ **Statistical (RSS) tolerance** = $\sqrt{\sum t_i^2}$ — realistic, assumes normal & independent, 99.73% within band.
- ☐ **Statistical tolerance is always ≤ worst-case tolerance**, often by **30–50%**.
- ☐ **Assumption hierarchy:** (1) statistical control, (2) normality, (3) independence, (4) continuous data.
- ☐ **Process Capability Analysis (PCA) steps:** Plan → Collect → Verify Stability → Check Normality → Compute Indices → Interpret.
- ☐ **Control charts** (X-bar and R) are the **gatekeepers** — no capability index is valid without prior stability verification.
- ☐ **Cr = 1/Cp** is the legacy European capability ratio.
- ☐ For **assemblies**, choose worst-case for safety-critical; RSS for non-critical, cost-sensitive applications.
- ☐ Always **state assumptions explicitly** in KTU answers — normality, independence, stability.
- ☐ Units cancel in Cp/Cpk (dimensionless) but must be **consistent** (all mm or all cm).
- ☐ Process improvement priority: **reduce variability first**, then re-center the mean.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single, Double, Multiple and Sequential Sampling Plans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single, Double, Multiple and Sequential Sampling Plans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Acceptance Sampling — The Broader Context

Before we dissect the four sampling plans, we must position them within **Acceptance Sampling**, the statistical quality-control discipline that decides whether a *lot* of incoming or finished goods should be accepted or rejected based on inspection of a randomly drawn sample.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Sampling (KTU Definition):** A statistical quality-control procedure in which a predetermined number of sample items (n) are drawn at random from a lot of size **N**, and the lot is either accepted or rejected based on the number of defectives (**d**) observed in the sample, in accordance with a pre-established rule called the **Sampling Plan**.

There are two broad classes:

| Class | Inspection Conducted | Cost per Item | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inspection by Attributes** | Item classified as *defective* or *non-defective* | Low | Visual inspection of bolts |
| **Inspection by Variables** | Actual measurement taken | High | Tensile strength of cables |

All four plans in this module are **attribute-based** sampling plans, where the decision is binary and the outcome is governed by the **Binomial distribution** (or Poisson approximation).

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Gatekeeper at the Factory Gate"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are the *Quality Gatekeeper* of a biscuit factory. Every hour, a huge truck of 5,000 biscuit packets arrives. You cannot open and eat-test each packet (that is 100% inspection — slow, costly, and ruins the goods). So you take a small handful — the *sample* — and taste them. If *too many* taste bad, you reject the entire truck. If only a *few* are bad, you let the truck in. The **Sampling Plan** is the *rule book* you follow: *how many* to taste, and *how many bad ones* are tolerable.

The "rule book" comes in four flavours — that is the heart of Module 3.

## 1.3 Quick Glance — The Four Plans at a Glance

- **Single Sampling Plan:** One sample → One decision. The simplest, classical, textbook standard.
- **Double Sampling Plan:** One sample may not be enough. If the first sample is "borderline", a *second* sample is drawn. Final decision combines both.
- **Multiple Sampling Plan:** A generalisation of the double plan, extended to *k* successive stages. Useful for very high-volume inspection lines.
- **Sequential Sampling Plan:** Items are inspected *one by one*, and after each item a fresh decision is made: *accept*, *reject*, or *continue*. Theoretically the most efficient in terms of *Average Sample Number (ASN)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need four plans?** Because inspection is **expensive**. The plan with the smallest average sample size for a given protection is preferred. Sequential plans minimise ASN at the cost of administrative complexity. KTU frequently tests the trade-off between these plans.

## 1.4 Key Nomenclature Used Throughout the Module

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **N** | Lot size | 100 – 10,000 |
| **n** | Sample size | 10 – 200 |
| **c** | Acceptance number | 0, 1, 2, 3 |
| **d** | Number of defectives found in sample | Integer ≥ 0 |
| **p** | Incoming fraction defective | 0.00 – 1.00 |
| **Pₐ** | Probability of acceptance (function of p) | 0.00 – 1.00 |
| **α** | Producer's risk | 0.01 – 0.10 |
| **β** | Consumer's risk | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| **AQL** | Acceptable Quality Level (p₀) | 0.001 – 0.10 |
| **LTPD / LQ** | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (p₁) | 0.02 – 0.20 |
| **ASN** | Average Sample Number | Plan-dependent |
| **AOQ** | Average Outgoing Quality | 0.00 – p (max) |
| **AOQL** | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | Maximum of AOQ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — Heart of Every Sampling Plan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OC Curve Definition:** The **Operating Characteristic Curve** is the graph of **Pₐ (Probability of Acceptance)** versus the **incoming fraction defective (p)**. It is the *fingerprint* of any sampling plan. Two points on this curve are of paramount importance:
> 1. **Producer's Risk Point:** (p = p₀, Pₐ = 1 − α)
> 2. **Consumer's Risk Point:** (p = p₁, Pₐ = β)

The OC curve tells the engineer *how the plan behaves* for *any* incoming quality — perfect, mediocre, or terrible. A *good* sampling plan has a steeply falling OC curve: high acceptance for good lots, low acceptance for bad lots.

## 2.2 Single Sampling Plan — Operational Logic

The operational logic proceeds in **three discrete steps**:

1. **Draw** a random sample of size **n** from a lot of size **N**.
2. **Inspect** each of the n items; count the number of defectives **d**.
3. **Decide**:
   - If **d ≤ c** → **Accept** the lot.
   - If **d > c** → **Reject** the lot.

### 2.2.1 Probability of Acceptance (Binomial Form)

The number of defectives **d** in a sample of size **n** follows a **Binomial distribution** with parameters **n** and **p**, provided n/N ≤ 0.10 (the *infinite lot* or *binomial* assumption). Hence:

$$P_{a} \;=\; P(d \le c) \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} \, p^{\,d} \, (1-p)^{\,n-d}$$

### 2.2.2 Poisson Approximation (Useful When n ≥ 16 and p ≤ 0.10)

When both **n** is moderately large and **p** is very small, the Binomial collapses to the **Poisson distribution** with mean **np = λ**. This is the form most KTU problems use for hand calculation:

$$P_{a} \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} \, (np)^{d}}{d!}$$

### 2.2.3 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) and AOQL

The **AOQ** measures the *long-run average* fraction defective in the lots that leave the inspection station, assuming all rejected lots are replaced by 100% good ones (rectifying inspection):

$$AOQ \;=\; \frac{p \, P_{a} \, (N-n)}{N} \;\approx\; p \, P_{a} \quad \text{(when N >> n)}$$

The **AOQL** is the *peak* value of the AOQ curve:

$$AOQL \;=\; \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \left[ \, AOQ(p) \, \right]$$

### 2.2.4 Average Total Inspection (ATI)

The **ATI** is the average number of items inspected per lot, including the 100% inspection cost on rejected lots:

$$ATI \;=\; n \, P_{a} \;+\; N \, (1 - P_{a})$$

## 2.3 Double Sampling Plan — Operational Logic

A double sampling plan is defined by **five parameters**: **n₁, c₁, c₂, n₂, n_total** (with n_total being the absolute maximum, often n₁ + n₂ or 2·n₁).

**Step 1:** Draw a sample of size **n₁** and count defectives **d₁**.

**Step 2 (Three branches):**

| Condition on d₁ | Action | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **d₁ ≤ c₁** | **Accept** the lot | Excellent evidence |
| **d₁ > c₂** | **Reject** the lot | Overwhelmingly bad evidence |
| **c₁ < d₁ ≤ c₂** | Take a *second* sample of size **n₂** | Borderline; inconclusive |

**Step 3:** Inspect the second sample; count defectives **d₂**. Compute combined defectives **D = d₁ + d₂**.

- If **D ≤ c₂** → Accept the lot.
- If **D > c₂** → Reject the lot.

### 2.3.1 Probability of Acceptance for a Double Plan

Let *P*₁ = probability of acceptance on first sample, *P*₂ = probability of acceptance after second sample, *P*₂' = probability of *going to second sample*.

$$P_{a} \;=\; P_{1} \;+\; P_{2}$$

$$P_{1} \;=\; P(d_1 \le c_1) \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{c_1} \binom{n_1}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n_1 - d}$$

$$P_{2}' \;=\; P(c_1 < d_1 \le c_2) \;=\; \sum_{d=c_1+1}^{c_2} \binom{n_1}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n_1 - d}$$

$$P_{2} \;=\; P_{2}' \;\times\; P(d_2 \le c_2 - d_1 \mid d_1)$$

For the *combined* second-stage acceptance, we sum over all values of d₁ that triggered the second sample:

$$P_{2} \;=\; \sum_{d_1 = c_1+1}^{c_2} \binom{n_1}{d_1} p^{d_1} (1-p)^{n_1 - d_1} \;\left[\, \sum_{d_2 = 0}^{c_2 - d_1} \binom{n_2}{d_2} p^{d_2} (1-p)^{n_2 - d_2} \,\right]$$

### 2.3.2 Average Sample Number (ASN) for a Double Plan

$$ASN \;=\; n_1 \;+\; n_2 \;\cdot\; P_{2}'$$

## 2.4 Multiple Sampling Plan — Logic Generalisation

A *k*-stage multiple sampling plan operates on the same principle, but extends the *continue* branch across **k stages**. The plan is fully specified by:

$$\{ n_1, c_1 \,;\, n_2, c_2 - c_1 \,;\, \dots \,;\, n_k, c_k - c_{k-1} \}$$

After each stage *i*, a decision is made:
- If cumulative defects **dᵢ ≤ cᵢ** → Accept.
- If cumulative defects **dᵢ > cᵢ'** (the upper cut-off) → Reject.
- Otherwise → Continue to next stage (draw **nᵢ₊₁** more items).

The **ASN** is:

$$ASN \;=\; n_1 \;+\; n_2 \, P(\text{continue at stage 1}) \;+\; n_3 \, P(\text{continue at stage 2}) \;+\; \dots$$

## 2.5 Sequential Sampling Plan — Wald's SPRT (1937)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT):** A sequential sampling plan in which items are inspected *one at a time* and after each item the cumulative evidence is tested against two parallel straight lines on a graph of cumulative defects (**d**) versus cumulative sample size (**n**).

The plan is defined by **two competing hypotheses** on the incoming fraction defective:

- **H₀:** p = p₀ (lot is *acceptable*, AQL)
- **H₁:** p = p₁ (lot is *rejectable*, LQ or LTPD)

with tolerable errors:
- **α** = Producer's risk (P[reject lot when p = p₀])
- **β** = Consumer's risk (P[accept lot when p = p₁])

### 2.5.1 The Two Boundary Constants

$$A \;=\; \frac{\beta}{1 - \alpha} \quad \text{(acceptance boundary constant)}$$

$$B \;=\; \frac{1 - \beta}{\alpha} \quad \text{(rejection boundary constant)}$$

### 2.5.2 The Slope and Intercepts

The slope of the boundary lines on the (n, d) plane:

$$s \;=\; \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_1}{p_0}\right)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_1}{p_0}\right) \;-\; \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - p_1}{1 - p_0}\right)} \;=\; \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_1}{p_0}\right)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_1(1-p_0)}{p_0(1-p_1)}\right)}$$

The two intercepts (on the d-axis at n = 0):

$$h_{0} \;=\; \frac{\ln B}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_1(1-p_0)}{p_0(1-p_1)}\right)} \;=\; \frac{-\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-\beta}{\alpha}\right)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_1(1-p_0)}{p_0(1-p_1)}\right)}$$

$$h_{1} \;=\; \frac{\ln A}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_1(1-p_0)}{p_0(1-p_1)}\right)} \;=\; \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{1-\alpha}\right)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_1(1-p_0)}{p_0(1-p_1)}\right)}$$

### 2.5.3 The Decision Rule

After each observation, plot the point (**n**, **d**):

- If **d ≤ h₀ + s·n** → **Accept H₀** (Accept the lot).
- If **d ≥ h₁ + s·n** → **Reject H₀** (Reject the lot).
- Otherwise → **Continue** (draw another item).

### 2.5.4 Truncation

In practice, the sequential test is *truncated* at a maximum sample size **n_max** (usually taken as 3 times the comparable single-sampling n). At n = n_max, a forced decision is made by comparing d with a single-sampling equivalent c.

### 2.5.5 Average Sample Number (ASN) for the SPRT

The ASN depends on the *true* incoming quality p. The two canonical cases:

**At the acceptable quality p = p₀:**

$$ASN_{p_0} \;=\; \frac{(1-\alpha) \ln B \;-\; \alpha \ln A}{p_0 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_0}{p_1}\right) \;+\; (1-p_0) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-p_0}{1-p_1}\right)}$$

**At the rejectable quality p = p₁:**

$$ASN_{p_1} \;=\; \frac{\beta \ln B \;-\; (1-\beta) \ln A}{p_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_0}{p_1}\right) \;+\; (1-p_1) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-p_0}{1-p_1}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the SPRT famous?** It is *optimal* in the sense of Wald-Wolfowitz (1948) — no other test, sequential or fixed-sample, can achieve a smaller expected sample size for the same (α, β) protection. This is why KTU loves to test it.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (One-Page Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Plan — Pₐ (Binomial) | $P_{a}=\sum_{d=0}^{c}\binom{n}{d}p^{d}(1-p)^{n-d}$ | Valid for any n, p |
| 2 | Single Plan — Pₐ (Poisson) | $P_{a}=\sum_{d=0}^{c}\frac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}$ | Use when $n \ge 16$ and $p \le 0.10$ |
| 3 | AOQ | $AOQ = p \, P_{a} \, (N-n)/N$ | Approx $\approx p \, P_{a}$ for $N \gg n$ |
| 4 | ATI | $ATI = n P_{a} + N(1-P_{a})$ | Average items inspected per lot |
| 5 | Double Plan — Pₐ | $P_{a}=P_{1}+P_{2}$ | With $P_{2}$ as the double sum above |
| 6 | Double Plan — ASN | $ASN = n_{1} + n_{2} \cdot P_{2}'$ | $P_{2}'=P(c_{1}<d_{1}\le c_{2})$ |
| 7 | Multiple Plan — ASN | $ASN = \sum_{i} n_{i} P(\text{continue at stage }i-1)$ | Generalised recursion |
| 8 | SPRT — A constant | $A = \beta/(1-\alpha)$ | Acceptance threshold constant |
| 9 | SPRT — B constant | $B = (1-\beta)/\alpha$ | Rejection threshold constant |
| 10 | SPRT — slope | $s = \ln(p_{1}/p_{0}) \,/\, \ln[p_{1}(1-p_{0})/(p_{0}(1-p_{1}))]$ | $0 < s < 1$ |
| 11 | SPRT — accept intercept | $h_{0} = \ln B \,/\, \ln[p_{1}(1-p_{0})/(p_{0}(1-p_{1}))]$ | Usually negative |
| 12 | SPRT — reject intercept | $h_{1} = \ln A \,/\, \ln[p_{1}(1-p_{0})/(p_{0}(1-p_{1}))]$ | Usually positive |
| 13 | SPRT — decision | $d \le h_{0}+sn$ Accept; $d \ge h_{1}+sn$ Reject | Otherwise continue |
| 14 | SPRT — ASN at p₀ | $ASN_{p_0} = [(1-\alpha)\ln B - \alpha \ln A] \,/\, [p_0 \ln(p_0/p_1) + (1-p_0)\ln((1-p_0)/(1-p_1))]$ |  |
| 15 | SPRT — ASN at p₁ | $ASN_{p_1} = [\beta \ln B - (1-\beta)\ln A] \,/\, [p_1 \ln(p_0/p_1) + (1-p_1)\ln((1-p_0)/(1-p_1))]$ |  |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Acceptance sampling plans are the statistical backbone of **incoming quality control** in automotive (IATF 16949), pharmaceuticals (FDA 21 CFR 820), electronics (IPC-A-610), and defence supply chains (MIL-STD-1916). Sequential plans are dominant in *destructive testing* (e.g., tensile strength of ammunition, fatigue life of bearings) where each item tested is lost forever, so minimising ASN saves both money and material.
