These four plans together form the **backbone of every industrial acceptance sampling decision** in automotive (TS 16949), defense (MIL-STD), pharmaceutical (FDA), and electronics manufacturing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MIL-STD 105E — Anatomy of the Standard

MIL-STD 105E is a **single, double, and multiple sampling system** designed for **inspection by attributes**. The standard uses three critical inputs:

#### Step-by-Step Logic of MIL-STD 105E

1. **Determine the Lot Size (N)** — Number of units in the incoming lot.
2. **Select the Inspection Level** — General Levels **I, II, III** (Level II is default). Special Levels **S-1 to S-4** are used for destructive or expensive tests.
3. **Determine the Sample Size Code Letter** — Cross-reference *Lot Size* with *Inspection Level* in **Table I** of MIL-STD 105E → yields a code letter (A → R).
4. **Choose the AQL Value** — Specified on the contract or by company policy (common values: 0.065, 0.10, 0.15, 0.25, 0.40, 0.65, 1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 4.0).
5. **Determine Plan Type** — Single, Double, or Multiple. Standard practice starts with **Single Sampling**.
6. **Choose Severity** — **Normal**, **Tightened**, or **Reduced** inspection based on the switching rules.
7. **Look up the Sampling Plan** — Use Tables II-A (Normal), II-B (Tightened), or II-C (Reduced) → yields sample size `n` and acceptance number `Ac` (and rejection number `Re`).

#### The Four Switching Rules of MIL-STD 105E

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Critical — Switching Rules Sequence:**
> 1. **Normal → Tightened:** When **2 out of 5 consecutive lots** have been *rejected* on original inspection.
> 2. **Tightened → Normal:** When **5 consecutive lots** have been *accepted* under tightened inspection.
> 3. **Normal → Reduced:** When **10 consecutive lots** have been *accepted* under normal inspection AND production is at a steady state AND the cumulative defect count is below a prescribed limit.
> 4. **Tightened → Discontinued:** When **10 consecutive lots** remain under tightened inspection → supplier loses approval for that AQL.

#### Operating Ratio (OR)

The **Operating Ratio** quantifies the relative steepness of an OC curve. A lower OR = steeper curve = better discrimination.

$$
\text{Operating Ratio (OR)} = \frac{\text{AQL}}{\text{LTPD}}
$$

For a well-designed MIL-STD 105E plan, OR typically lies between **1.5 and 4.0**.

---

### 2.2 Dodge–Romig Sampling Tables

Developed by H.F. Dodge and H.G. Romig in 1941, these are **rectifying inspection plans** for **continuous production lots**. They assume:
- Lots are essentially infinite.
- Defective items found are **replaced** with good ones.
- A **process average** is known.

**Two Parallel Plan Sets:**

| Plan Type | Optimization Criterion | Consumer's Risk β | Producer's Risk α |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **AOQL Plan** | Minimize ATI (Average Total Inspection) | Variable (≈ 0.10) | Variable |
| **LTPD Plan** | Minimize ATI for a given LTPD | **Fixed at 0.10** | Variable |

#### Rectifying Inspection Logic

$$
\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a) \cdot (N - n)
$$

where $P_a$ is the probability of acceptance at a given incoming quality $p$. ATI is the *expected* number of items inspected — Dodge–Romig tables select `n` and `c` that **minimize ATI** for a given $p$ (process average).

---

### 2.3 ABC Standards (Department of Defense)

The **ABC Standards** (AQL Tables) classify attribute inspection into three quality categories:

> [!NOTE]
> **ABC Severity Levels:**
> - **A Level** — *Critical Defects* (safety, hazardous) — AQL = **0.10%**
> - **B Level** — *Major Defects* (functional failure, scrap) — AQL = **1.50%**
> - **C Level** — *Minor Defects* (cosmetic, appearance) — AQL = **2.50% to 4.00%**

These AQL values are *combined* with MIL-STD 105E sample size code letters to form complete inspection plans. KTU frequently tests this **defect-classification** concept.

---

### 2.4 AOQL Plans — The "Limit-on-Defectives" Philosophy

The **AOQL** is the maximum average percentage of defectives that will appear in *all accepted lots plus all rejected-and-100%-inspected lots*.

Mathematically:

$$
\text{AOQL} = \max_{p} \left[ p \cdot P_a(p) \right]
$$

In a Dodge–Romig AOQL plan, the acceptance number $c$ is selected so that the **maximum** of the function $p \cdot P_a(p)$ equals the **pre-specified AOQL** for the given process average.

---

### 2.5 LTPD Plans — The "Consumer-Protection" Philosophy

The **LTPD** is the quality level at which the probability of acceptance equals the **consumer's risk $\beta$**:

$$
P_a(\text{LTPD}) = \beta = 0.10
$$

For a single sampling plan:

$$
n = \frac{\chi^2_{(2c+2,\ 1-\beta) \cdot 100}}{2 \cdot \text{LTPD}}
$$

where $\chi^2$ is the chi-square critical value. The LTPD plan protects the *consumer* — even with a bad lot, only 10% chance the bad lot slips through.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Expression | Use / Meaning |
|---------|------------|---------------|
| **Hypergeometric** (Lot < 5N) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{D}{d} \binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | Exact probability of acceptance |
| **Binomial Approx.** (Lot > 5N, $p \leq 0.10$) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Most common KTU exam choice |
| **Poisson Approx.** ($n \geq 50$, $p \leq 0.10$) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{(np)^d e^{-np}}{d!}$ | Easy lookup in K-tables |
| **ATI (Rectifying)** | $\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$ | Dodge–Romig optimization target |
| **AOQL** | $\text{AOQL} = \max_p [p \cdot P_a(p)]$ | Worst-case outgoing quality |
| **LTPD Definition** | $P_a(\text{LTPD}) = 0.10$ | Consumer's risk fixed |
| **LTPD Sample Size** | $n = \dfrac{\chi^2_{(2c+2,\ 0.90)}}{2 \cdot p_1}$ | Where $p_1$ = LTPD |
| **Operating Ratio** | $\text{OR} = \dfrac{\text{AQL}}{\text{LTPD}}$ | Curve steepness indicator |
| **Producer's Risk** | $P_a(\text{AQL}) = 1 - \alpha$ | α typically = 0.05 |
| **Process Average** | $\bar{p} = \dfrac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}}$ | Used to switch to reduced inspection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** In exam calculations, if $n/N \leq 0.10$, always use **Binomial**; if $n \cdot p \leq 5$, use **Poisson** for easier computation using K-table values.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Standard Used | Application |
|----------|--------------|-------------|
| Automotive | ISO 2859-1 (equivalence of MIL-STD 105E) | Incoming parts acceptance |
| Pharmaceuticals | FDA-mandated sampling | Tablet, vial, ampoule testing |
| Electronics | LTPD-based plans | PCB lot acceptance |
| Defense | MIL-STD 105E + ABC Standards | Ammunition, electronics inspection |
| Textiles | AOQL Dodge–Romig | Continuous fabric production |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Probability of Acceptance under Binomial Model

Starting from the Hypergeometric distribution and applying the Binomial approximation when $n/N \leq 0.10$:

$$
P_a = \frac{\binom{N}{pN}\binom{N(1-p)}{n - pN}}{\binom{N}{n}}
$$

Step 1: When the lot is large and sampling fraction is small, the *without-replacement* effect becomes negligible. We approximate $N - D \approx N$ and $N - n \approx N$.

$$
P_a \approx \binom{n}{d} \left(\frac{D}{N}\right)^{d} \left(1 - \frac{D}{N}\right)^{n-d}
$$

Step 2: Recognizing that $D/N = p$ (true fraction defective), and summing over all $d$ from $0$ to $c$ (acceptance number):

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

This is the **Binomial CDF** at $d = c$. The KTU board expects you to **write the summation explicitly** rather than just cite the formula.

Step 3: If $n \cdot p \leq 5$, we further approximate using the Poisson limit:

$$
P_a \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}
$$

This form lets you directly read $P_a$ from a **K-table** (Poisson CDF) for the parameter $m = np$ and the acceptance number $c$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Total Inspection (ATI) for Rectifying Plans

When a lot of size $N$ is sampled and the rejected lots are **100% inspected with defectives replaced**, the expected number of inspected items per lot is:

Step 1: With probability $P_a$, the lot is accepted after inspecting only $n$ items.

$$
\text{Inspections in Accepted Lot} = n
$$

Step 2: With probability $(1 - P_a)$, the lot is rejected and undergoes full rectifying inspection. The expected extra inspections in a rejected lot is $(N - n)$, since the $n$ items are already inspected.

$$
\text{Inspections in Rejected Lot} = n + (N - n) = N
$$

Step 3: Total expected inspections per lot (ATI):

$$
\text{ATI} = P_a \cdot n + (1 - P_a) \cdot N
$$

Step 4: Simplifying by isolating the common term $n$:

$$
\text{ATI} = n \left[ P_a - 1 \right] + N (1 - P_a) + n
$$

Step 5: Final compact form:

$$
\boxed{\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)}
$$

This equation is the **objective function** that Dodge–Romig tables minimize. KTU's Part B questions frequently test the student's ability to compute ATI at a given $p$ and identify the optimal `c`.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LTPD Sample Size from Chi-Square

For a single sampling plan with acceptance number $c$, the probability of acceptance at defect rate $p$ using Poisson approximation is:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}
$$

Setting $P_a = 0.10$ at $p = p_1$ (LTPD), the chi-square relationship is:

$$
2 n p_1 = \chi^2_{(2c+2,\ 0.90)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{n = \frac{\chi^2_{(2c+2,\ 0.90)}}{2 p_1}}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU Style):** Find $n$ for LTPD = 0.02, $c = 1$.

Degrees of freedom $= 2(1) + 2 = 4$, probability = 0.90.

From chi-square tables: $\chi^2_{(4,\ 0.90)} = 7.779$.

$$
n = \frac{7.779}{2 \times 0.02} = \frac{7.779}{0.04} = 194.475
$$

Since sample size must be an integer, **round up to 195**. This is the standard KTU-style LTPD problem.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — AOQL Computation from Tabular $P_a$

Given a single sampling plan $(n, c)$, AOQL is found by:

Step 1: Compute $p \cdot P_a(p)$ for several values of $p$.

Step 2: Find the value of $p$ that **maximizes** $p \cdot P_a(p)$.

Step 3: That maximum value is AOQL.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A plan has $n = 75$, $c = 1$, lot $N = 1000$. Compute AOQL.

| $p$ | $np$ | $P_a$ (from Poisson K-table, $c=1$) | $p \cdot P_a$ |
|-----|------|--------------------------------------|----------------|
| 0.005 | 0.375 | 0.9112 | 0.004556 |
| 0.010 | 0.750 | 0.8266 | 0.008266 |
| 0.015 | 1.125 | 0.7524 | 0.011286 |
| 0.020 | 1.500 | 0.6861 | 0.013722 |
| 0.025 | 1.875 | 0.6274 | 0.015685 |
| 0.030 | 2.250 | 0.5750 | 0.017250 |
| 0.035 | 2.625 | 0.5283 | 0.018491 ← **MAX** |
| 0.040 | 3.000 | 0.4866 | 0.019464 |
| 0.050 | 3.750 | 0.4135 | 0.020675 ← Wait, re-evaluate |

Recomputing carefully (using exact Poisson K-values for $m=np$ and $c=1$, $P_a = e^{-m}(1+m)$):

| $p$ | $m$ | $P_a = e^{-m}(1+m)$ | $p \cdot P_a$ |
|-----|-----|---------------------|----------------|
| 0.005 | 0.375 | 0.9112 | 0.004556 |
| 0.010 | 0.750 | 0.8266 | 0.008266 |
| 0.015 | 1.125 | 0.7524 | 0.011286 |
| 0.020 | 1.500 | 0.6861 | 0.013722 |
| 0.025 | 1.875 | 0.6274 | 0.015685 |
| 0.030 | 2.250 | 0.5750 | 0.017250 |
| 0.035 | 2.625 | 0.5283 | 0.018491 |
| 0.040 | 3.000 | 0.4866 | 0.019464 |
| 0.050 | 3.750 | 0.4135 | 0.020675 |
| 0.060 | 4.500 | 0.3528 | 0.021168 |
| 0.070 | 5.250 | 0.3020 | 0.021140 |
| 0.080 | 6.000 | 0.2593 | 0.020744 |
| 0.090 | 6.750 | 0.2232 | 0.020088 |
| 0.100 | 7.500 | 0.1927 | 0.019270 |

Maximum $p \cdot P_a$ occurs near $p \approx 0.060$ to $0.070$.

$$
\boxed{\text{AOQL} \approx 0.0212 \text{ or } 2.12\%}
$$

This is the **classical Dodge–Romig AOQL calculation** pattern that KTU questions demand.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Four Plans

| Parameter | MIL-STD 105E | Dodge–Romig AOQL | Dodge–Romig LTPD | ABC Standard |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Basis** | AQL | AOQL | LTPD | Defect Class |
| **Sampling Type** | Single/Double/Multiple | Single | Single | Single |
| **Inspection Mode** | Attributes | Attributes (Rectifying) | Attributes (Rectifying) | Attributes |
| **Switching Rules** | Yes (4 rules) | No | No | No |
| **Consumer's Risk (β)** | Variable | ≈ 0.10 | Fixed at 0.10 | N/A |
| **Producer's Risk (α)** | ≈ 0.05 | Variable | Variable | N/A |
| **Lot Size** | Finite | Infinite (assumed) | Infinite (assumed) | Finite |
| **KTU Frequency** | Very High | High | High | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MIL-STD 105E — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the sequential decision logic of MIL-STD 105E, from lot arrival to final disposition with all four switching rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lotin[LOT ARRIVES Size N] --> lvlsel{SELECT INSPECTION LEVEL}
    lvlsel --> codeletter[TABLE I LOOKUP Code Letter A to R]
    codeletter --> aqlpick{PICK AQL VALUE}
    aqlpick --> severity{CURRENT SEVERITY}
    severity --> nrm[Normal Inspection Table II A]
    severity --> tgh[Tightened Inspection Table II B]
    severity --> rdc[Reduced Inspection Table II C]
    nrm --> ttype{Pick Plan Type}
    ttype --> single[Single Sampling n Ac Re]
    ttype --> double[Double Sampling n1 n2 c1 c2]
    ttype --> multi[Multiple Sampling]
    single --> sample1[Draw Random Sample n]
    double --> sample1
    multi --> sample1
    sample1 --> dcount{Count Defects d}
    dcount --> acpt[d less or equal Ac ACCEPT LOT]
    dcount --> rjct[d greater than Ac REJECT LOT]
    acpt --> sw1{2 of last 5 REJECTED}
    rjct --> sw2{5 consecutive REJECTED}
    sw1 --> yes1[Switch to TIGHTENED]
    sw1 --> no1[Stay on NORMAL]
    sw2 --> yes2[Submit to 100 percent inspection]
    sw2 --> no2[Stay on TIGHTENED]
    yes1 --> log[Update Switching Counter]
    no1 --> log
    yes2 --> log
    no2 --> log
    rdc --> redcrit{10 consecutive accepted AND quality limit met}
    redcrit --> redok[Continue REDUCED]
    redcrit --> rednrm[Return to NORMAL]
    log --> end1[End of Lot Processing]
    redok --> end1
    rednrm --> end1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every node in the Mermaid block uses pure alphanumeric IDs (e.g., `nrm`, `tgh`, `rdc`, `acpt`). The arrows trace both the *inspection path* (downward) and the *switching path* (rightward). KTU students should be able to redraw this from memory for a 14-mark question.

---

### 4.2 Dodge–Romig Plan Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start LTPD or AOQL Decision] --> obj{WHICH OBJECTIVE}
    obj --> aoqlpath[Minimize ATI for fixed AOQL]
    obj --> ltpdpath[Minimize ATI for fixed LTPD with beta equals 0.10]
    aoqlpath --> know1{Process Average p known}
    ltpdpath --> know2{LTPD and process average known}
    know1 --> look1[Look up Dodge Romig AOQL Table for given p and lot size]
    look1 --> result1[Output Sample Size n and Acceptance Number c]
    know2 --> look2[Look up Dodge Romig LTPD Table for given p and lot size]
    look2 --> result2[Output Sample Size n and Acceptance Number c]
    result1 --> atichk[Compute ATI and verify minimum]
    result2 --> atichk
    atichk --> apply[Apply Rectifying Inspection Replace defectives with good ones]
    apply --> done[End Procedure]
```

---

### 4.3 OC Curve Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SAMPLING_A[PLAN CHARACTERISTICS]
        paxis[Incoming Quality p] --> oc[OC CURVE P of a vs p]
        oc --> slope{SLOPE STEEPNESS}
    end
    slope --> aoql[STEEP AOQL PLAN]
    slope --> ltpds[STEEPER LTPD PLAN]
    slope --> m105[MIL STD 105E MODERATE SLOPE]
    aoql --> akey[Pa at AQL equals 0.95]
    ltpds --> lkey[Pa at LTPD equals 0.10]
    m105 --> mkey[Pa at AQL equals 0.95 and Pa at LTPD equals 0.10]
```

---

### 4.4 ABC Standard Severity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    def[DEFECT DETECTED] --> crit{CRITICALITY}
    crit --> a[A LEVEL Safety hazard loss of life]
    crit --> b[B LEVEL Functional failure scrap cost]
    crit --> c[C LEVEL Cosmetic appearance minor rework]
    a --> aqlA[AQL 0.10 percent]
    b --> aqlB[AQL 1.50 percent]
    c --> aqlC[AQL 2.50 to 4.00 percent]
    aqlA --> planA[Apply MIL STD 105E Code Letter with AQL 0.10]
    aqlB --> planB[Apply MIL STD 105E Code Letter with AQL 1.50]
    aqlC --> planC[Apply MIL STD 105E Code Letter with AQL 2.50]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)" and state the switching rules of MIL-STD 105E in the order they are applied.**

**Model Answer:**

> **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** AQL is the maximum percentage of defective items that, for purposes of acceptance sampling, can be considered *satisfactory* as a process average. It is the defect level at which the probability of acceptance equals $1 - \alpha = 0.95$ in a MIL-STD 105E plan.
>
> **Switching Rules (in order of application during normal inspection):**
> 1. **Normal → Reduced:** When 10 consecutive lots are accepted under Normal inspection AND cumulative defect count is below the limit AND production is steady.
> 2. **Normal → Tightened:** When 2 out of 5 consecutive lots are rejected under Normal inspection.
> 3. **Tightened → Normal:** When 5 consecutive lots are accepted under Tightened inspection.
> 4. **Tightened → Discontinued:** When 10 consecutive lots remain under Tightened inspection without going to Normal.

*(3 Marks – 1 for AQL definition, 2 for four switching rules.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between AOQL and LTPD sampling plans. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AOQL Plan | LTPD Plan |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Optimization target | Minimize ATI for a given **AOQL value** | Minimize ATI for a given **LTPD** with $\beta = 0.10$ |
| Consumer's risk β | Variable (≈ 0.10) | **Fixed at 0.10** |
| Lot protection | Limits the *average* outgoing defectives | Limits the *worst* lot quality accepted |
| Application | Continuous production of bolts, fabric, sheets | End-item acceptance of expensive electronic assemblies |

*(3 Marks – 2 for distinction, 1 for application.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* A manufacturer of automotive components uses **MIL-STD 105E** for incoming inspection. The lot size is $N = 3000$ and the **AQL = 0.10%**. Using inspection level II and Normal inspection (Single Sampling), determine:
1. The sample size code letter.
2. The sample size $n$ and acceptance number $c$.
3. The probability of acceptance at $p = 0.005$ using the Poisson approximation.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **four switching rules of MIL-STD 105E** with a clear flowchart description. State what happens if 10 consecutive lots are rejected under tightened inspection.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1:** Lot size $N = 3000$ falls in the range **2801 – 5000** in MIL-STD 105E Table I.

**Step 2:** For Inspection Level II and lot size 2801–5000, the code letter is **"K"**.

**Step 3:** Looking up Table II-A (Normal, Single) for code letter **K** and **AQL = 0.10%** (use AQL line 0.10):

From standard MIL-STD 105E Table II-A: For code letter K, sample size = 125, Ac = 0, Re = 1 (since 0.10% falls in the arrow region that points to the 0.10 AQL column for code K).

> Verification with standard table: Code K, AQL 0.10 → **n = 125, c = 0 (downward arrow to 0.065 region)**. For AQL = 0.10 exactly, we use **n = 125, Ac = 1** at the boundary.
>
> For a clean solution (commonly tested), take **n = 125, c = 1** (this gives AQL ≈ 0.10% at $P_a = 0.95$).

**Step 4:** Compute $P_a$ at $p = 0.005$ using Poisson approximation:

$$
np = 125 \times 0.005 = 0.625
$$

From the Poisson CDF (K-table), for $m = 0.625$ and $c = 1$:

$$
P_a = e^{-0.625} (1 + 0.625)
$$

$$
P_a = 0.5353 \times 1.625 = 0.8698
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a \approx 0.870 \text{ or } 87.0\%}
$$

*(Marks distribution: Step 1 → 1, Step 2 → 1, Step 3 → 2, Step 4 → 3 = 7 marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

The four switching rules of MIL-STD 105E are:

**Rule 1: Normal → Tightened Inspection.**
When **2 out of any 5 consecutive lots** have been rejected during original Normal inspection (i.e., without re-inspection of rejected lots), the procedure switches to Tightened inspection for the next lot. This rule protects the *consumer* from a deteriorating process.

**Rule 2: Tightened → Normal Inspection.**
When **5 consecutive lots** have been accepted under Tightened inspection, the procedure reverts to Normal inspection. This is a "reward" for consistent quality.

**Rule 3: Normal → Reduced Inspection.**
When **10 consecutive lots** are accepted under Normal inspection, **and** the cumulative total defectives is less than the *reduced inspection limit* (specified in Table III of MIL-STD 105E), **and** production is in a stable state, the inspector may switch to Reduced inspection. This is a reward for sustained quality.

**Rule 4: Tightened → Discontinue Inspection.**
When **10 consecutive lots** remain under Tightened inspection (i.e., the supplier cannot return to Normal), the **acceptance procedure is discontinued**. The supplier loses approval for that AQL class, and re-submission requires a new quality audit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 1:** Students frequently write "Normal → Reduced" as the *first* switching rule. The *correct chronological order* during the lifecycle of a supplier is **Normal → Reduced** (quality is good) → **Normal → Tightened** (quality drops) → **Tightened → Normal** (recovers) → **Tightened → Discontinued** (fails to recover). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for wrong ordering.
>
> **Pitfall 2:** Students often miss the *cumulative defect count* condition for Normal → Reduced. Writing only "10 consecutive acceptances" is incomplete; the defect-count condition must also be stated.

*(Marks distribution: 4 rules × 1.5 marks = 6 marks + 1 mark for the discontinuation consequence = 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* A continuous production process has **process average = 0.40%** and a **lot size N = 1500**. Using the **Dodge–Romig AOQL plan**, determine:
1. The optimal sample size $n$ and acceptance number $c$.
2. The corresponding AOQL value.
3. The Average Total Inspection (ATI) at the process average.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A consumer requires an **LTPD = 0.50%** with **consumer's risk β = 0.10** for an end-item acceptance plan. If the acceptance number is **c = 2**, determine the **sample size n** using the chi-square relationship. Verify your answer using the Poisson approximation at $p = \text{LTPD}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Enter Dodge–Romig AOQL Tables.**

For Process Average $\bar{p} = 0.40\% = 0.004$ and Lot Size Range 1001–2000, the Dodge–Romig AOQL Master Table (McGraw-Hill / standard reference) gives:

$$
n = 90, \quad c = 1
$$

This is the **minimum-ATI plan** for the given conditions.

**Step 2: Compute the AOQL for the plan (n = 90, c = 1).**

The AOQL is the maximum value of $p \cdot P_a(p)$ for $0 \leq p \leq 1$.

Using Poisson approximation $m = np$:

$$
P_a(p) = e^{-np}(1 + np)
$$

Tabulate $p \cdot P_a$:

| $p$ | $np$ | $P_a = e^{-m}(1+m)$ | $p \cdot P_a$ |
|-----|------|----------------------|----------------|
| 0.001 | 0.090 | 0.9853 | 0.000985 |
| 0.005 | 0.450 | 0.8998 | 0.004499 |
| 0.010 | 0.900 | 0.8127 | 0.008127 |
| 0.015 | 1.350 | 0.7373 | 0.011060 |
| 0.020 | 1.800 | 0.6703 | 0.013406 |
| 0.025 | 2.250 | 0.6102 | 0.015255 |
| 0.030 | 2.700 | 0.5565 | 0.016695 ← Peak region |
| 0.040 | 3.600 | 0.4647 | 0.018588 |
| 0.050 | 4.500 | 0.3890 | 0.019450 ← **MAX** |
| 0.060 | 5.400 | 0.3270 | 0.019620 ← **MAX** |
| 0.080 | 7.200 | 0.2352 | 0.018816 |
| 0.100 | 9.000 | 0.1720 | 0.017200 |

Maximum occurs near $p \approx 0.060$, giving:

$$
\boxed{\text{AOQL} \approx 0.0196 \text{ or } 1.96\%}
$$

**Step 3: Compute ATI at $\bar{p} = 0.004$.**

$$
np = 90 \times 0.004 = 0.360
$$

From Poisson K-table for $m = 0.36$, $c = 1$:

$$
P_a = e^{-0.36}(1 + 0.36) = 0.6977 \times 1.36 = 0.9489
$$

Now compute ATI:

$$
\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n) = 90 + (1 - 0.9489)(1500 - 90)
$$

$$
\text{ATI} = 90 + (0.0511)(1410) = 90 + 72.05 = 162.05
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ATI} \approx 162 \text{ units}}
$$

*(Marks distribution: Step 1 → 2, Step 2 → 3, Step 3 → 2 = 7 marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Use the Chi-Square Relationship.**

For LTPD plan with $c = 2$ and $P_a(\text{LTPD}) = 0.10$:

$$
n = \frac{\chi^2_{(2c+2,\ 0.90)}}{2 \cdot p_1}
$$

Degrees of freedom $= 2(2) + 2 = 6$.

From chi-square table: $\chi^2_{(6,\ 0.90)} = 10.645$.

$$
n = \frac{10.645}{2 \times 0.005} = \frac{10.645}{0.010} = 1064.5
$$

Round up to nearest integer:

$$
\boxed{n = 1065 \text{ units}}
$$

**Step 2: Verify Using Poisson Approximation at $p = 0.005$.**

$$
np = 1065 \times 0.005 = 5.325
$$

For $c = 2$:

$$
P_a = e^{-5.325} \left(1 + 5.325 + \frac{5.325^2}{2}\right)
$$

$$
P_a = e^{-5.325} (1 + 5.325 + 14.178) = 0.00488 \times 20.503
$$

$$
P_a = 0.1001 \approx 0.10
$$

This confirms $P_a(\text{LTPD}) = 0.10$, validating the chi-square computation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 1:** Many students use the **wrong** degrees of freedom, writing $2c$ instead of $2c+2$. The correct formula is $\chi^2_{(2c+2,\ 0.90)}$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> **Pitfall 2:** Students often forget to **round up** $n$ to the next integer. Rounding down violates the consumer's risk protection.
>
> **Pitfall 3:** The verification step is **mandatory** for full marks in KTU. Skipping it costs 1 mark.

*(Marks distribution: Step 1 → 4, Step 2 → 3 = 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3:**
>
> - **MIL-STD 105E** uses **AQL** as the key parameter. AQL is *not* an exact defect limit — it is the *level acceptable* on average.
> - The **four switching rules** are: Normal↔Reduced, Normal↔Tightened, Tightened→Normal, and Tightened→Discontinue. Memorize the **triggers** (2/5, 5, 10, 10).
> - **Inspection Levels** in MIL-STD 105E: I (looser), II (default), III (stricter), S-1 to S-4 (special/destructive).
> - **Dodge–Romig AOQL** minimizes ATI for a fixed **average outgoing quality**; assumes *rectifying inspection* (replace defectives with good ones).
> - **Dodge–Romig LTPD** minimizes ATI for a fixed **consumer's risk** $\beta = 0.10$ per lot.
> - **ATI formula:** $\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$ — applies to *both* AOQL and LTPD plans.
> - **ABC Standards** classify defects as **A (Critical, 0.10%)**, **B (Major, 1.50%)**, **C (Minor, 2.50–4.00%)**.
> - **LTPD Sample Size:** Use chi-square with $\nu = 2c+2$ df at probability **0.90** (NOT 0.95, which is for AQL).
> - **Poisson approximation** is valid when $n \cdot p \leq 5$; use **Binomial** when $n/N \leq 0.10$ but $np > 5$.
> - **Operating Ratio (OR) = AQL/LTPD**: smaller OR = steeper OC = better plan.
> - **Switching to Reduced** requires *three* simultaneous conditions: 10 consecutive acceptances + defect-count limit + steady production.
> - **LTPD = 0.10 probability of acceptance**; this is the *fixed* anchor — do not confuse with producer's risk.
> - **Producer's risk α = 0.05** at AQL means $P_a(\text{AQL}) = 0.95$.
> - The **AOQL value is always greater than the AQL** of the same plan — the plan *cannot* improve the worst-case outgoing quality below AOQL no matter what the process actually delivers.
> - For a single sampling plan, increasing `c` while keeping `n` constant **loosens** the plan (raises AQL and AOQL).
> - For a single sampling plan, increasing `n` while keeping `c` constant **tightens** the plan (lowers AQL and AOQL, but increases inspection cost).
> - **Double and multiple sampling** plans achieve the same OC curve as a single plan but with **lower average sample size** — frequently tested in KTU 14-mark questions.
> - MIL-STD 105E has been **withdrawn** by the U.S. DoD (2017) and replaced by **ISO 2859-1** for international use, but the table structures are mathematically identical.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis and Event Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis and Event Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a **top-down, deductive** analytical technique used in reliability and safety engineering to determine the causes of an undesired top event by representing the system logic through Boolean logic gates (AND, OR, NOT) and identifying combinations of basic events that can lead to the top event. It is governed by the international standard **IEC 61025** and was originally developed at Bell Laboratories in **1962** by H.A. Watson for the Minuteman missile launch control system.

A Fault Tree is a graphical model that displays the **causal relationships** between basic component failures (or human errors / environmental conditions) and an undesired top-level system state. Each level of the tree represents a logical combination of events at the next lower level, ultimately terminating at the **basic events** whose probabilities are known from field data, manufacturer data, or reliability handbooks.

### 1.2 Event Tree Analysis (ETA) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is a **bottom-up, inductive** analytical technique that begins with an **initiating event** and traces all possible subsequent success or failure pathways through various safety barriers or functional systems, culminating in a set of defined end-states (outcomes). ETA is widely used in Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) and is formalized in **IEC 62502**.

ETA maps the **time-ordered sequence** of events following an initiating event, branching at each decision point where a safety function either succeeds (S) or fails (F). Each branch is weighted by its conditional probability, allowing engineers to compute the frequency or probability of each end-state.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**FTA Analogy — "The Forensic Investigation":**
Imagine a detective arriving at the scene of a major factory shutdown (the **top event**). The detective asks, "How did this happen?" and works backward, asking for each cause, "What were the conditions for this to occur?" If the answer is "EITHER power failed OR the safety valve stuck," the detective places an **OR gate**. If the answer is "BOTH the alarm failed AND the operator was absent," the detective places an **AND gate**. The detective keeps drilling down until arriving at root-level events (e.g., a blown fuse, a tired operator) — these are the **basic events**. The complete tree is essentially a structured "why-because" map of system failure.

**ETA Analogy — "The Domino Cascade with Shields":**
Now imagine a fire breaks out in a chemical plant (the **initiating event**). The plant has three independent shields: a smoke detector, a sprinkler system, and a fire wall. At each shield, the question is asked: "Did this shield hold (Success) or fail (Failure)?" Starting from the left, the cascade produces a unique path through the event tree, ending in a final outcome such as "Safe Shutdown" or "Total Loss." ETA is essentially a forward-projection of consequences through sequential barriers.

### 1.4 Key Symbols & Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core FTA Symbols** (IEC 61025 Standardized):
> - **Top Event** (Rectangle) — The undesired event being analyzed.
> - **Intermediate Event** (Rectangle) — A logical sub-event that is the output of a gate.
> - **Basic Event** (Circle) — A component failure or human error with a known probability; the tree terminates here.
> - **AND Gate** (Flat D-shape) — Output occurs **only when ALL** inputs occur.
> - **OR Gate** (Concave shield-shape) — Output occurs if **ANY** input occurs.
> - **Inhibit Gate** (Oval) — Output occurs when the conditional event AND the single input both occur.
> - **Transfer Symbol** (Triangle) — Used to connect sub-trees to avoid clutter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core ETA Symbols:**
> - **Initiating Event** (Hexagon or Block) — The starting event that triggers the sequence.
> - **Branch Point** (Node) — A decision point where success (up-branch) or failure (down-branch) occurs.
> - **End-State** (Rectangle) — The final outcome of a path (e.g., Safe, Minor Damage, Major Damage).
> - **Path Probability** — The product of conditional probabilities along a single branch sequence.

### 1.5 Fundamental Comparison

| Dimension | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Top-down (deductive) | Bottom-up (inductive) |
| **Question Asked** | "How can this top event occur?" | "What happens after this initiating event?" |
| **Logic Used** | Boolean gates (AND / OR) | Branching with conditional probabilities |
| **Output** | Minimal Cut Sets, Top Event Probability | Sequence of end-states with frequencies |
| **Phase of System Lifecycle** | Used in design & reliability analysis | Used in operational safety & PSA studies |
| **Standard Reference** | IEC 61025 | IEC 62502 |
| **Typical Application** | Identify single-point failures, redundancy gaps | Quantify accident sequence frequencies |



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fault Tree Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define the Top Event.**
The undesired system failure state is precisely defined (e.g., "Loss of Power to Critical Load"). A clear, unambiguous definition is critical because the entire tree branches from this single event.

**Step 2 — Identify Immediate Causes (First Level).**
Determine the immediate conditions that can cause the top event. These are connected to the top event via an **OR gate** if any one cause is sufficient, or an **AND gate** if all are required.

**Step 3 — Recursive Decomposition.**
For each intermediate event, ask: "What are the immediate causes of THIS event?" Connect them with appropriate gates. Continue until the events cannot be practically broken down further — these become the **basic events**.

**Step 4 — Identify Cut Sets.**
A **Cut Set** is a combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the top event. For example, if basic events A, B, C must all occur for the top event, then $\{A, B, C\}$ is a cut set.

**Step 5 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets (MCS).**
A **Minimal Cut Set** is the smallest cut set such that removal of any one event from the set eliminates the failure path. MCS represent the **most efficient failure combinations** and are the key outputs of FTA. They are obtained by applying Boolean algebra laws:
- Absorption: $A + AB = A$
- Idempotent: $AA = A$
- Identity: $A \cdot 1 = A$

**Step 6 — Quantitative Evaluation.**
Assign failure probabilities $q_i$ to each basic event and compute the top event probability using the **rare-event approximation** (valid when $q_i \ll 1$):
$$P_{top} \approx \sum_{i} P(MCS_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(MCS_i \cap MCS_j) + \dots$$
When $q_i$ are very small, the intersections of MCSs become negligible, giving the simplified form:
$$P_{top} \approx \sum_{i} P(MCS_i)$$

### 2.2 Event Tree Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the Initiating Event.**
A credible internal or external event (e.g., pipe break, loss of offsite power, fire, operator error) is selected based on system knowledge and prior hazard analysis (HAZOP, FMEA).

**Step 2 — Identify the Safety Functions / Barriers.**
List the engineered safety features (ESFs) and operator actions that are designed to mitigate the initiating event. These are usually **time-sequenced** (e.g., detection → isolation → suppression → containment).

**Step 3 — Construct the Event Tree.**
At each branch point, divide the path into Success (S) and Failure (F). The number of end-states equals $2^n$ for $n$ safety functions.

**Step 4 — Assign Probabilities.**
For each safety function, assign a failure-on-demand probability $P(F_i)$ and a success probability $P(S_i) = 1 - P(F_i)$.

**Step 5 — Compute End-State Probabilities.**
For each path, multiply the conditional probabilities along the path. The product gives the path probability. The total probability over all paths must equal 1 (or $P(I)$ for an initiating event probability).

**Step 6 — Compute End-State Frequencies (if applicable).**
If the initiating event has an occurrence frequency $f_I$ (events/year) rather than a probability, multiply each path probability by $f_I$ to get the frequency of each end-state (events/year).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every formula required for KTU examination problems on FTA and ETA. All absolute-value bars are rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to comply with markdown table constraints.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AND Gate Probability | $P_{AND} = P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3 \dots P_n$ | Output occurs if **all** inputs occur |
| 2 | OR Gate Probability (Exact) | $P_{OR} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)$ | Output occurs if **any** input occurs |
| 3 | OR Gate Probability (Rare-Event Approx.) | $P_{OR} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Used when $P_i \ll 1$ |
| 4 | Series System Reliability | $R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All components must work |
| 5 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_S = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | At least one must work |
| 6 | Cut Set Probability | $P_{CS} = \prod_{j \in CS} q_j$ | Product of basic event failure probs |
| 7 | Top Event Probability (MCS Sum) | $P_{top} \approx \sum_{k=1}^{m} P(MCS_k)$ | Sum of MCS probabilities |
| 8 | Component Unavailability | $Q_i = 1 - R_i$ | Failure probability of component $i$ |
| 9 | Demand Failure Probability (ETA) | $P_{D,i} = \lambda_i \cdot T_i / 2$ | Avg. unavailability over interval $T_i$ |
| 10 | End-State Frequency | $f_{end} = f_I \cdot \prod_{i} P_i$ | Frequency = initiating freq × path prob |
| 11 | Birnbaum Importance (Basic) | $I_B(i) = \frac{\partial P_{top}}{\partial q_i}$ | Sensitivity of top event to component $i$ |
| 12 | Criticality Importance | $I_C(i) = \frac{q_i}{P_{top}} \cdot I_B(i)$ | Normalized Birnbaum importance |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Nuclear Power:** FTA is mandatory in NASA NPR 8715.3C and U.S. NRC regulatory guides for safety-critical systems. The Three Mile Island and Challenger investigations leveraged FTA principles to trace root causes.
- **Chemical & Petrochemical Industries:** ETA forms the backbone of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** per ISO 31000 and is used to design **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** per IEC 61511.
- **Automotive Functional Safety:** ISO 26262 mandates FTA at the system and software levels to demonstrate that automotive E/E systems meet ASIL requirements.
- **Software Reliability:** FTA is increasingly applied to software architectures (e.g., microservices failure propagation) and DevOps pipeline failures.
- **Medical Devices:** FDA submissions for Class III medical devices typically require FTA to demonstrate single-fault safety.

### 2.5 Cut Set Reduction — Worked Boolean Algebra

A typical KTU question presents a fault tree and asks for minimal cut sets. The method proceeds as follows:

Suppose the top event $T$ in Boolean form is:
$$T = AB + AC + BC$$

Applying the **distributive law** and the **consensus theorem** ($AB + \bar{A}C + BC = AB + \bar{A}C$):
$$T = A(B + C) + BC$$

But $BC$ is the **consensus** of $AB$ and $AC$ and is redundant. The minimal form is:
$$T = AB + AC = A(B + C)$$

Thus the minimal cut sets are $\{A, B\}$ and $\{A, C\}$.



<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series System Unavailability (Top Event Probability for a Series Reliability Block)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A system consists of three components $X$, $Y$, $Z$ connected in series (i.e., the system functions **only if all three components function**). Given the failure probabilities $q_X = 0.02$, $q_Y = 0.03$, $q_Z = 0.05$, determine the system unreliability (top event probability) using FTA with an AND gate.

**Step 1 — Identify the Top Event in Reliability Terms.**
For a series system, the top event "System Failure" occurs if **any** component fails. In FTA terms, the top event is the **OR** of the individual component failure events $X_f$, $Y_f$, $Z_f$:
$$T = X_f + Y_f + Z_f$$

**Step 2 — Define Component Reliabilities.**
The reliability of each component is the complement of its failure probability:
$$R_X = 1 - q_X = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$$
$$R_Y = 1 - q_Y = 1 - 0.03 = 0.97$$
$$R_Z = 1 - q_Z = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$$

**Step 3 — Compute System Reliability (Success Probability).**
For a series system, all components must succeed:
$$R_{sys} = R_X \cdot R_Y \cdot R_Z$$

Substituting numerical values:
$$R_{sys} = 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.95$$

**Step 4 — Step-by-Step Multiplication.**
$$R_{sys} = 0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9506$$
$$R_{sys} = 0.9506 \times 0.95 = 0.90307$$

**Step 5 — Convert to System Unreliability (Top Event Probability).**
$$Q_{sys} = P(T) = 1 - R_{sys} = 1 - 0.90307 = 0.09693$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
The probability of system failure is approximately **9.69%**, which is greater than the sum of individual failure probabilities ($0.02 + 0.03 + 0.05 = 0.10$) — wait, this is actually slightly *less* than 0.10. Let us verify: for an OR gate with rare events, the **exact** formula is:
$$P_{OR} = 1 - (1 - q_X)(1 - q_Y)(1 - q_Z) = 1 - 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 = 0.09693$$
This matches. The rare-event approximation $0.02 + 0.03 + 0.05 = 0.10$ overestimates by about 3.2% relative error, confirming that the exact formula must be used unless $q_i$ is very small (< 0.001).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel Redundant System (OR Gate) Minimal Cut Sets

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A critical load is powered by two parallel UPS units, $U_1$ and $U_2$, each with failure probability $q_1 = q_2 = 0.01$. Each UPS has a manual bypass switch with failure probability $q_{S1} = q_{S2} = 0.005$. The load fails only if power is lost from BOTH paths. Construct the FTA, identify MCS, and compute $P_{top}$.

**Step 1 — Define Top Event.**
$$T = \text{Load Power Failure}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Immediate Causes (First Level).**
Power is lost if **either** Path 1 fails **OR** Path 2 fails (an OR gate at the top level):
$$T = (\text{Path 1 Failure}) + (\text{Path 2 Failure})$$

**Step 3 — Decompose Each Path.**
Path 1 fails if UPS $U_1$ fails **AND** its bypass switch $S_1$ fails (an AND gate):
$$P_1 = U_1 \cap S_1$$
Similarly, $P_2 = U_2 \cap S_2$.

**Step 4 — Construct Full Boolean Expression.**
$$T = (U_1 \cdot S_1) + (U_2 \cdot S_2)$$

**Step 5 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets.**
Applying absorption and idempotent laws, the expression is already in minimal form. The MCS are:
$$MCS_1 = \{U_1, S_1\}, \quad MCS_2 = \{U_2, S_2\}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Top Event Probability (Exact Form).**
$$P_{top} = 1 - (1 - q_1 q_{S1})(1 - q_2 q_{S2})$$

Substituting values:
$$q_1 q_{S1} = 0.01 \times 0.005 = 0.00005$$
$$q_2 q_{S2} = 0.01 \times 0.005 = 0.00005$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Exact OR Formula.**
$$P_{top} = 1 - (1 - 0.00005)(1 - 0.00005) = 1 - (0.99995)^2$$
$$(0.99995)^2 = 0.99995 \times 0.99995 = 0.9999000025$$
$$P_{top} = 1 - 0.9999000025 = 0.0000999975 \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 8 — Cross-Check with Rare-Event Approximation.**
Since $q_1 q_{S1} \ll 1$, the approximation is:
$$P_{top} \approx 0.00005 + 0.00005 = 0.0001$$

This matches the exact result to 5 decimal places, confirming the validity of the rare-event approximation for this scenario.

**Step 9 — Engineering Interpretation.**
The redundancy reduces the system failure probability from $0.01$ (single UPS) to $0.0001$ (two parallel UPS with bypass), a **100-fold improvement**. This is the principle of **defense-in-depth** in reliability engineering.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Event Tree End-State Probability (Fire Scenario)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A chemical reactor has an initiating event "Internal Fire" with annual frequency $f_I = 0.01$ per year. Three independent safety barriers exist: (1) Fire Detection (success probability $P_{D} = 0.95$), (2) Automatic Suppression (success given detection $P_{S \vert D} = 0.90$), (3) Containment Wall (success given suppression $P_{C \vert S} = 0.85$). Construct the ETA and compute all end-state frequencies.

**Step 1 — Identify the Initiating Event and Barriers.**
- Initiating Event (IE): Internal Fire, $f_I = 0.01$ /year
- Barrier 1: Detection, $P(D) = 0.95$, $P(\bar{D}) = 0.05$
- Barrier 2: Suppression (conditional on detection), $P(S \vert D) = 0.90$, $P(\bar{S} \vert D) = 0.10$
- Barrier 3: Containment (conditional on suppression), $P(C \vert S) = 0.85$, $P(\bar{C} \vert S) = 0.15$

**Step 2 — Construct the Event Tree with All Possible Paths.**
With 3 barriers, there are $2^3 = 8$ theoretical end-states, but only 4 are physically realizable (containment is only relevant if suppression succeeded, which requires detection).

**Step 3 — Define End-States.**
- **ES1: Safe Shutdown** — Detected, Suppressed, Contained.
- **ES2: Minor Damage** — Detected, Suppressed, Containment Failed.
- **ES3: Major Damage** — Detected, Suppression Failed.
- **ES4: Catastrophic** — Not Detected.

**Step 4 — Compute Conditional Path Probabilities.**

For **ES1** (Safe Shutdown):
$$P(ES1 \vert IE) = P(D) \cdot P(S \vert D) \cdot P(C \vert S) = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85$$
$$= 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$$
$$= 0.855 \times 0.85 = 0.72675$$

For **ES2** (Minor Damage):
$$P(ES2 \vert IE) = P(D) \cdot P(S \vert D) \cdot P(\bar{C} \vert S) = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.15$$
$$= 0.855 \times 0.15 = 0.12825$$

For **ES3** (Major Damage):
$$P(ES3 \vert IE) = P(D) \cdot P(\bar{S} \vert D) = 0.95 \times 0.10 = 0.095$$

For **ES4** (Catastrophic):
$$P(ES4 \vert IE) = P(\bar{D}) = 0.05$$

**Step 5 — Verify Completeness (Probabilities Sum to Unity).**
$$\sum P(ES_k \vert IE) = 0.72675 + 0.12825 + 0.095 + 0.05$$
$$= 0.85500 + 0.09500 + 0.05 = 1.000$$

The sum is **exactly 1**, confirming completeness of the event tree.

**Step 6 — Compute End-State Frequencies.**
Since the initiating event has an annual frequency $f_I$, each end-state frequency is:
$$f_{ESk} = f_I \cdot P(ES_k \vert IE)$$

- $f_{ES1} = 0.01 \times 0.72675 = 7.2675 \times 10^{-3}$ /year
- $f_{ES2} = 0.01 \times 0.12825 = 1.2825 \times 10^{-3}$ /year
- $f_{ES3} = 0.01 \times 0.09500 = 9.5000 \times 10^{-4}$ /year
- $f_{ES4} = 0.01 \times 0.05000 = 5.0000 \times 10^{-4}$ /year

**Step 7 — Compute Aggregate Damage Frequency.**
$$f_{damage} = f_{ES2} + f_{ES3} + f_{ES4} = (1.2825 + 0.9500 + 0.5000) \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 2.7325 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /year}$$

This corresponds to a return period of:
$$T_{damage} = 1 / f_{damage} \approx 366 \text{ years}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cut Set Order vs. Risk Priority

| **Cut Set Order** | **Number of Events** | **Risk Priority** | **Engineering Action** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Point (Order 1)** | 1 | **Highest** | Eliminate redundancy gaps; add backups |
| **Order 2** | 2 | **High** | Improve component reliability; add diagnostics |
| **Order 3** | 3 | **Medium** | Procedural controls; preventive maintenance |
| **Order 4+** | $\geq 4$ | **Lower** | Generally acceptable; document for audit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Examiner Insight:** Questions on FTA almost always ask for **minimal cut sets** and the **top event probability**. The order of the cut set determines its importance — order-1 (single-point failure) cut sets are the **most critical** because they involve no redundancy. Students who simply compute $P_{top}$ without identifying MCS lose significant marks.

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Common Cause Failure (CCF) Adjustment

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Two identical pumps in parallel each have independent failure rate $\lambda = 10^{-3}$ /hour. The **common cause failure** parameter is $\beta = 0.10$ (i.e., 10% of failures affect both pumps simultaneously). Compute the unavailability using the **Beta Factor Model**.

**Step 1 — Decompose Failure Rate into Independent and Common Components.**
$$\lambda_I = (1 - \beta) \lambda = 0.90 \times 10^{-3} = 0.9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /hour}$$
$$\lambda_C = \beta \lambda = 0.10 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /hour}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Independent Unavailability (Mission Time $T = 8760$ hours).**
$$Q_I = 1 - e^{-\lambda_I T} \approx \lambda_I T = 0.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 8760 = 7.884$$

This is greater than 1, which is **physically impossible**. Therefore, for long mission times, we must use the asymptotic unavailability:
$$Q_I \approx \frac{\lambda_I}{\mu_I}$$

But for an exam, if $\lambda T \ll 1$, we can use the linear approximation. Let us assume a shorter mission time $T = 100$ hours:
$$Q_I \approx 0.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.09$$

**Step 3 — Compute Common Cause Unavailability.**
$$Q_C \approx 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.01$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total System Unavailability.**
For a parallel redundant system with CCF:
$$Q_{sys} = Q_I^2 \cdot (1 - Q_C) + Q_C$$

Substituting:
$$Q_I^2 = (0.09)^2 = 0.0081$$
$$Q_I^2 \cdot (1 - Q_C) = 0.0081 \times 0.99 = 0.008019$$
$$Q_{sys} = 0.008019 + 0.01 = 0.018019$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Neglecting CCF.**
If CCF is ignored:
$$Q_{sys}^{noCCF} = Q_I^2 = 0.0081$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Insight.**
Ignoring CCF underestimates the system failure probability by a factor of $0.018019 / 0.0081 \approx 2.22$. This is a major pitfall — students who ignore CCF in redundant systems will produce **overly optimistic** safety assessments.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Generic Fault Tree Structure (Top-Down)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop["TOP EVENT System Failure"]
    nodeOR["OR GATE G1"]
    nodeAND["AND GATE G2"]
    nodeOR2["OR GATE G3"]
    nodeA["Basic Event A Component X Fails"]
    nodeB["Basic Event B Component Y Fails"]
    nodeC["Basic Event C Power Loss"]
    nodeD["Basic Event D Sensor Error"]
    nodeE["Basic Event E Cable Cut"]
    nodeF["Basic Event F Overload"]
    nodeTop --> nodeOR
    nodeOR --> nodeAND
    nodeOR --> nodeA
    nodeAND --> nodeC
    nodeAND --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeOR2
    nodeOR2 --> nodeE
    nodeOR2 --> nodeF
    nodeB -. "alternative branch" .-> nodeTop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The top event "System Failure" is caused by EITHER the AND of (Power Loss AND Sensor Error) OR Component X Fails (which itself is caused by Cable Cut OR Overload). Component Y Fails is an independent alternative branch. The structure yields three minimal cut sets: $\{C, D\}$, $\{E\}$, and $\{F\}$.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Event Tree for Fire Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIE["INITIATING EVENT Internal Fire fI equals 0.01 per year"]
    nodeB1["BARRIER 1 Detection"]
    nodeB2["BARRIER 2 Suppression"]
    nodeB3["BARRIER 3 Containment"]
    nodeS1["ES1 Safe Shutdown freq equals 7.27e-3 per year"]
    nodeS2["ES2 Minor Damage freq equals 1.28e-3 per year"]
    nodeS3["ES3 Major Damage freq equals 9.50e-4 per year"]
    nodeS4["ES4 Catastrophic freq equals 5.00e-4 per year"]
    nodeIE --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 -- "S probability 0.95" --> nodeB2
    nodeB1 -- "F probability 0.05" --> nodeS4
    nodeB2 -- "S probability 0.90" --> nodeB3
    nodeB2 -- "F probability 0.10" --> nodeS3
    nodeB3 -- "S probability 0.85" --> nodeS1
    nodeB3 -- "F probability 0.15" --> nodeS2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each path is a unique combination of barrier outcomes. The numbers shown are the conditional probabilities of each branch transition. The product along each path gives the end-state conditional probability; multiplying by $f_I$ gives the frequency.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — FTA-ETA Integrated Risk Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["System Under Analysis"]
    nodeHazard["Step 1 Hazard Identification HAZOP FMEA"]
    nodeIE["Step 2 Initiating Event Catalog"]
    nodeETA["Step 3 Event Tree Analysis Forward Path"]
    nodeES["Step 4 End State List with Frequencies"]
    nodeFTA["Step 5 Fault Tree Analysis per End State"]
    nodeMCS["Step 6 Minimal Cut Sets and Component Failures"]
    nodeImportance["Step 7 Importance Measures Birnbaum Criticality"]
    nodeImprove["Step 8 Design Improvements and SIS Design"]
    nodeVerify["Step 9 Verification via Independent FTA"]
    nodeStart --> nodeHazard
    nodeHazard --> nodeIE
    nodeIE --> nodeETA
    nodeETA --> nodeES
    nodeES --> nodeFTA
    nodeFTA --> nodeMCS
    nodeMCS --> nodeImportance
    nodeImportance --> nodeImprove
    nodeImprove --> nodeVerify
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Workflow Insight:** In modern Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA), FTA and ETA are **complementary, not alternatives**. ETA is performed first to identify critical accident sequences (end-states), and FTA is then applied to each significant end-state to identify the root cause failure combinations. This is the standard methodology used in nuclear PSA (ASME / ANS combined PRA standard) and is expected in advanced KTU questions.

### 4.4 Logic Gate Symbol Reference Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAND["AND GATE Output if ALL inputs occur"]
    nodeOR["OR GATE Output if ANY input occurs"]
    nodeNOT["NOT GATE Inverts input logic"]
    nodeXOR["XOR GATE Output if EXACTLY ONE input occurs"]
    nodeVote["VOTING GATE k of n Output if at least k of n inputs occur"]
    nodeANDnote["Example 2 of 2 system"]
    nodeORnote["Example redundant paths"]
    nodeNOTnote["Example normally closed contact"]
    nodeXORnote["Example single failure tolerance"]
    nodeVotenote["Example 2 of 3 redundancy"]
    nodeAND --- nodeANDnote
    nodeOR --- nodeORnote
    nodeNOT --- nodeNOTnote
    nodeXOR --- nodeXORnote
    nodeVote --- nodeVotenote
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU problems, voting gates (k-out-of-n) are sometimes used to model **triple modular redundancy (TMR)** in aerospace systems. For a 2-out-of-3 system, the output occurs if at least 2 of 3 inputs occur — the system is fault-tolerant to any single component failure.
