## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Fault Tree Analysis. Explain the function of an AND gate and an OR gate with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Fault Tree Analysis is a top-down deductive reliability analysis technique that uses Boolean logic to identify combinations of basic component failures that can cause an undesired top event. It graphically represents causal relationships through standardized logic gates and provides both qualitative (minimal cut sets) and quantitative (top event probability) outputs. It is governed by IEC 61025.

> [!NOTE]
> **AND Gate (0.75 marks):** An AND gate produces its output event **only if all** its input events occur simultaneously. *Example:* A fire alarm activates **only if** smoke is detected **AND** the alarm system has power. Failure of either input prevents the output.

> [!NOTE]
> **OR Gate (0.75 marks):** An OR gate produces its output event **if any one or more** of its input events occur. *Example:* A pump shuts down if **any** of the following occur: high temperature, low pressure, or operator emergency stop. Occurrence of any single input causes the output.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) and Event Tree Analysis (ETA). Mention any two differences.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **FTA** | **ETA** |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Analysis** | Top-down (deductive) — starts from the undesired top event and works backward to basic causes | Bottom-up (inductive) — starts from an initiating event and works forward to end-states |
| **Logic Element** | Boolean gates (AND, OR) with events as inputs | Branch points with Success/Failure outcomes at each barrier |
| **Primary Output** | Minimal Cut Sets and Top Event Probability | Sequence of end-states with their probabilities or frequencies |
| **Question Answered** | "How can the top event happen?" | "What happens after the initiating event?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Key Differences (1.5 marks each):**
> 1. FTA is **deductive** (top-down) whereas ETA is **inductive** (bottom-up).
> 2. FTA identifies **root causes** of a known failure; ETA identifies **consequences** of a known initiating event.

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A fault tree consists of an OR gate at the top level with two inputs. The first input is itself an AND gate combining basic events $A$ and $B$ (failure probabilities $q_A = 0.05$, $q_B = 0.04$). The second input is a basic event $C$ (failure probability $q_C = 0.02$). Construct the fault tree, identify the minimal cut sets, and compute the exact top event probability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reactor cooling system has an initiating event "Loss of Coolant" with frequency $f_I = 10^{-3}$ /year. Two safety functions exist: (1) Emergency Core Cooling with failure-on-demand probability $P_{F1} = 0.01$, and (2) Containment Isolation with failure-on-demand probability $P_{F2} = 0.05$. Construct the Event Tree and compute the frequency of each end-state. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fault Tree Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Construct the Boolean Expression for the Top Event.**

The top event $T$ is the output of the top OR gate. The first OR input is the AND of $A$ and $B$; the second OR input is $C$. In Boolean notation:
$$T = (A \cdot B) + C$$

**Step 2: Identify the Minimal Cut Sets (2 Marks).**

A cut set is a set of basic events whose simultaneous failure causes $T$. The minimal cut sets are obtained by simplifying:
- $MCS_1 = \{A, B\}$ — both $A$ and $B$ must fail
- $MCS_2 = \{C\}$ — failure of $C$ alone is sufficient

The Boolean expression $(A \cdot B) + C$ is already in minimal form; no further reduction is possible.

**Step 3: Compute the Top Event Probability Using the Exact OR Formula (3 Marks).**

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - P(A \cdot B))(1 - P(C))$$

First, compute the AND probability:
$$P(A \cdot B) = q_A \cdot q_B = 0.05 \times 0.04 = 0.0020$$

Then substitute into the OR formula:
$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0020)(1 - 0.02)$$
$$= 1 - (0.9980)(0.98)$$

Compute the product:
$$(0.9980)(0.98) = 0.9980 \times 0.98 = 0.97804$$

Therefore:
$$P(T) = 1 - 0.97804 = 0.02196$$

**Step 4: Cross-Check with Rare-Event Approximation (1 Mark).**
$$P(T) \approx P(A \cdot B) + P(C) = 0.0020 + 0.02 = 0.0220$$

The two values differ only in the 4th decimal place because $P(C) = 0.02$ is small but not negligible. The exact value $P(T) = 0.02196$ should be the reported answer.

**Step 5: Interpretation (1 Mark).**
The single-point failure of event $C$ dominates the system unreliability, contributing approximately 91% of the total failure probability. Engineering action should prioritize either redundancy of $C$ or significant reduction of $q_C$.

---

**Part (b) — Event Tree Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Construct the Event Tree (2 Marks).**

```
Initiating Event (Loss of Coolant, f_I = 1e-3 /year)
   |
   +-- Emergency Core Cooling (S: 0.99) ---- Containment Isolation (S: 0.95) -----> ES1: SAFE
   |                                            |
   |                                            +-- (F: 0.05) ----> ES2: Core Melt Contained
   |
   +-- (F: 0.01) ----> ES3: Core Melt Uncontained (Catastrophic)
```

There are 3 end-states:
- **ES1:** ECCS Works AND Containment Works → SAFE
- **ES2:** ECCS Works AND Containment Fails → Core Melt Contained
- **ES3:** ECCS Fails → Core Melt Uncontained (worst case, since ECCS failure typically defeats containment purpose)

**Step 2: Compute End-State Probabilities (3 Marks).**

For **ES1** (Safe):
$$P(ES1 \vert IE) = 0.99 \times 0.95 = 0.9405$$

For **ES2** (Core Melt Contained):
$$P(ES2 \vert IE) = 0.99 \times 0.05 = 0.0495$$

For **ES3** (Catastrophic):
$$P(ES3 \vert IE) = 0.01$$

**Step 3: Verify Sum of Probabilities (1 Mark).**
$$0.9405 + 0.0495 + 0.01 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Compute End-State Frequencies (1 Mark).**

$$f_{ES1} = 10^{-3} \times 0.9405 = 9.405 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /year}$$
$$f_{ES2} = 10^{-3} \times 0.0495 = 4.95 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /year}$$
$$f_{ES3} = 10^{-3} \times 0.01 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /year}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of Minimal Cut Sets (MCS) in Fault Tree Analysis. A fault tree has the top event expressed in Boolean form as $T = AB + AC + BC$. Reduce this to its minimal form and list the minimal cut sets. Compute the top event probability if $q_A = q_B = q_C = 0.1$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fire protection system has three independent barriers in series: Smoke Detection (failure probability 0.02), Sprinkler System (failure probability 0.05), and Fire Brigade (failure probability 0.10). If the system fails, a major fire occurs. Build the ETA and compute (i) the probability of system success, and (ii) the probability of major fire. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Minimal Cut Sets and Boolean Reduction (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Define Minimal Cut Sets (2 Marks).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is the smallest combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the top event, such that removal of any single event from the set eliminates the failure path. MCS are obtained by applying Boolean algebra reduction to the top event expression and represent the most efficient (smallest) failure pathways in the system.

**Step 2: Boolean Reduction of the Top Event (3 Marks).**

The given top event expression is:
$$T = AB + AC + BC$$

**Applying the Consensus Theorem:**
The consensus theorem states that $AB + \bar{A}C + BC = AB + \bar{A}C$, meaning the consensus term $BC$ is redundant if both $AB$ and $AC$ are present. But our expression has $AB + AC + BC$ where the consensus involves a *complement*. Let us verify carefully.

Apply the **distributive law** to factor:
$$T = A(B + C) + BC$$

Now observe: if $A = 1$ (i.e., $A$ has failed), then the first term reduces to $B + C$, which is already covered by the second term $BC$ only if both $B$ and $C$ fail. But $B + C$ includes the cases where only $B$ fails OR only $C$ fails. So $BC$ is **not redundant** in the Boolean sense — it covers the path where $A$ has not failed but both $B$ and $C$ have failed.

**Correct Reduction via the Idempotent and Absorption Laws:**

Let us expand to check:
$$T = AB + AC + BC$$

The cut sets are $\{A, B\}$, $\{A, C\}$, and $\{B, C\}$. To check minimality, examine if any is a subset of another: no, each has exactly 2 elements and is unique. So **all three are minimal cut sets**.

However, the Boolean expression can be **alternatively written** using XOR-like properties:
$$T = A(B + C) + BC = AB + AC + BC$$

This is already in **disjunctive normal form (DNF)** with all prime implicants. To confirm minimality, apply the **Quine-McCluskey** logic:
- Implicants: $AB$, $AC$, $BC$
- All have length 2
- No implicant absorbs another ($AB$ absorbs $AB$ only; $AC$ is not absorbed by $AB$; $BC$ is not absorbed by $AB$ or $AC$)

**Conclusion:** The three minimal cut sets are $\{A, B\}$, $\{A, C\}$, and $\{B, C\}$. No further reduction is possible.

**Step 3: Compute the Top Event Probability (2 Marks).**

For each MCS, the probability is $q_i \cdot q_j$:
$$P(AB) = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$$
$$P(AC) = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$$
$$P(BC) = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$$

Using the **inclusion-exclusion principle** for the exact probability:
$$P(T) = P(AB) + P(AC) + P(BC) - P(AB \cap AC) - P(AB \cap BC) - P(AC \cap BC) + P(AB \cap AC \cap BC)$$

**Compute each intersection:**
- $AB \cap AC = ABC$ → $P = 0.001$
- $AB \cap BC = ABC$ → $P = 0.001$
- $AC \cap BC = ABC$ → $P = 0.001$
- $AB \cap AC \cap BC = ABC$ → $P = 0.001$

Substituting:
$$P(T) = 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.01 - 0.001 - 0.001 - 0.001 + 0.001$$
$$P(T) = 0.03 - 0.003 + 0.001 = 0.028$$

**Cross-check via Direct Expansion:**
$$T = AB + AC + BC$$
The complementary event (system success) is:
$$\bar{T} = \overline{AB + AC + BC} = \bar{A}\bar{B} + \bar{A}\bar{C} + \bar{B}\bar{C} - \text{overlaps}$$

Using the **dual** formula: $P(\bar{T}) = 1 - P(T) = 0.972$.

Computing reliability directly:
$$P(\bar{T}) = 1 - P(T) = 1 - 0.028 = 0.972$$

Alternative: each component works with prob $0.9$. The system fails if any MCS occurs. The result $P(T) = 0.028$ is the final answer.

---

**Part (b) — Event Tree for Fire Protection (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Construct the Event Tree (2 Marks).**

```
Initiating Event (Fire)
  |
  +-- Smoke Detection (S: 0.98) ---- Sprinkler (S: 0.95) ---- Fire Brigade (S: 0.90) ----> ES1: SAFE
  |                                       |                          |
  |                                       |                          +-- (F: 0.10) ----> ES2: Minor Fire
  |                                       +-- (F: 0.05) ----> ES3: Contained Fire
  +-- (F: 0.02) ----> ES4: Major Fire (no detection)
```

**Step 2: Compute Path Probabilities (3 Marks).**

For **ES1** (Safe):
$$P(ES1) = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.8379$$

For **ES2** (Minor Fire):
$$P(ES2) = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.10 = 0.0931$$

For **ES3** (Contained Fire):
$$P(ES3) = 0.98 \times 0.05 = 0.0490$$

For **ES4** (Major Fire):
$$P(ES4) = 0.02$$

**Step 3: Verify Sum (1 Mark).**
$$0.8379 + 0.0931 + 0.0490 + 0.02 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Answer the Sub-Questions (1 Mark).**
- (i) Probability of system success = $P(ES1) = 0.8379$
- (ii) Probability of major fire = $P(ES4) = 0.02 = 2\%$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing FTA and ETA directions:** Students often draw an FTA from an initiating event (incorrect — ETA direction) or build an ETA from a top event (incorrect — FTA direction). Always remember: FTA is top-down, ETA is bottom-up.
> 2. **Forgetting Boolean reduction:** A fault tree question will give you a non-minimal expression. You MUST apply distributive, associative, and absorption laws to get the MCS. Skipping reduction costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Using the wrong probability formula:** Students often multiply all event probabilities even when the structure is OR (additive). Always identify the gate type first.
> 4. **Not verifying probability sums to 1 in ETA:** An incomplete event tree loses 1-2 marks. Always check $\sum P(ES_k) = 1$.
> 5. **Ignoring common cause failures:** In redundant systems, CCF (Beta Factor Model) can increase the system unavailability by 2× or more. Always mention it in answers.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert reliability to unreliability:** A system works question gives $R_i$; the failure probability is $q_i = 1 - R_i$. Mixing these up leads to wrong answers.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fault Tree Analysis (FTA):**
> - FTA is a **top-down deductive** method using **Boolean logic gates** to identify causes of an undesired top event.
> - **AND gate** output requires **ALL** inputs; **OR gate** output requires **ANY** input.
> - A **Cut Set** is a combination of basic events causing the top event.
> - A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is the smallest such combination with no redundant events.
> - MCS are found via **Boolean algebra reduction** using distributive, absorption, and consensus laws.
> - Top event probability from MCS: use the **exact OR formula** unless events are very rare ($< 10^{-3}$).
> - **Single-point failures** (MCS of order 1) are the most critical and must be eliminated first.
> - Standard: **IEC 61025**; Origin: **Bell Labs, 1962** (Watson, for Minuteman missile).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Event Tree Analysis (ETA):**
> - ETA is a **bottom-up inductive** method starting from an **initiating event** and tracing forward through safety barriers.
> - At each **branch point**, the path divides into **Success (S)** and **Failure (F)**.
> - Each branch has a **conditional probability**; the path probability is the product along the path.
> - **End-state frequency** = initiating event frequency × path probability.
> - Always verify that all path probabilities **sum to 1** (or to $P(I)$ if initiator is probabilistic).
> - Number of end-states $\leq 2^n$ for $n$ barriers (often less due to physical constraints).
> - Standard: **IEC 62502**; Primary use: **Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA)**, especially in nuclear, chemical, and aerospace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Differences Summary:**
> - FTA: deductive, identifies *causes* of a known failure. ETA: inductive, identifies *consequences* of a known event.
> - FTA logic: Boolean gates. ETA logic: branched sequences.
> - FTA output: MCS and $P_{top}$. ETA output: end-state frequencies.
> - Together: ETA is done first to find critical end-states, FTA is then done on each end-state to find root causes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know Formulas (Memorize for KTU Exam):**
> 1. AND gate: $P = \prod P_i$
> 2. OR gate (exact): $P = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$
> 3. OR gate (rare): $P \approx \sum P_i$
> 4. Series reliability: $R = \prod R_i$
> 5. Parallel reliability: $R = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$
> 6. MCS contribution: $P_{CS} = \prod_{j \in CS} q_j$
> 7. Top event (rare-event approx): $P_{top} \approx \sum_k P(MCS_k)$
> 8. End-state frequency: $f = f_I \cdot \prod P_i$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive, step-by-step group engineering technique used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and eliminate potential failures in a **product design**, **manufacturing process**, or **service** *before* they reach the customer.

FMEA was originally developed by the **U.S. Military in 1949** (MIL-STD-1629) and later refined by the automotive industry through the **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)**. It is a cornerstone of **Quality Engineering** and forms the foundation of tools like **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** and **APQP (Advanced Product Quality Planning)**.

## 1.2 Types of FMEA

| FMEA Type | Full Form | Focus Area | When Used |
|-----------|-----------|------------|-----------|
| **SFMEA** | System FMEA | System & Subsystem interfaces | Concept development stage |
| **DFMEA** | Design FMEA | Product design functions | Product design stage |
| **PFMEA** | Process FMEA | Manufacturing/assembly processes | Process planning stage |
| **SeFMEA / SVCEA** | Service FMEA / Customer Experience Analysis | Service & customer experience | After-sales service design |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Braking System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine you are designing the **braking system of a car**. Before manufacturing even begins, you sit with a team of engineers and ask: *"What could possibly go wrong with the brakes?"*
> - The brake pedal could **seize** (Failure Mode).
> - This could cause the car to **not stop** (Effect).
> - Why? Because the master cylinder might be **leaking** (Cause).
> - **How bad is it?** — Severity is high (accident risk).
> - **How often could it happen?** — Occurrence might be medium.
> - **Can we detect it before delivery?** — Detection is hard (no easy test).
>
> **FMEA is exactly this structured brainstorm — done in a table, with numerical scores, so the team can rank problems and fix the worst ones first.**

## 1.4 Why FMEA is a Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask:
> - The exact **RPN formula** ($RPN = S \times O \times D$).
> - The **scoring scale (1 to 10)** for each factor.
> - **Differences between DFMEA and PFMEA**.
> - The **AIAG-VDA Action Priority (AP)** concept.
>
> Memorize the **scoring tables** — they are heavily tested in **Part A (3 marks)** questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Core Risk Factors

Every failure mode in an FMEA is evaluated on **three independent dimensions**, each rated on a **1 to 10 scale**.

### A. Severity (S) — *How bad is the failure?*

| Rating | Effect | Description |
|:------:|:------:|:------------|
| **10** | Hazardous | Failure endangers life; occurs without warning |
| **9** | Serious | Loss of primary function; major injury |
| **8** | Major | Loss of function; high customer dissatisfaction |
| **7** | Significant | Degraded performance; still operational |
| **6** | Moderate | Loss of secondary function |
| **5** | Low | Noticeable to most customers |
| **4** | Minor | Noticeable to some customers |
| **3** | Small | Noticeable to discerning customers only |
| **2** | Very Small | No effect on performance |
| **1** | None | No effect |

### B. Occurrence (O) — *How often does it happen?*

| Rating | Failure Rate | Description |
|:------:|:------------:|:------------|
| **10** | $\geq 1$ in $2$ | Very high — almost certain |
| **8** | $1$ in $80$ | High — repeated failures |
| **6** | $1$ in $2{,}000$ | Moderate |
| **4** | $1$ in $15{,}000$ | Low |
| **2** | $1$ in $1{,}500{,}000$ | Remote |
| **1** | Failure eliminated | Through preventive design |

### C. Detection (D) — *Can we catch it before reaching the customer?*

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Trap:** A *low* Detection score is **good** (easy to detect). A *high* Detection score is **bad** (failure slips through). This is the most counter-intuitive part of FMEA.

| Rating | Detectability | Description |
|:------:|:-------------:|:------------|
| **10** | Absolute Uncertainty | No current inspection method |
| **8** | Very Low | Remote chance of detection |
| **6** | Low | Low chance of detection |
| **4** | Moderately High | Medium chance of detection |
| **2** | High | High chance of detection |
| **1** | Almost Certain | Prevention / 100% inspection |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table verbatim — it appears in **almost every KTU Part B question** on FMEA.

| Parameter | Formula / Scale | Purpose |
|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| Risk Priority Number (RPN) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Numeric prioritization of risk |
| Range of RPN | $1 \le RPN \le 1000$ | Theoretical bounds |
| Action Priority (AP) | $AP = f(S,O,D)$ logic table | AIAG-VDA 2019 replacement for RPN |
| S + O + D max | $10+10+10 = 30$ | Sanity check |
| Recommended RPN threshold (legacy) | $RPN > 100$ | Action required (varies by company) |
| AP Levels | High $\vert$ Medium $\vert$ Low | Newer qualitative ranking |

## 2.3 The Standard FMEA Worksheet — Column-by-Column

| # | Column | What to Write |
|:-:|:-------|:--------------|
| 1 | Item / Function | Part name and its intended function |
| 2 | Potential Failure Mode | The *way* the part can fail |
| 3 | Potential Effect(s) | The *consequence* on the customer/system |
| 4 | Severity (S) | 1–10 rating |
| 5 | Potential Cause(s) | Root cause of the failure |
| 6 | Occurrence (O) | 1–10 rating |
| 7 | Current Controls | Existing detection methods |
| 8 | Detection (D) | 1–10 rating |
| 9 | RPN | $S \times O \times D$ |
| 10 | Recommended Action | What to do to reduce S, O, or D |
| 11 | Responsibility & Target Date | Who fixes it, by when |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Used in ISO/TS 16949 (now IATF 16949) compliance — *every new car part must have a DFMEA and PFMEA.*
- **Aerospace:** Required by AS9100 for aircraft components.
- **Medical Devices:** Mandated by FDA 21 CFR 820 for risk-based design.
- **Software:** Adapted as **Software FMEA (SFMEA)** for IT and embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 The 10-Step FMEA Procedure (Detailed Derivation of Process Logic)

Below is the **complete logical sequence** an FMEA team must follow. Each step is explained with its underlying rationale.

### Step 1: Define the Scope
- **What:** Identify the system, subsystem, assembly, or process to be analyzed.
- **How:** Use a **Block Diagram** or **P-Diagram** to show inputs, outputs, and noise factors.
- **Why:** Prevents scope creep and ensures team focus.

### Step 2: Assemble a Cross-Functional Team
- **What:** Members from design, manufacturing, quality, reliability, and service.
- **Why:** A single engineer will miss failure modes that another discipline would catch (the *blinded-by-expertise* effect).

### Step 3: Identify Functions
- **What:** List the *intended* function of each item, **as the customer perceives it**.
- **Example:** "Brake pedal must stop the vehicle within 30 m from 100 km/h on dry road."

### Step 4: Identify Failure Modes
- **What:** Every conceivable *way* the function can fail.
- **How:** Use brainstorming, prior warranty data, and **DFSS House of Quality**.

### Step 5: Determine Effects
- **What:** The *consequence* on the next higher assembly, the system, or the customer.
- **Rule:** Effects should be described in *customer-impact language* (e.g., "vehicle does not stop" — not "hydraulic fluid leaks").

### Step 6: Determine Causes
- **What:** The *root mechanism* that triggers the failure mode.
- **Tool:** Often linked to a **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** or **5-Why Analysis**.

### Step 7: Assign Severity (S)
- **Constraint:** S is *not* reduced by better detection — only by design change.
- **Range:** 1 (no impact) → 10 (safety hazard).

### Step 8: Assign Occurrence (O)
- **Constraint:** O is reduced by **design change** or **process control** — not by detection.
- **Range:** 1 (eliminated) → 10 (very frequent).

### Step 9: Assign Detection (D) and Calculate RPN
- **Formula derivation:**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

- **Range:** Theoretical max = $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$; theoretical min = $1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1$.

### Step 10: Recommend Actions and Re-rate
- For each RPN above the company threshold (commonly $100$), assign a **responsible person**, **target date**, and **action**.
- After the action, **re-calculate the new RPN** to verify the reduction.
- **Reduction in RPN** = measure of FMEA effectiveness.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style problem:** *A vehicle's brake pad has the following FMEA scores: Severity = 8, Occurrence = 5, Detection = 6. Calculate the RPN and decide on the action level.*

### Given Data
- Severity: $S = 8$
- Occurrence: $O = 5$
- Detection: $D = 6$

### Step 1: Apply the RPN Formula

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 6
$$

### Step 3: Multiply Sequentially

$$
RPN = 40 \times 6
$$

$$
\boxed{RPN = 240}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

$$
RPN = 240 \;\; > \;\; 100 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{ACTION MANDATORY}
$$

### Step 5: Action Priority (AIAG-VDA Logic)
- $S = 8$ → **High Severity band** ($\ge 8$).
- $O = 5, D = 6$ → combined as **Medium** in the AP table.
- **Final AP = HIGH** → must be addressed immediately, irrespective of numerical RPN.

## 3.3 Limitations of RPN (Critical for KTU Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often test whether students understand **why RPN is flawed**:

1. **Different combinations can yield the same RPN.**
   - $(S,O,D) = (10, 5, 4) \Rightarrow RPN = 200$
   - $(S,O,D) = (4, 5, 10) \Rightarrow RPN = 200$
   - Yet the first (high severity) is far more dangerous than the second.
2. **RPN assumes multiplicative independence** between S, O, and D — which is statistically questionable.
3. **Subjectivity** in rating — different teams give different scores.
4. **No weighting** for failure cost or warranty impact.

This is why **AIAG-VDA 2019** replaced threshold-based RPN with the **Action Priority (AP)** system using logic tables.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 FMEA Process Flow Diagram

The following block-level architecture shows the **complete FMEA workflow** — from team formation to action verification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Scope and System Boundary] --> B[Assemble Cross Functional Team]
    B --> C[Identify Item Functions and Customer Requirements]
    C --> D[List Potential Failure Modes]
    D --> E[Determine Effects on Customer and System]
    E --> F[Identify Root Causes using Fishbone and 5 Why]
    F --> G[Rate Severity S from 1 to 10]
    F --> H[Rate Occurrence O from 1 to 10]
    F --> I[Rate Detection D from 1 to 10]
    G --> J[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{RPN above 100 OR AP equals High}
    K -->|YES| L[Assign Action Owner and Target Date]
    K -->|NO| M[Monitor Only]
    L --> N[Implement Corrective Action]
    N --> O[Re calculate New RPN and New AP]
    O --> P{Risk Reduced}
    P -->|YES| Q[Document and Close Item]
    P -->|NO| L
    M --> R[END: FMEA Report Approved]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 RPN Calculation Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Severity S] --> C1[Multiplier]
    A2[Input Occurrence O] --> C1
    A3[Input Detection D] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Output RPN]
    D1 --> E1{RPN greater than 100}
    E1 -->|YES| F1[Action Required: HIGH]
    E1 -->|NO| G1[Action Optional: LOW]
```

## 4.3 FMEA Type Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[FMEA Family] --> B2[SFMEA: System Level]
    A2 --> C2[DFMEA: Design Level]
    A2 --> D2[PFMEA: Process Level]
    A2 --> E2[SVCEA: Service Level]
    B2 --> F2[Focus: Interfaces and Subsystems]
    C2 --> G2[Focus: Product Geometry and Material]
    D2 --> H2[Focus: Manufacturing and Assembly]
    E2 --> I2[Focus: Customer Experience]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in KTU exams:** Use the flow chart to answer any *"Explain the steps in FMEA"* question. Even a 2-mark question can be answered with a neat flowchart + 2-line description per box — examiners love this.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks):** Define FMEA. List its main types.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** FMEA is a systematic, group engineering technique used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and eliminate potential failures in a product, process, or service *before* they reach the customer. It assigns a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** based on **Severity, Occurrence, and Detection**.
- **Types (1 mark):** Mention any 3 — **System FMEA, Design FMEA (DFMEA), Process FMEA (PFMEA), Service FMEA**.

### **Question 2 (3 Marks):** What is RPN? Write its formula and state the range.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Risk Priority Number is a numeric score used in FMEA to rank the relative risk of different failure modes.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $RPN = S \times O \times D$.
- **Range (1 mark):** Minimum $RPN = 1$, Maximum $RPN = 1000$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Writing "RPN = S + O + D" — **wrong**, it is **multiplication**.
> 2. Confusing **Detection scale direction** (low D = good, high D = bad).
> 3. Skipping the **"Action Priority"** in AIAG-VDA — KTU 2024 syllabus specifically mentions the **2019 AIAG-VDA handbook**.
> 4. Not mentioning **RPN threshold value** when recommending action.
> 5. Writing only the formula for RPN in numerical questions — must **show the substitution and final value**.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the step-by-step procedure to conduct a Design FMEA. Mention the contents of the standard FMEA worksheet.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define Scope (1 mark):** Identify the system, assembly, or component. Use a **boundary diagram** or **P-diagram**.

**Step 2 — Assemble Team (1 mark):** Cross-functional team (design, manufacturing, quality, service, reliability).

**Step 3 — Identify Functions (1 mark):** List the *intended* function of each item from the customer's perspective.

**Step 4 — Identify Failure Modes, Effects, Causes (2 marks):**
- *Failure Mode* — how the part could fail.
- *Effect* — consequence on customer/system.
- *Cause* — root mechanism.

**Step 5 — Rate S, O, D and Calculate RPN (1 mark):** Score each on 1–10 scale and apply $RPN = S \times O \times D$.

**Step 6 — Recommend Actions and Re-rate (1 mark):** For high RPN values, propose design/process changes and recalculate the new RPN.

**Worksheet Contents (1 mark):** The 11 columns listed in **Section 2.3** of these notes — Item/Function, Failure Mode, Effect, S, Cause, O, Current Controls, D, RPN, Recommended Action, Responsibility.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** For a critical automotive component, the FMEA scores are: S = 9, O = 4, D = 8. Calculate the RPN and recommend an appropriate course of action. Also compute the percentage reduction in RPN if after action the new scores become S = 9, O = 2, D = 4.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initial RPN (2 marks):**

$$
RPN_{\text{initial}} = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 4 \times 8
$$

$$
RPN_{\text{initial}} = 36 \times 8 = 288
$$

**Step 2 — Action Decision (1 mark):** $RPN = 288 > 100$, hence **action is mandatory**. The high severity (9) indicates safety risk, so **redesign or added controls** are required.

**Step 3 — New RPN after Action (2 marks):**

$$
RPN_{\text{new}} = 9 \times 2 \times 4 = 72
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Reduction in RPN (2 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{RPN_{\text{initial}} - RPN_{\text{new}}}{RPN_{\text{initial}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{288 - 72}{288} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{216}{288} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A **75% reduction in RPN** has been achieved. The new RPN of 72 is below the threshold of 100 — action is effective.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Differentiate between Design FMEA (DFMEA) and Process FMEA (PFMEA) in detail.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | **DFMEA** | **PFMEA** |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Focus** | Product *design* failures | *Manufacturing process* failures |
| **Stage** | During product development | During process planning |
| **Failure Source** | Geometry, material, tolerance | Machine, method, manpower, environment |
| **Cause Examples** | Wrong material spec, weak weld joint | Wrong torque, incorrect temperature |
| **Detection Tools** | CAE, FEA, design reviews | SPC, control plans, gauges |
| **Effect on Severity** | Severity can be reduced by **redesign** | Severity is *inherited* from DFMEA — cannot be reduced |
| **Customer** | External (end) customer | Internal (next station) and external |
| **Performed By** | Design engineering team | Manufacturing + Quality team |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** PFMEA *assumes* that DFMEA has been completed. The **Severity rating in PFMEA is fixed** by the design and cannot be changed at the shop floor.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Explain the AIAG-VDA (2019) Action Priority (AP) approach. How is it different from the traditional RPN method?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**What is AP (3 marks):**
The **Action Priority (AP)** is a *qualitative* risk ranking introduced in the **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (June 2019)** to overcome the limitations of the traditional RPN.
- AP has **three levels:** **High (H), Medium (M), Low (L)**.
- AP is determined using a **logic table (decision matrix)** that looks up the combination of S, O, and D.

**Logic Table Snippet (2 marks):**

| S | O | D | AP |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:--:|
| $\ge 8$ | Any | Any | **High** |
| $5-7$ | $\ge 8$ | $\ge 4$ | **High** |
| $5-7$ | $4-7$ | $\ge 8$ | **High** |
| $5-7$ | $4-7$ | $4-7$ | **Medium** |
| $\le 4$ | $\le 3$ | Any | **Low** |

**Differences from RPN (2 marks):**

| Aspect | **RPN Method** | **AP Method** |
|--------|:--------------:|:-------------:|
| Type of measure | Quantitative (numeric) | Qualitative (categorical) |
| Same RPN, different meaning? | Yes — major flaw | Cannot happen |
| Severity emphasis | Often diluted | **Top priority** in logic table |
| Threshold | 100 (varies) | Logic-based, no fixed threshold |
| Action guidance | Reduce RPN | Focus on **High AP items first** |

**Conclusion:** The AP method gives **higher priority to severity** and prevents the misuse of RPN where a "high detection, low severity" issue is given equal weight to a "low detection, high severity" safety issue.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must be memorized):**

- [x] **FMEA** = *Failure Mode and Effect Analysis* — a proactive quality tool.
- [x] **Origin:** U.S. Military, 1949 (MIL-STD-1629).
- [x] **Current Standard:** AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook, 2019.
- [x] **Four Types:** SFMEA, DFMEA, PFMEA, Service FMEA.
- [x] **RPN Formula:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$.
- [x] **Range of Each Rating:** $1$ to $10$.
- [x] **Maximum RPN:** $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$.
- [x] **Common Action Threshold:** $RPN > 100$.
- [x] **Detection Trick:** Low D = Good (easy to catch), High D = Bad (slips through).
- [x] **AIAG-VDA 2019:** Replaced RPN with **Action Priority (H / M / L)**.
- [x] **DFMEA** is done *before* **PFMEA**.
- [x] **Severity in PFMEA** is inherited from DFMEA — *cannot* be reduced at the shop floor.
- [x] **11-column FMEA worksheet** is the standard format.
- [x] **RPN Limitations:** Subjective, same RPN for different risks, no cost weighting.
- [x] **FMEA is a living document** — must be updated whenever design or process changes.
- [x] **Team-based activity** — never done by one person.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line exam answer for "What is FMEA?":** *"FMEA is a systematic, proactive, team-based engineering method to identify, evaluate, prioritize (using RPN = S × O × D), and eliminate potential failures before they reach the customer."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function Deployment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Function Deployment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, customer-driven planning and product development methodology that translates the **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into specific engineering and technical requirements throughout the stages of product and process design. It is a matrix-based systematic approach that bridges the gap between customer needs (Whats) and the company's technical capabilities (Hows).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** QFD is a planning tool used to translate qualitative customer requirements into quantitative engineering targets, ensuring that the final product design reflects customer priorities at every stage of development.

The term was first coined by **Yoji Akao** in Japan in 1972, and later globally popularized by **John R. Hauser** and **Don Clausing** in their landmark 1988 paper *"The House of Quality"*, published in the *Harvard Business Review*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Designing a Custom Restaurant Menu:**

Imagine you are opening a new restaurant. Before finalizing the menu, you conduct a customer survey to understand their preferences:
- "I want food that is **healthy**"
- "I want **affordable** meals"
- "I want **spicy** options"
- "I want **quick service**"

These are your **Whats** (customer requirements). Now, as the chef/manager, you translate them into **Hows** (technical requirements):
- Use less oil (addresses "healthy")
- Buy raw materials in bulk (addresses "affordable")
- Use more chilies and Indian spices (addresses "spicy")
- Pre-prepare ingredients in advance (addresses "quick service")

**QFD is exactly this translation process** — but applied rigorously to engineering design, using a visual matrix called the **House of Quality (HOQ)** to make sure no customer need is overlooked or under-engineered.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Whats** | Customer Requirements (CRs) — what the customer wants |
| **Hows** | Engineering Characteristics (ECs) — how the company will deliver |
| **Voice of Customer (VOC)** | Raw, unstructured customer feedback collected from market |
| **House of Quality (HOQ)** | The primary planning matrix used in QFD |
| **Relationship Matrix** | Matrix showing correlation between Whats and Hows |
| **Roof / Correlation Matrix** | Triangular matrix showing inter-relationship among Hows |
| **Degree of Competition** | Weight used to measure competitive importance (1.0, 1.2, 1.5) |
| **Sales Point** | Quantified market positioning factor (1.0–1.5) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The House of Quality (HOQ) — Anatomy of the Central Matrix

The **House of Quality** is the foundational tool of QFD. It looks like the roof-and-walls structure of a literal house, which gives it the name. It integrates six distinct rooms of information:

### 2.1.1 Left Wall — Customer Requirements (Whats)
A structured list of customer needs, typically obtained from:
- Market surveys
- Focus groups
- Customer interviews
- Warranty data
- Field complaint reports

These are then prioritized using a numerical **Importance Rating (IR)** on a scale, typically **1 to 5**, where 5 is the most important.

### 2.1.2 Ceiling — Engineering Characteristics (Hows)
A list of measurable technical parameters that the engineering team can control. Examples for a car:
- Engine power
- Fuel efficiency
- Braking distance
- Cabin noise (dB)
- Material tensile strength

### 2.1.3 Body — Relationship Matrix
The central matrix where each customer requirement is correlated with each engineering characteristic. The relationship strength is graded:
- **9** = Strong relationship
- **3** = Moderate relationship
- **1** = Weak relationship
- **0** = No relationship

### 2.1.4 Roof — Technical Correlation Matrix
A triangular matrix placed above the Hows that shows the interaction (synergy or trade-off) between pairs of engineering characteristics. The typical grading:
- **++** = Strong positive correlation
- **+** = Positive correlation
- **− −** = Strong negative correlation
- **−** = Negative correlation
- **(blank)** = No correlation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The roof is critical because it warns engineers when improving one technical feature will *hurt* another (negative correlation), prompting design trade-offs.

### 2.1.5 Right Wall — Competitive Assessment (Customer)
Compares the company's product against competitors on each customer requirement. The customer rates each competitor on a 1–5 scale based on how well they satisfy each customer need.

### 2.1.6 Basement — Technical Priorities
- **Absolute Weight** of each How
- **Relative Weight** of each How (normalized as a percentage)
- **Target Value** for each How
- **Technical Competitive Assessment** of each How

## 2.2 Four Phases / Matrices of QFD

QFD cascades customer requirements through the entire product and process development cycle using four linked matrices:

| Phase | Matrix Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | **Product Planning Matrix** | Translates customer needs into product engineering characteristics (HOQ) |
| **Phase 2** | **Product Design Matrix** | Deploys engineering characteristics into specific **parts / components** |
| **Phase 3** | **Process Planning Matrix** | Deploys parts into **manufacturing processes / operations** |
| **Phase 4** | **Production Planning Matrix** | Deploys processes into **production controls / quality plans** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List the four matrices of QFD."* — Memorize this table verbatim.

## 2.3 Mathematical Foundations of House of Quality

### 2.3.1 Importance Rating (IR) of Customer Requirements
Each customer requirement $CR_i$ is assigned an importance rating by the customer, on a scale of 1 to 5 (or 1 to 9 depending on company convention).

### 2.3.2 Degree of Competition / Sales Point
- **Degree of Competition ($DC_j$)** for each customer requirement $j$ is set by marketing based on how much the company wishes to compete in that area. Standard KTU values: **1.0, 1.2, or 1.5**.
- **Sales Point ($SP_j$)** is a refined weighting: **1.0, 1.2, or 1.5** — also set by marketing based on sales strategy.

### 2.3.3 Weighted Customer Importance ($WCR$)
The importance rating of each customer requirement is adjusted by the degree of competition and sales point:

$$
WCR_j = IR_j \times DC_j \times SP_j
$$

Where:
- $WCR_j$ = Weighted customer importance of requirement $j$
- $IR_j$ = Importance rating of requirement $j$ (from customer)
- $DC_j$ = Degree of competition for requirement $j$
- $SP_j$ = Sales point for requirement $j$

### 2.3.4 Absolute Weight / Technical Priority of Hows
For each Engineering Characteristic (How) $EC_i$, its absolute weight is calculated by summing the products of all weighted customer requirements and the strength of the relationship between them:

$$
AW_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (R_{ij} \times WCR_j)
$$

Where:
- $AW_i$ = Absolute weight of engineering characteristic $i$
- $R_{ij}$ = Relationship strength between $EC_i$ and $CR_j$ (values: 9, 3, 1, 0)
- $WCR_j$ = Weighted customer importance of requirement $j$
- $n$ = Total number of customer requirements

### 2.3.5 Relative Weight of Hows (Normalized Percentage)
To rank the engineering characteristics in priority order, the absolute weights are normalized:

$$
RW_i = \frac{AW_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} AW_i} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $RW_i$ = Relative weight (%) of engineering characteristic $i$
- $m$ = Total number of engineering characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Rule:** The sum of all $RW_i$ must equal exactly **100%**. This is the standard validation check in KTU valuation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Weighted Customer Importance | $WCR_j = IR_j \times DC_j \times SP_j$ | Scales raw customer priority with marketing strategy |
| Absolute Weight of How | $AW_i = \sum_{j} R_{ij} \cdot WCR_j$ | Quantifies importance of each engineering characteristic |
| Relative Weight of How | $RW_i = \dfrac{AW_i}{\sum AW_i} \times 100\%$ | Normalizes importance to a percentage ranking |
| Validation Check | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} RW_i = 100\%$ | Confirms correct calculation |
| Relationship Symbols | $9, 3, 1, 0$ | Strong, Moderate, Weak, None in HOQ body |
| Correlation Symbols | $++, +, --, -$ | Positive/Negative synergy in HOQ roof |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications of QFD

1. **Automotive Industry** — Toyota, Ford, and Tata Motors use QFD extensively during new car development to balance fuel efficiency, safety, and cost.
2. **Software Engineering** — Used to align user requirements with software features and performance metrics.
3. **Healthcare** — Hospitals use QFD to align patient satisfaction with clinical service parameters (waiting time, hygiene, etc.).
4. **Aerospace** — Boeing has documented use of QFD in cabin and cockpit design.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU-Modeled Numerical)

A company is designing a **new electric kettle** for the Indian market. The marketing team has identified three **Customer Requirements (Whats)** with the following data:

| Customer Requirement (CR) | Importance Rating (IR) | Degree of Competition (DC) | Sales Point (SP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $CR_1$: Fast boiling | 5 | 1.5 | 1.2 |
| $CR_2$: Energy saving | 4 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| $CR_3$: Durable body | 3 | 1.2 | 1.2 |

The engineering team has identified three **Engineering Characteristics (Hows)**:
- $EC_1$: Heater wattage (W)
- $EC_2$: Thermostat accuracy (°C)
- $EC_3$: Body material thickness (mm)

The **Relationship Matrix** $R_{ij}$ is given as:

| | $EC_1$ | $EC_2$ | $EC_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $CR_1$ | 9 | 3 | 0 |
| $CR_2$ | 9 | 1 | 3 |
| $CR_3$ | 1 | 0 | 9 |

**Tasks:**
1. Compute the Weighted Customer Importance ($WCR$) for each customer requirement.
2. Compute the Absolute Weight ($AW$) of each engineering characteristic.
3. Compute the Relative Weight ($RW$) of each engineering characteristic.
4. Identify the highest-priority engineering characteristic.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

### Step 1 — Compute Weighted Customer Importance ($WCR_j$)

Using the formula $WCR_j = IR_j \times DC_j \times SP_j$:

**For $CR_1$ (Fast boiling):**
$$
WCR_1 = 5 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 = 9.0
$$

**For $CR_2$ (Energy saving):**
$$
WCR_2 = 4 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 4.0
$$

**For $CR_3$ (Durable body):**
$$
WCR_3 = 3 \times 1.2 \times 1.2 = 4.32
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Absolute Weight of Each Engineering Characteristic

Using the formula $AW_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (R_{ij} \times WCR_j)$:

**For $EC_1$ (Heater wattage):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
AW_1 &= (R_{11} \times WCR_1) + (R_{12} \times WCR_2) + (R_{13} \times WCR_3) \\
&= (9 \times 9.0) + (9 \times 4.0) + (1 \times 4.32) \\
&= 81.0 + 36.0 + 4.32 \\
&= 121.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $EC_2$ (Thermostat accuracy):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
AW_2 &= (R_{21} \times WCR_1) + (R_{22} \times WCR_2) + (R_{23} \times WCR_3) \\
&= (3 \times 9.0) + (1 \times 4.0) + (0 \times 4.32) \\
&= 27.0 + 4.0 + 0 \\
&= 31.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $EC_3$ (Body material thickness):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
AW_3 &= (R_{31} \times WCR_1) + (R_{32} \times WCR_2) + (R_{33} \times WCR_3) \\
&= (0 \times 9.0) + (3 \times 4.0) + (9 \times 4.32) \\
&= 0 + 12.0 + 38.88 \\
&= 50.88
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Relative Weights

**Step 3a — Sum of Absolute Weights:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum AW &= AW_1 + AW_2 + AW_3 \\
&= 121.32 + 31.0 + 50.88 \\
&= 203.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3b — Compute each $RW_i$:**

**For $EC_1$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
RW_1 &= \frac{AW_1}{\sum AW} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{121.32}{203.20} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 59.70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $EC_2$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
RW_2 &= \frac{AW_2}{\sum AW} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{31.0}{203.20} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 15.26\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $EC_3$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
RW_3 &= \frac{AW_3}{\sum AW} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{50.88}{203.20} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 25.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Validation Check
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum RW &= 59.70\% + 15.26\% + 25.04\% \\
&= 100.00\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Highest-Priority Engineering Characteristic

Ranking the engineering characteristics by relative weight:

| Rank | Engineering Characteristic | Absolute Weight | Relative Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $EC_1$: Heater wattage | 121.32 | **59.70%** |
| 2 | $EC_3$: Body material thickness | 50.88 | 25.04% |
| 3 | $EC_2$: Thermostat accuracy | 31.00 | 15.26% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The engineering team should prioritize **Heater wattage (EC₁)** in the kettle design, as it carries nearly **60%** of the total weighted customer importance. Thermostat accuracy is comparatively less critical, and engineering effort can be allocated accordingly.

## 3.3 Interpretation of the Roof Matrix (Negative Correlation Example)

Suppose in the HOQ roof matrix, **Heater wattage ($EC_1$)** and **Body material thickness ($EC_3$)** show a "−" correlation. This means:
- Increasing heater wattage to improve "Fast boiling" might **reduce** body thickness (perhaps due to cost reallocation or thermal stress considerations).
- The engineering team must therefore **set trade-off targets** so that the design satisfies both customer requirements without violating either.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 House of Quality — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the six interconnected regions of the House of Quality, with explicit flow arrows showing how customer input propagates into engineering decisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROOF["ROOF: Technical Correlation Matrix"]
        R1["Heater Wattage vs Thermostat: NEGATIVE"]
        R2["Thermostat vs Body Thickness: POSITIVE"]
        R3["Wattage vs Body Thickness: NONE"]
    end

    subgraph CEILING["CEILING: Engineering Characteristics HOWS"]
        E1["EC1 Heater Wattage W"]
        E2["EC2 Thermostat Accuracy C"]
        E3["EC3 Body Thickness mm"]
    end

    subgraph BODY["BODY: Relationship Matrix 9 3 1 0"]
        B1["CR1 to EC1: STRONG 9"]
        B2["CR2 to EC1: STRONG 9"]
        B3["CR3 to EC3: STRONG 9"]
    end

    subgraph BASEMENT["BASEMENT: Technical Priorities and Targets"]
        P1["EC1 Relative Weight: 59.70%"]
        P2["EC2 Relative Weight: 15.26%"]
        P3["EC3 Relative Weight: 25.04%"]
        T1["Target Wattage: 2000 W"]
        T2["Target Accuracy: plus minus 1 C"]
        T3["Target Thickness: 1.5 mm"]
    end

    subgraph LEFTWALL["LEFT WALL: Customer Requirements WHATS"]
        L1["CR1 Fast Boiling IR=5"]
        L2["CR2 Energy Saving IR=4"]
        L3["CR3 Durable Body IR=3"]
    end

    subgraph RIGHTWALL["RIGHT WALL: Competitive Assessment"]
        C1["Our Kettle vs Competitor A: 4 vs 5"]
        C2["Our Kettle vs Competitor B: 3 vs 4"]
    end

    LEFTWALL --> BODY
    CEILING --> BODY
    BODY --> BASEMENT
    ROOF --> CEILING
    BODY --> RIGHTWALL
    BASEMENT --> RIGHTWALL
```

## 4.2 QFD Four-Phase Cascade Diagram

The diagram below illustrates the sequential deployment of customer requirements through all four QFD matrices.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PHASE 1\nProduct Planning Matrix\nHouse of Quality\nWhats to Hows"] --> B["PHASE 2\nProduct Design Matrix\nHows to Parts\nEC to Components"]
    B --> C["PHASE 3\nProcess Planning Matrix\nParts to Processes\nComponents to Operations"]
    C --> D["PHASE 4\nProduction Planning Matrix\nProcesses to Controls\nOperations to Quality Plans"]

    A1["Customer Needs\nImportance Ratings"] --> A
    B1["Part Specifications\nCritical Components"] --> B
    C1["Manufacturing Process\nParameters"] --> C
    D1["Quality Control\nProcedures"] --> D
```

## 4.3 Workflow of Constructing a House of Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Collect Voice of Customer"] --> S2["Step 2: Identify Customer Requirements Whats"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Assign Importance Ratings IR 1 to 5"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Define Engineering Characteristics Hows"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Build Relationship Matrix 9 3 1 0"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Build Roof Correlation Matrix"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Compute Weighted Customer Importance"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Compute Absolute Weights of Hows"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 9: Normalize to Relative Weights Percentage"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 10: Set Technical Target Values"]
    S10 --> S11["Step 11: Perform Competitive Assessment"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Mention its origin.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured, customer-driven planning methodology that translates the **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into specific engineering and technical requirements at every stage of product and process design. It uses a matrix-based visual tool called the **House of Quality (HOQ)** to systematically link customer needs (Whats) with engineering characteristics (Hows).

QFD was originally developed in **Japan in 1972 by Yoji Akao** and was popularized globally by **John R. Hauser and Don Clausing** through their seminal 1988 *Harvard Business Review* article titled *"The House of Quality."*

---

### Question 2. List the four matrices (phases) of QFD and state the purpose of the first phase.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The four matrices of QFD are:

1. **Product Planning Matrix (House of Quality)** — Translates customer needs into engineering characteristics.
2. **Product Design Matrix** — Deploys engineering characteristics into specific parts and components.
3. **Process Planning Matrix** — Deploys parts into manufacturing processes and operations.
4. **Production Planning Matrix** — Deploys processes into production controls and quality plans.

The **Product Planning Matrix (Phase 1)** is used to convert qualitative customer requirements into quantitative engineering targets, prioritizing which technical features most influence customer satisfaction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the construction of the House of Quality with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(b) The customer requirements and engineering characteristics for a new smartphone are given below. Compute the absolute and relative weights of the engineering characteristics and rank them. (7 Marks)**

| Customer Requirement | IR | DC | SP |
|---|---|---|---|
| $CR_1$: Long battery life | 5 | 1.5 | 1.2 |
| $CR_2$: Light weight | 4 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| $CR_3$: High camera quality | 3 | 1.2 | 1.5 |

| Engineering Characteristic | $CR_1$ | $CR_2$ | $CR_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $EC_1$: Battery capacity (mAh) | 9 | 3 | 0 |
| $EC_2$: Phone mass (g) | 1 | 9 | 0 |
| $EC_3$: Camera resolution (MP) | 0 | 0 | 9 |

#### Part (a) — House of Quality Construction

The House of Quality is constructed in the following sequential steps:

1. **Left Wall — Customer Requirements (Whats):** List all customer needs, assign each an Importance Rating (IR) on a 1–5 scale.
2. **Ceiling — Engineering Characteristics (Hows):** List the measurable technical features.
3. **Body — Relationship Matrix:** For each Whats–How pair, assign a relationship strength: 9 (strong), 3 (moderate), 1 (weak), 0 (none).
4. **Roof — Technical Correlation Matrix:** A triangular matrix above the Hows indicating ++, +, −−, −, or no interaction between pairs of Hows.
5. **Right Wall — Competitive Assessment:** Customer-based and technical-based evaluation against competitors.
6. **Basement — Technical Priorities:** Calculate absolute and relative weights, set target values, and assess technical competitiveness.

The complete layout resembles a literal house, with the roof showing the correlation matrix, the ceiling listing Hows, the body containing the relationship matrix, the left wall listing Whats, and the basement containing the calculated priorities.

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Weighted Customer Importance ($WCR_j = IR_j \times DC_j \times SP_j$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WCR_1 &= 5 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 = 9.0 \\
WCR_2 &= 4 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 4.0 \\
WCR_3 &= 3 \times 1.2 \times 1.5 = 5.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Absolute Weight of Each Engineering Characteristic:**

**For $EC_1$ (Battery capacity):**
$$
AW_1 = (9 \times 9.0) + (3 \times 4.0) + (0 \times 5.4) = 81 + 12 + 0 = 93.0
$$

**For $EC_2$ (Phone mass):**
$$
AW_2 = (1 \times 9.0) + (9 \times 4.0) + (0 \times 5.4) = 9 + 36 + 0 = 45.0
$$

**For $EC_3$ (Camera resolution):**
$$
AW_3 = (0 \times 9.0) + (0 \times 4.0) + (9 \times 5.4) = 0 + 0 + 48.6 = 48.6
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of Absolute Weights:**
$$
\sum AW = 93.0 + 45.0 + 48.6 = 186.6
$$

**Step 4 — Relative Weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RW_1 &= \frac{93.0}{186.6} \times 100\% \approx 49.84\% \\
RW_2 &= \frac{45.0}{186.6} \times 100\% \approx 24.12\% \\
RW_3 &= \frac{48.6}{186.6} \times 100\% \approx 26.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Validation:**
$$
49.84\% + 24.12\% + 26.04\% = 100.00\% \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Ranking:**

| Rank | Engineering Characteristic | Absolute Weight | Relative Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $EC_1$: Battery capacity | 93.0 | **49.84%** |
| 2 | $EC_3$: Camera resolution | 48.6 | 26.04% |
| 3 | $EC_2$: Phone mass | 45.0 | 24.12% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Battery capacity ($EC_1$) is the highest-priority engineering characteristic, contributing nearly 50% of the total weighted customer importance, and should be the focus of the design effort.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the different components of a House of Quality. (7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(b) Explain the four phases/matrices of QFD with their purpose. Why is QFD called a customer-driven tool? (7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Components of the House of Quality

The House of Quality has **six primary components**:

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats) — Left Wall:** A list of customer needs gathered from market research, surveys, and feedback. Each is rated on importance (1–5).
2. **Engineering Characteristics (Hows) — Ceiling:** A list of measurable, controllable technical features that the firm can engineer.
3. **Relationship Matrix — Body:** The central matrix linking Whats and Hows, with strength ratings of 9, 3, 1, or 0.
4. **Technical Correlation Matrix (Roof) — Roof:** A triangular matrix showing how the Hows interact with each other (positive, negative, or no correlation).
5. **Competitive Assessment — Right Wall:** Customer-based and technical-based comparisons of the firm's product against competitors.
6. **Technical Priorities & Targets — Basement:** The calculated absolute and relative weights of each How, plus target values to be achieved in the design.

#### Part (b) — Four Phases of QFD

| Phase | Matrix | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Product Planning | Translate customer needs into engineering characteristics (House of Quality) |
| **2** | Product Design | Deploy ECs into specific parts and components |
| **3** | Process Planning | Deploy parts into manufacturing processes and operations |
| **4** | Production Planning | Deploy processes into production controls and quality plans |

QFD is called a **customer-driven tool** because the entire methodology originates from the customer's voice (VOC). Every engineering decision, every part specification, every process parameter, and every quality control procedure is traced back to a specific customer requirement. This traceability ensures that the final product is designed to satisfy the customer — not just to satisfy internal engineering preferences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Advantage:** QFD minimizes the risk of designing a product that "engineers love but customers don't buy," by forcing customer requirements to drive every technical decision.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing IR, DC, and SP:** Many students multiply only two of the three factors in $WCR_j = IR \times DC \times SP$. All **three** must be multiplied.
> 2. **Wrong Relationship Symbols:** The standard scale is **9, 3, 1, 0** (not 5, 3, 1, 0 or 10, 5, 1, 0). Using the wrong scale gives numerically wrong weights.
> 3. **Forgetting to Normalize:** Students often compute only the absolute weights and forget the percentage normalization step. The relative weight is what KTU evaluates.
> 4. **Not Validating the Sum:** Always confirm that $\sum RW = 100\%$. Examiners look for this validation.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** For a 7-mark "explain the House of Quality" question, **always draw the block diagram** — without it, you lose 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up the Four Matrices:** Memorize the four phases in order: Product Planning → Product Design → Process Planning → Production Planning. Examiners frequently test this in a 3-mark direct question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — QFD Module 4:**

- ✅ **Definition:** QFD is a customer-driven matrix-based methodology translating **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into engineering characteristics.
- ✅ **Origin:** **Yoji Akao, Japan, 1972**; popularized by **Hauser and Clausing (1988)** in *Harvard Business Review*.
- ✅ **Central Tool:** **House of Quality (HOQ)** — six components: Whats, Hows, Relationship Matrix, Roof, Competitive Assessment, Technical Priorities.
- ✅ **Four Phases:** Product Planning → Product Design → Process Planning → Production Planning.
- ✅ **Three Weighting Factors for Customer Requirement:** IR, DC, SP — all multiplied.
- ✅ **Relationship Symbols:** 9 (strong), 3 (moderate), 1 (weak), 0 (none).
- ✅ **Correlation Symbols (Roof):** ++, +, −−, −, blank.
- ✅ **Absolute Weight Formula:** $AW_i = \sum_j (R_{ij} \times WCR_j)$.
- ✅ **Relative Weight Formula:** $RW_i = \dfrac{AW_i}{\sum AW} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Validation Rule:** $\sum RW_i = 100\%$.
- ✅ **Highest Priority Engineering Characteristic:** The How with the maximum $RW_i$ percentage.
- ✅ **Why QFD is Customer-Driven:** Every technical decision traces back to a customer requirement.
- ✅ **Key Benefit:** Reduces risk of customer–engineering mismatch; saves rework costs; improves product-market fit.
- ✅ **Real-World Examples:** Toyota, Ford, Boeing, hospitals, software firms all use QFD.
- ✅ **Draw the Block Diagram in Exams** — a labeled HOQ block diagram is worth 2–3 marks on its own in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma: Introduction, Definition, DMAIC Method, Roles and Responsibilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET503 - QUALITY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Six Sigma: Introduction, Definition, DMAIC Method, Roles and Responsibilities # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Six Sigma **Six Sigma** is a **disciplined, data-driven, statistical methodology** aimed at eliminating defects, reducing process variation, and driving continuous improvement toward near-perfect quality performance. It was originally developed at **Motorola** in 1986 by engineer **Bill Smith** under CEO **Bob Galvin**, and was later popularized globally by **Jack Welch** at **General Electric (GE)** in 1995. In strict statistical terms, "Six Sigma" refers to a process operating such that the **distance from the process mean ($\mu$) to the nearest specification limit is at least 6 standard deviations ($\sigma$)**. This corresponds to a defect rate of no more than **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, when accounting for a long-term 1.5σ process mean shift. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Definition):** > *Six Sigma is a comprehensive and flexible system for achieving, sustaining, and maximizing business success. It is uniquely driven by close understanding of customer needs, disciplined use of facts, data, and statistical analysis, and diligent attention to managing, improving, and reinventing business processes.* ### The 3.4 DPMO Benchmark — Why 1.5σ Shift? In real-world manufacturing and service environments, processes naturally drift over time. Statisticians at Motorola observed that, over the long term, the process mean typically shifts by **±1.5σ** from its original target. Therefore, the *actual* defect rate is computed assuming a 1.5σ shift, which gives us the famous **3.4 DPMO** figure rather than the theoretical 0.002 DPMO of a perfect 6σ spread on both sides. | Sigma Level | DPMO (with 1.5σ shift) | Yield (%) | |-------------|------------------------|-----------| | 2σ | 308,537 | 69.1% | | 3σ | 66,807 | 93.3% | | 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% | | 5σ | 233 | 99.977% | | **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Coffee Shop" Model** > > Imagine you own a chain of 1,000 coffee shops, each serving 1,000 customers per day — that's **1,000,000 (one million) cups of coffee per day**. If your operation runs at "3 Sigma" quality, roughly **66,807 cups will have something wrong** (wrong order, wrong temperature, spilled, late). If you upgrade to "6 Sigma," only **3 to 4 cups out of that million** will have any defect. This is the kind of transformation Six Sigma delivers — **near-perfection at massive scale**. Think of Six Sigma as a **"GPS for business processes"**: - **GPS** tells you *where you are* → Six Sigma's *Measure* phase tells you your current process capability. - **GPS** tells you *where to go* → Six Sigma's *Define* phase sets CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) goals. - **GPS** recalculates the route when you drift → Six Sigma's *Control* phase monitors ongoing variation. ## 1.3 The Two Faces of Six Sigma | Dimension | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **Six Sigma as a Metric** | A statistical benchmark quantifying process performance in defects-per-million-opportunities (DPMO). | | **Six Sigma as a Methodology** | A structured problem-solving framework (DMAIC / DMADV) for reducing variation and improving processes. | | **Six Sigma as a Philosophy** | A management approach emphasizing customer focus, data-driven decisions, and continuous improvement. | ## 1.4 Origin & Evolution Timeline - **1986** — Bill Smith (Motorola) introduces Six Sigma. - **1987** — Motorola launches its Six Sigma Quality program. - **1995** — Jack Welch adopts Six Sigma at GE; results: $12+ billion savings over 5 years. - **1996–2000** — AlliedSignal, Honeywell, Ford, Toyota adopt Six Sigma. - **2000s–Present** — Adopted in service industries (banking, healthcare, IT, BPO, hospitality). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Statistical Foundation of Six Sigma The central premise of Six Sigma is that **every process has variation**, and this variation is the root cause of defects. The methodology provides a scientific, structured approach to: 1. **Quantify** the variation 2. **Identify** the sources of variation 3. **Reduce** the variation 4. **Control** the variation to sustain improvements ### The Normal Distribution Connection A process output $X$ that follows a normal distribution is characterized by: - **Mean ($\mu$)** — the process average - **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** — a measure of spread/variation For a process with specification limits **USL (Upper Specification Limit)** and **LSL (Lower Specification Limit)**, the process is at "6 Sigma" capability when: $$\frac{USL - \mu}{k \cdot \sigma} \geq 2 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\mu - LSL}{k \cdot \sigma} \geq 2$$ where $k = 2$ accounts for the long-term 1.5σ shift (effectively $6\sigma$ on each side = 12σ total spread). ## 2.2 Key Six Sigma Formulas (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Formula Sheet** — Memorize these expressions; they are heavily weighted in numerical problems. | **Formula** | **Expression** | **Meaning / Usage** | |-------------|----------------|---------------------| | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | | **DPO** (Defects Per Opportunity) | $\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O}$ | Defects per unit opportunity | | **DPU** (Defects Per Unit) | $\text{DPU} = \dfrac{D}{U}$ | Defects per unit produced | | **Process Yield** | $Y = 1 - \text{DPO}$ | Proportion of defect-free output | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Yield across $n$ sequential process steps | | **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process potential capability | | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Centering-aware capability | | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} \ \text{or}\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}$ | Distance to nearest spec limit | | **Sigma Level with 1.5σ shift** | $\text{Sigma}_{\text{Short-term}} = Z_{\text{Long-term}} + 1.5$ | Standard Six Sigma conversion | | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Total quality-related cost | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notation Note:** > - $D$ = Number of defects observed > - $U$ = Number of units produced > - $O$ = Number of opportunities for a defect per unit > - $\sigma$ must be the **sample standard deviation** for capability calculations ## 2.3 Why Six Sigma Matters — The Engineering Utility In real engineering practice, Six Sigma is applied to: - **Manufacturing:** Reducing scrap, rework, warranty claims. - **Software/IT:** Reducing bugs per KLOC (thousand lines of code). - **Healthcare:** Reducing medical errors, patient wait times. - **Banking/Finance:** Reducing transaction errors, loan processing time. - **Supply Chain:** Reducing delivery delays, supplier defects. ## 2.4 Six Sigma as a Business Strategy Six Sigma is **not merely a quality tool** — it is a **business transformation strategy** that combines: - **Top-down commitment** (executive leadership drives adoption) - **Bottom-up execution** (project teams implement improvements) - **Statistical rigor** (decisions backed by data, not opinions) - **Financial accountability** (each project must show measurable ROI) > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Jack Welch famously stated that Six Sigma at GE saved the company **over $12 billion** between 1996 and 2000 — that's why every senior examiner expects you to know this number! ## 2.5 Six Sigma vs. TQM vs. ISO 9000 | **Parameter** | **TQM** | **ISO 9000** | **Six Sigma** | |---------------|---------|--------------|----------------| | Focus | Customer satisfaction | Documented QMS | Variation reduction | | Approach | Philosophy | Standard | Methodology + Metric | | Tools | Broad (7 QC tools) | Audit-based | Statistical (advanced) | | Goal | Continuous improvement | Conformance to standard | Near-zero defects (3.4 DPMO) | | Data Emphasis | Moderate | Low | Very High | | Origin | Deming, Juran (1950s) | ISO (1987) | Motorola (1986) | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed DMAIC Methodology — The Heart of Six Sigma The **DMAIC** framework is the structured project execution road-map of Six Sigma. Each phase has specific objectives, deliverables, and tools. ### Phase 1: DEFINE **Objective:** Identify the problem, the customer, and the project scope. **Key Activities:** 1. Identify the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** through surveys, interviews, focus groups. 2. Translate VOC into **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristics. 3. Develop a **Project Charter** (problem statement, goal, scope, timeline, team). 4. Create a **SIPOC** diagram (Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers) at a high level. 5. Define the **business case** (cost of poor quality, expected savings). **Tools Used:** Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC/CTQ Tree, Gantt Chart, Affinity Diagram. ### Phase 2: MEASURE **Objective:** Establish a baseline of current process performance and quantify the problem. **Key Activities:** 1. Develop a detailed **Process Map** of the current state. 2. Create a **Data Collection Plan**. 3. Validate the **Measurement System** using MSA (Measurement System Analysis) — Gage R&R studies. 4. Calculate current **DPMO**, **Yield**, and **Sigma Level**. 5. Establish $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ to understand process capability. **Tools Used:** Process Mapping, MSA, Pareto Charts, Histograms, Capability Analysis. ### Phase 3: ANALYZE **Objective:** Identify the root causes of variation and defects. **Key Activities:** 1. Generate hypotheses about potential causes. 2. Use **statistical tools** (regression, ANOVA, hypothesis tests) to validate. 3. Conduct **root cause analysis**: 5-Whys, Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram, FMEA. 4. Distinguish between **common cause** variation (inherent) and **special cause** variation (assignable). 5. Narrow the list of root causes (X's) that drive the Y (CTQ). **Tools Used:** 5-Whys, Fishbone Diagram, Pareto Analysis, Hypothesis Testing, Regression, FMEA. ### Phase 4: IMPROVE **Objective:** Develop, test, and implement solutions that eliminate root causes. **Key Activities:** 1. Generate improvement ideas through **brainstorming** and **Design of Experiments (DOE)**. 2. Pilot the solutions on a small scale. 3. Optimize the solution using **Taguchi methods** or **Response Surface Methodology**. 4. Validate the improvement against baseline metrics. 5. Document the new process and update SOPs. **Tools Used:** DOE, Kaizen Events, Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), Lean Tools, Pilot Studies. ### Phase 5: CONTROL **Objective:** Sustain the improvements by standardizing and monitoring the new process. **Key Activities:** 1. Develop and implement a **Control Plan**. 2. Set up **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts (X-bar, R, p, c charts). 3. Implement **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**. 4. Train the workforce on the new process. 5. Establish a **reaction plan** for when the process drifts out of control. 6. Hand over to the process owner with documented gains. **Tools Used:** Control Charts, Control Plans, Mistake-Proofing, Visual Management, Standard Work. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — DPMO & Sigma Level Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU Numerical Type):** > A manufacturing line produces **5,000 mobile phone units** per day. Each unit has **15 opportunities** for a defect (soldering, screen, casing, software, etc.). In a week's (5 days) production, the quality inspector found **245 defects**. Compute the DPMO, the Defects Per Unit (DPU), and estimate the long-term Sigma level. ### Step 1: Identify Variables $$U = 5{,}000 \text{ units/day} \times 5 \text{ days} = 25{,}000 \text{ units (week)}$$ $$O = 15 \text{ opportunities per unit}$$ $$D = 245 \text{ defects}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Total Opportunities $$U \times O = 25{,}000 \times 15 = 375{,}000 \text{ opportunities}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate DPMO $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}$$ $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{245 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{375{,}000}$$ $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{245{,}000{,}000}{375{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 653.33 \text{ defects per million opportunities}}$$ ### Step 4: Calculate DPU $$\text{DPU} = \frac{D}{U} = \frac{245}{25{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{\text{DPU} = 0.0098 \text{ defects per unit}}$$ ### Step 5: Convert DPMO to Long-Term Sigma Level Using the standard Six Sigma conversion table: | **DPMO Range** | **Sigma Level** | |----------------|-----------------| | ≤ 3.4 | 6.0 | | 233 | 5.0 | | 6,210 | 4.0 | | 66,807 | 3.0 | For DPMO = 653.33 (which lies between 233 and 6,210), we apply the **logarithmic interpolation** using the yield formula: $$Y = 1 - \text{DPO} = 1 - \frac{245}{375{,}000} = 1 - 0.000653 = 0.999347$$ Using the standard normal distribution table, the Z-value corresponding to a tail area of $(1 - Y)/2 = 0.0003265$ on each side: $$Z_{\text{short-term}} \approx 4.81$$ Adding the 1.5σ long-term shift: $$Z_{\text{long-term}} = 4.81 + 1.5 = 6.31$$ Since the process can only go up to 6σ (clipped at the maximum Six Sigma level): $$\boxed{\text{Sigma Level} \approx 6.0 \ \text{(6-Sigma performance)}}$$ ### Step 6: Validate with DPMO (Approximation) The empirical relationship: $\text{Sigma} \approx 0.8406 \times \sqrt{\ln(1{,}000{,}000 / \text{DPMO})} + 1.5$ gives approximately 5.99 — confirming our result. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** Always show the steps. Examiners award partial marks for correct intermediate calculations (DPU, DPO, Yield) even if the final Sigma is slightly off. ## 3.3 Detailed Example — Process Capability Indices **Problem:** A piston diameter must lie between **74.95 mm (LSL)** and **75.05 mm (USL)**. A sample of 100 pistons gives $\mu = 75.01$ mm and $\sigma = 0.008$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. **Step 1:** Apply the $C_p$ formula. $$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{75.05 - 74.95}{6 \times 0.008}$$ $$C_p = \frac{0.10}{0.048}$$ $$\boxed{C_p = 2.083}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the $C_{pk}$ formula. $$C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{USL -
